details: My neck was at an angle that shouldn't be possible. The page I was on had a crease across it now.
At least the candle didn't catch anything. That's the real lucky part.
reactions: I keep telling myself I'll stop reading in bed. I keep lying to myself.

entries.intro: Someone left a lantern burning at the {poi_type} all night again. You could see the glow from across the settlement.
details: It's a waste of oil, but I'll admit it's reassuring to see a light on when everything else is dark.
I don't think anyone's forgetting to put it out. I think someone leaves it on purpose.
reactions: I've never asked who. I don't want them to stop.

entries.intro: I woke up last night because my arm had gone completely numb. Slept on it wrong.
details: That horrible tingling sensation when the blood comes back. Like a thousand tiny needles.
I shook it for five minutes, made a fist, opened it. Felt like someone else's hand.
reactions: It's fine now. But for a moment in the dark it was deeply unsettling.

entries.intro: I've taken to having a warm drink before bed. Something about the routine helps me wind down.
details: Same cup, same chair, same quiet. I sit by the last of the fire and sip until I'm drowsy.
My partner thinks it's funny that I've become so particular about it. But it works.
reactions: Sleep is easier when you approach it properly. Like anything else, really.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} keeps visiting the garden at night. I can see the tracks every morning.
details: It follows the same route each time: along the wall, through the herb patch, out the side gap.
I've started leaving the gap open on purpose. Seems rude to block its commute.
reactions: We're sharing the space. It just doesn't know we've come to an arrangement.

entries.intro: The wind picked up a shutter and slammed it against the house last night. I thought someone was knocking.
details: I actually got up and opened the front door: nobody there, just the wind howling past.
Found the shutter banging on the side wall. The latch had come loose.
reactions: I fixed the latch, went back to bed, and lay there listening for knocking that wasn't there.

statCheck.pass: The latch pin is worn. I should replace it with a heavier gauge pin that sits deeper in the catch.
statCheck.fail: Wind broke something and I fixed it. That's the whole story.

entries.intro: I woke up to my dog pressed against me, trembling. Must've been a noise I slept through.
details: She was shaking hard enough I could feel it through the blanket. Ears flat, body tense.
I stroked her head until she calmed down, but it took a while. She's usually braver than me.
reactions: Whatever it was, it was bad enough to scare a dog and not enough to wake me. That's the worrying part.

entries.intro: I can always tell when it's about to rain at night. The air changes first: heavier, warmer, pressing in.
details: Then there's this pause where everything goes still. Even the insects quiet down.
And then the first drops hit the roof and it's like the night exhales.
reactions: I've gotten good at reading the air. It's the one useful thing insomnia has taught me.

statCheck.pass: The pressure drops before rain and the air holds more moisture. Your skin can feel it even when your eyes can't see the clouds building.
statCheck.fail: I just know the feeling. Couldn't tell you the science behind it.

entries.intro: The settlement bonfire burned until well past midnight last night. Nobody wanted to be the first to leave.
details: We all just sat there watching the embers, talking less and less as the night went on.
By the end it was four of us and a pile of coals, then three, then just me.
reactions: The walk home in the dark after a fire is the coldest walk there is.

entries.intro: Heard something scraping along the outside wall of my house last night. Slow, deliberate.
details: Went on for about a minute, then stopped. I didn't move.
Checked the wall this morning: long scratch in the plaster. Could've been an antler or a branch.
reactions: I'm going with 'branch.' That's what I've decided and I'm sticking with it.

statCheck.pass: A {wildlife_type} probably rubbed against the wall: they use rough surfaces to scratch their sides. The mark was at the right height for it.
statCheck.fail: Something scratched the wall. I don't know what and I'm not investigating.

entries.intro: On the clearest nights you can see your shadow by starlight alone. No moon needed.
details: It's faint: just a suggestion on the ground, barely there. But it moves when you move.
I showed my kid and she was fascinated. Spent ten minutes waving at her star-shadow.
reactions: You have to be away from the torches to see it. But it's there.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} have started coming closer to the walls on overcast nights. When the torchlight is the only light.
details: On moonlit nights they stay back. But when it's cloudy and dark, they push in closer.
The guards have noticed it too. They've started keeping the fires higher on cloudy evenings.
reactions: I look at the sky before bed now: clear means I sleep well, overcast means I don't.

statCheck.pass: They use the cloud cover the same way we'd use a cloak: it hides them from the torches. Smart, in a way you'd rather not think about.
statCheck.fail: Dark makes them bolder. That's all I understand.

entries.intro: I sleep with socks on now. Started doing it a few weeks ago and my sleep has never been better.
details: My partner thinks it's ridiculous. Says only children sleep in socks.
But my feet stay warm and I don't wake up shivering. I call that a win.
reactions: I'll take warm feet over dignity any night of the week.

entries.intro: There's a screech owl that hunts near the settlement. You hear it around midnight: this piercing, ragged scream.
details: First time I heard it I was sure someone was hurt. Went running out with a lantern.
Now I know what it is and I just pull the blanket higher.
reactions: Knowing what a sound is helps. But it still gets under your skin every time.

statCheck.pass: They scream to claim territory and flush out prey. A loud owl is a hunting owl, which means the mice should be worried, not us.
statCheck.fail: It's an owl. That's the extent of my bird knowledge.

entries.intro: I've memorized the pattern of cracks on my ceiling. Stared at them enough nights to draw them from memory.
details: There's one that looks like a river, one that looks like a branch, and one that doesn't look like anything.
Sometimes I trace them with my eyes when I can't sleep. It's the most boring thing in the world, which is the point.
reactions: If I ever move, I'll miss that ceiling. It's been my companion through a lot of long nights.

entries.intro: Found a puddle of wax on the windowsill this morning. My candle must've tipped while I was asleep.
details: The wax ran across the sill and down the wall. Hard and white by the time I found it.
Nothing caught fire, which is the important thing. But it could have.
reactions: I need a proper candleholder. This 'balanced on a saucer' arrangement has run its course.

statCheck.pass: The draft from the window gap probably blew the flame sideways and melted the base unevenly. A deeper dish would hold it steady.
statCheck.fail: Candle fell and wax spilled. I'm lucky nothing burned.

entries.intro: The smell of night air is different from day air. Cooler, damper, with something green underneath.
details: You notice it most right at dusk when the transition happens. One breath is warm, the next is cool.
By full dark it's settled: that clean, heavy scent that only comes when the sun's gone.
reactions: I like the way night smells. That's a strange thing to say, but it's true.

entries.intro: I had a coughing fit in the middle of the night and couldn't stop for ten minutes.
details: Dry throat, dust in the air, something. Once it started I couldn't get it under control.
I'm sure the neighbors heard me. The walls don't stop that kind of noise.
reactions: I feel fine today. But at three in the morning I was convinced I was dying.

entries.intro: The night watch changed over right outside my window again. I heard every word of the handoff.
details: Nothing to report, all quiet, the usual. But they always say it louder than they need to.
Then the boots crunch off in both directions and it's quiet again.
reactions: I shouldn't complain. Those people are the reason I can sleep at all.

entries.intro: I tried sleeping without a pillow last night because someone told me it's better for your neck.
details: It is not better for your neck. Or if it is, my neck hasn't gotten the message.
I lasted about an hour before I reached down, grabbed the pillow, and accepted my limitations.
reactions: I'm too old to change how I sleep. The habits have won.

entries.intro: Last night was the kind of dark where you hold your hand in front of your face and see nothing. Absolutely nothing.
details: New moon, heavy clouds, no torches in range. The dark was total.
I stood in my doorway and couldn't see the ground at my feet.
reactions: That kind of dark makes you feel like the world has shrunk to the size of your body.

statCheck.pass: I'll admit it: I went back inside and lit every candle I had. The dark won that round.
statCheck.fail: It was dark and I went to bed. That's the whole of it.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} was sitting on the fence post when I went out before dawn. Just staring at me.
details: We looked at each other for a long moment: it didn't blink and neither did I.
Then I sneezed and it bolted. Moment over.
reactions: I like those little encounters. The ones where you and an animal briefly acknowledge each other.

entries.intro: My hearth makes a ticking sound as it cools down at night. Like a slow clock.
details: The stones expand when they're hot and contract as they cool. Every crack is a little tick.
I've learned to tell the time by how fast the ticking is: fast means the fire's just gone out, slow means it's been hours.
reactions: It's my house keeping time in its own way.

entries.intro: I've been having the same dream three nights running. Walking down a road that doesn't end.
details: It's always daytime in the dream, which is nice. Sunny, warm, just a road going forward.
I never arrive anywhere, I just walk. And somehow I wake up more tired.
reactions: I'd like to know where I'm going. Even the dream version of me seems confused.

entries.intro: The guard's torch hissed and went out right as he passed my window last night. Heard him swear under his breath.
details: There was a pause, some fumbling, then a spark and the light came back. All in the dark.
He muttered something about wet pitch and moved on.
reactions: I'm glad it's not my job. Standing in the dark with a dead torch sounds like a test I'd fail.

entries.intro: I woke up from a dream where I was falling and my whole body jerked. Scared my partner half to death.
details: You know that feeling: the ground drops out and your body panics before you're even awake.
My arm swung out and caught the water cup. Water everywhere.
reactions: I apologized. She wasn't ready to hear it at that hour.

statCheck.pass: It happens when you're falling asleep and your muscles relax too fast. Your body thinks you're actually falling and catches itself.
statCheck.fail: Bad dream, bad reflexes. That's all I've got.

entries.intro: There's a patch of moss on the north wall of my house that glows faintly at night. I only just noticed it.
details: It's barely visible: a soft green shimmer, close to the ground. You'd miss it if you weren't looking.
During the day it just looks like regular moss. But in the dark it gives off this gentle light.
reactions: It's oddly comforting. Like the house has its own nightlight.

statCheck.pass: Some mosses collect light during the day and release it slowly at night. Not magic, just the way the plant works on damp, shaded stone.
statCheck.fail: I don't know why it glows: it's moss. I'm not going to overthink it.

entries.intro: I heard the {mob_type} earlier than usual last night. Usually they don't start until well after dark.
details: The sun was barely down and I could already hear them {direction}. Earlier every week, it seems.
The guards have moved the perimeter torches lighting time back to match.
reactions: I liked it better when they waited until I was asleep.

statCheck.pass: The days are getting shorter. They're not starting earlier: the dark is just arriving sooner.
statCheck.fail: Maybe they're getting bolder. I don't know.

entries.intro: The owls were busy last night. I counted at least three different calls going back and forth.
details: Each one has a different pitch. You can tell them apart after a while: a deep one, a raspy one, and one that trills.
They were having a conversation across the rooftops for most of the night.
reactions: I've grown fond of them. At least someone's enjoying the dark.

entries.intro: I left my tools out overnight and the handles were cold enough this morning to hurt when I gripped them.
details: Iron holds the cold worse than anything. My hammer felt like I was gripping a block of ice.
I had to warm them by the fire for ten minutes before I could work.
reactions: That's laziness coming back to bite me. Should've brought them in.

entries.intro: I've noticed the settlement gets quieter earlier these days. Everyone's in by dusk.
details: Used to be you'd hear people on the paths until well after dark. Now the streets are empty by sunset.
Not sure if people are tired or nervous. Probably a bit of both.
reactions: I'm one of them. I don't linger outside the way I used to.

statCheck.pass: It started after we heard things close to the walls a few weeks back. Nobody talks about it, but everyone comes in earlier now.
statCheck.fail: People go to bed early. Shorter days, maybe.

entries.intro: I watched a spider spin a web in my window by candlelight last night. Took it about twenty minutes.
details: It worked methodically, ring by ring, without stopping. The candlelight caught every thread.
By the time it finished, it sat right in the center and went completely still.
reactions: I left the web alone. Felt wrong to destroy something that patient.

entries.intro: Woke up to rain so fine I could barely hear it. More like a whisper than a sound.
details: I only knew it was raining because the air coming through the window felt wet.
By first light it had coated everything in a thin sheen. The world looked varnished.
reactions: I like the gentle rains better than the heavy ones. They don't demand anything.

entries.intro: There's a loose tile on my roof that taps when the wind changes. I know every rhythm by now.
details: North wind: slow tap, west wind: fast rattle, east wind: nothing, it sits flat.
I could tell you the wind direction from bed with my eyes closed.
reactions: I should fix it. But it's the most reliable weather report I've got.

statCheck.pass: When the tile goes silent for a long stretch and then starts tapping fast, rain's about six hours out. Never been wrong.
statCheck.fail: It taps when the wind blows. That's the whole system.

entries.intro: I had a {wildlife_type} walk right past me during my watch last night. Close enough to touch.
details: It didn't see me: I was standing still in the shadow and it went right past like I was part of the wall.
I held my breath until it was gone. Felt like I was the one trespassing.
reactions: The night belongs to them more than it does to us.

entries.intro: My candle threw a shadow on the wall that looked like a face for a moment. My own face, distorted.
details: It was just the angle: my shadow hitting a crease in the plaster. But for a second my blood went cold.
I moved the candle and it became a shapeless blob again. Harmless.
reactions: Candlelight is kind during dinner and cruel during insomnia.

statCheck.pass: Your mind is wired to find faces, even where there are none. In low light it gets desperate and sees them everywhere.
statCheck.fail: Shadows look like things sometimes. That's all it was.

entries.intro: I found a snail trail across my floor this morning. A long silver line from the door to the hearth.
details: It must've come in through the crack under the door and made a beeline for the warmth.
Found the snail behind the woodpile, tucked into its shell. Living its best life.
reactions: I put it outside. It'll probably be back tonight.

entries.intro: There's a gap in the fence where the torchlight doesn't cover. I've asked about it twice.
details: It's maybe twenty paces wide. Not enough for anything to get through quickly, but it's a blind spot.
The guards know about it and say they'll add a torch when they have the bracket. Still waiting.
reactions: Probably fine. But I'd sleep better if it were lit.

statCheck.pass: I've been hanging my own lantern on the fence post nearest the gap. It's not much, but it's better than nothing.
statCheck.fail: I just want more light. Doesn't seem like a big ask.

entries.intro: The sunset painted everything red last night. Even the water in the trough turned the color of rust.
details: It held for longer than usual. The whole settlement was bathed in this warm, fading glow.
People slowed down. You could see them pausing in doorways, looking west.
reactions: A good sunset is free and everyone gets one. Hard to beat that.

entries.intro: I've started leaving my boots by the door at night. Lined up, laces tucked in, ready to go.
details: Wasn't always like this. Used to kick them off wherever they landed.
Now I want them within arm's reach. Just in case I need to move quickly in the dark.
reactions: It's a small thing, but it makes the night feel a little less uncertain.

statCheck.pass: I know it's about control. When you can't control the dark, you control the things you can: your boots, your door, your candle.
statCheck.fail: It's just a habit. Doesn't mean anything.

entries.intro: The smoke from the chimneys hangs low on cold nights. By morning the whole settlement smells like a forge.
details: It settles into the alleys and just sits there. Thick enough you can taste it.
Some mornings you can't see the end of the street through the haze.
reactions: Means everyone's got their fires lit, at least. Warm houses make up for smoky mornings.

statCheck.pass: Smoke hangs when the air above is warmer than the air below, trapping it in a layer. Happens most on clear, cold nights.
statCheck.fail: Smoke goes down instead of up sometimes. I don't know the reason.

entries.intro: There was a {wildlife_type} in the road this morning that wouldn't move. Just sat there while everyone walked around it.
details: It had clearly been out all night and wasn't in any rush. Yawning, blinking, completely unbothered.
Someone tried to shoo it and it just looked at them.
reactions: I respect the attitude. We could all use a bit more of that.

entries.intro: I could hear something chewing under the floorboards last night. Methodical, persistent.
details: It went on for hours. This steady gnawing sound, like teeth on wood.
I stamped on the floor and it stopped for about ten seconds, then started again.
reactions: There's something living under my house and it's making itself comfortable.

statCheck.pass: That's mice or rats working on the joists. If you can smell sawdust in the morning, they're through the soft wood and into the frame.
statCheck.fail: Something's chewing. I don't want to know what.

entries.intro: I like the moment just after you blow out the candle. The dark rushes in and then your eyes start adjusting.
details: First there's nothing, then shapes, then the window emerges and the furniture and the walls.
After ten minutes you can see almost everything. The dark isn't as dark as it first pretends.
reactions: Your eyes are better than you give them credit for. They just need time.

entries.intro: The wind was warm last night for the first time in weeks. Felt like something had finally turned.
details: It came from {direction}, soft and steady, carrying the smell of earth and growing things.
I opened the window wide and just stood there breathing it in.
reactions: Winter ends slowly, then all at once. Last night was the all at once.

entries.intro: I heard two cats yowling at each other from opposite rooftops last night. Just screaming back and forth.
details: It started low and escalated into this ear-splitting duet. Sounded like a murder in progress.
Someone threw open a shutter and yelled at them. They paused, then started right back up.
reactions: Cats don't care about your sleep, your opinions, or your shutters.

entries.intro: I found a bird asleep on my windowsill this morning. Tucked up, head under its wing, dead to the world.
details: It was so small. Just this tiny bundle of feathers pressed against the glass.
I opened the window slowly and it didn't stir until the light hit it. Then it blinked and flew off.
reactions: It picked my window. I don't know why that made me feel good, but it did.

entries.intro: I keep a knife by the bed and haven't needed it once. But I'd rather have it and not need it.
details: Some nights I reach over and touch the handle just to know it's there. Then I roll over and go back to sleep.
My partner thinks I'm being dramatic. I think I'm being practical.
reactions: Everybody's got something that makes them feel safe. Mine just happens to have an edge.

statCheck.pass: Honestly, if something came through the door I'd probably fumble it. The knife is for my nerves, not for a fight.
statCheck.fail: It's just there and it makes me feel better. That's enough.

entries.intro: The morning mist was glowing gold when I stepped out today. The sun was just hitting it.
details: Everything was soft and warm and hazy. The fences, the rooftops, the people: all wrapped in gold light.
It lasted maybe ten minutes before the mist burned off. But those ten minutes were something.
reactions: You have to be awake early to see it, and most people miss it. Their loss.

entries.intro: I dreamed the settlement was underwater last night. Everything slow and blue and floating.
details: People were walking around like normal, just slower. The rooftops had seaweed on them.
I was trying to talk and bubbles came out instead of words.
reactions: I woke up gasping. Dreams where you can't speak are the worst kind.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} were quiet last night. First time in a while.
details: No howling, no movement, no nothing. Just the normal night sounds.
The guard said the same thing. He seemed uneasy about it.
reactions: Funny how their silence is almost worse than their noise. You start wondering where they went.

statCheck.pass: Quiet usually means they've moved to hunt elsewhere for a few days, cycling through areas. They'll be back.
statCheck.fail: Could mean anything. I'd rather hear them and know where they are.

entries.intro: I burned my tongue on soup before bed and it kept me up. Couldn't stop running my tongue over the sore spot.
details: You know how a tiny burn on the tongue becomes the only thing in the world? That.
Water didn't help, bread didn't help. Just had to wait for it to stop throbbing.
reactions: Lost a night's sleep to a spoonful of soup. That's the kind of defeat you can't even be angry about.

entries.intro: There's a particular star that's always in the same spot when I look out my window at night. Low and bright.
details: It's the first one that appears after sunset and the last one I see before sleep.
I don't know its name. But I look for it every night and feel better when it's there.
reactions: Reliable things are rare. Even if it's just a dot of light, I appreciate the consistency.

entries.intro: I heard the {poi_type} door creak open in the wind last night. Back and forth, slow and steady.
details: In the daylight it's just a door. At night it sounds like the building is breathing.
I almost got up to close it, but that would've meant walking there in the dark.
reactions: I let it creak. My bed won out over my annoyance.

entries.intro: My feet get cold first, then the cold crawls upward. By midnight it's reached my knees.
details: I've tried extra blankets, I've tried tucking them under. The cold finds a way in.
Some nights I end up curled into a ball with only my face exposed.
reactions: I'd give a lot for a warmer house, or warmer feet. Either would do.

statCheck.pass: Heating a stone by the fire and wrapping it in cloth works. Put it at the foot of the bed and it stays warm for hours.
statCheck.fail: Cold feet, cold night. I just endure it.

entries.intro: The clouds broke apart around midnight and suddenly the room filled with moonlight. Woke me right up.
details: It was like someone had thrown the curtains open. Bright as a late afternoon, only silver instead of gold.
I blinked at the ceiling for a while, then the clouds closed again and it was gone.
reactions: Moonlight in a dark room is startling. Even when it's beautiful.

entries.intro: A mouse ran across my arm while I was sleeping. That's how I learned I had mice.
details: The feeling of tiny feet on bare skin woke me faster than any alarm could. I threw myself upright.
It was gone before I could even see it. Just a whisper of weight and then nothing.
reactions: I've set traps now. But I still flinch at any tickle on my arm.

statCheck.pass: Check along the base of the walls for droppings and small holes. Mice follow the same paths every night: block those and they'll move on.
statCheck.fail: I just set traps and hoped for the best. Not exactly a plan.

entries.intro: The night was so warm I dragged my bedroll outside and slept under the stars.
details: The ground was hard but the air was perfect. Cool enough to breathe, warm enough to be comfortable.
I could see the whole sky from edge to edge. More sky than I've seen in months.
reactions: Slept better outside than I have indoors all week. Makes you wonder about walls.

entries.intro: I've been grinding my teeth in my sleep. Woke up with my jaw aching.
details: My partner says I do it when I'm worried. I didn't think I was worried about anything, but my jaw disagrees.
The teeth marks on the inside of my cheek say otherwise too.
reactions: The body keeps score of things the mind tries to ignore.

statCheck.pass: I know exactly what's bothering me, I just don't want to say it out loud. Saying it makes it real.
statCheck.fail: Bodies do strange things. Doesn't always mean something.

entries.intro: I noticed the fireflies move in patterns last night. Not random at all: they drift one way, then the other, in unison.
details: Like a slow tide of light moving through the grass. Left, pause, right, pause.
I watched for a while trying to figure out who was leading. None of them seemed to be.
reactions: Tiny lights with their own logic. I found it oddly reassuring.

entries.intro: The watch horn blew once last night: short blast, then silence. My heart stopped.
details: I lay there rigid, waiting for the second blast that means trouble. It never came.
Found out this morning it was a false alarm. The guard thought he saw movement and reacted.
reactions: That one blast aged me a year. I could feel every second of the silence after it.

statCheck.pass: One blast is an alert, two blasts means confirmed threat, three means everyone up and armed. We drill it, but hearing it for real is different.
statCheck.fail: I just know the horn means something bad. The details escape me in the moment.

entries.intro: I've been sleeping with my socks on and off all night. Can't find the right temperature.
details: On: too warm, off: too cold, on again: still too warm. It never balances.
I must've reached down twenty times last night.
reactions: It shouldn't be this complicated. It's a sock.

entries.intro: Saw a falling star last night while I was on watch, and then another, and then a third.
details: All in the same part of the sky, within a few minutes. Like the sky was shedding sparks.
I called out to the other guard to look, but he was facing the wrong way and missed all three.
reactions: Three wishes, if you believe in that. I made mine just in case.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} left something dead outside the fence last night. Bones and scraps.
details: Whatever it was, they ate most of it out there. Just the remains left behind.
The guard cleaned it up before the kids could see it.
reactions: They were eating that close to the walls. That's the part that sits wrong.

statCheck.pass: They eat where they feel safe. If they're comfortable enough to sit and feed near the fence, they don't see it as a threat.
statCheck.fail: I don't want to think about what it means. I'd rather just not.

entries.intro: My pillow was damp when I woke up this morning. Must've been sweating in my sleep.
details: Had to flip it to the dry side, but by then I was awake. No going back.
I don't remember any dreams. Just woke up soaked and confused.
reactions: Bodies do strange things in the night. I try not to read into it.

statCheck.pass: Night sweats usually mean the room's too warm or the blankets are too heavy. But sometimes it's just stress the body won't let go of.
statCheck.fail: Sweating happens. I changed the pillow and moved on.

entries.intro: I've started recognizing individual guard footsteps at night. Heavy boots for the tall one, quick steps for the new recruit.
details: It's not something I tried to learn. Just happened from lying awake too many nights.
There's a third one who drags his right foot slightly. I've never asked why.
reactions: Insomnia makes you an expert in things nobody asked about.

statCheck.pass: I can tell you the exact time of each guard change based on the footsteps. My sleeplessness has turned me into a very specific kind of clock.
statCheck.fail: I hear things at night. It doesn't amount to anything useful.

entries.intro: There were clouds shaped like mountains on the horizon last night. Huge and dark, lit from behind by the moon.
details: They looked solid enough to walk on. Towering up, ridge after ridge, like a whole range of shadow peaks.
By morning they'd flattened out into nothing. Just thin grey haze.
reactions: The night sky puts on shows that nobody asked for and nobody watches.

entries.intro: I keep a spare candle in my pocket when I'm on watch. Just in case the main one goes out.
details: Lost my light once on a cloudy night and couldn't find my way back to the post for ten minutes.
Never again. Now I've always got a backup.
reactions: One candle between you and total dark makes you appreciate how fragile light is.

statCheck.pass: I keep the wick pre-charred so it catches faster. You lose less time relighting if the tip is already black.
statCheck.fail: I just carry an extra. Nothing clever about it.

entries.intro: I woke up to the most complete silence I've ever heard: no wind, no animals, no house sounds.
details: It was so quiet I could hear the blood in my ears. A soft, rushing hiss.
I lay perfectly still and just listened to my own body keeping itself alive.
reactions: In that kind of silence, you realize you're never truly alone. Your own heartbeat sees to that.

entries.intro: The last thing I do every night is check the door latch twice. It's become a rule.
details: I check it, walk to bed, then think 'did I actually check it?' and go back.
I know I checked it, I always check it. But the doubt is stronger than the memory.
reactions: Night turns every habit into a ritual and every ritual into a necessity.

statCheck.pass: It's not really about the latch. It's about needing one last thing to be certain before you close your eyes.
statCheck.fail: I just like to make sure. No deeper reason.

entries.intro: I rolled over in bed last night and my shoulder popped so loud it woke me up. Thought something had snapped.
details: Lay there for a minute flexing my arm, making sure it still worked. It did, but the adrenaline took an hour to fade.
My partner didn't even stir. I'm lying there in a panic and they're breathing like nothing happened.
reactions: Getting older at night is worse than getting older during the day.

entries.intro: There was a {wildlife_type} on the roof last night. I could hear it walking around up there.
details: Little scratching steps, back and forth, like it was looking for something. Then a thump and silence.
I think it jumped off the far side. Found some scratch marks in the thatch this morning.
reactions: It's welcome to the roof. I just wish it would pick quieter hours.

entries.intro: The dew was so heavy this morning that water was dripping off the eaves like it had rained. Must've been a cold clear night.
details: Everything outside was soaked: the benches, the fence posts, the firewood stack I forgot to cover.
The grass was silver with it. Looked like the whole settlement had been dipped in water overnight.
reactions: Pretty to look at. Less pretty when you sit on a wet bench without checking first.

entries.intro: I went to bed early last night and slept straight through to dawn. First time in weeks.
details: No waking up, no strange noises, no tossing. Just solid dark and then sunlight.
I actually woke up feeling rested. I'd forgotten what that was like.
reactions: One good night doesn't fix a month of bad ones, but it helps you believe they're possible.

entries.intro: Heard {mob_type} fighting among themselves {direction} last night. Awful racket.
details: Snarling and screeching for a while, then a loud yelp, then quiet. Sounded like it ended badly for one of them.
The guards were alert but it stayed out there. Nothing came close.
reactions: At least when they're fighting each other they're not bothering us.

statCheck.pass: They fight over food and territory, same as anything. When they're loud about it, it usually means the group is splitting up.
statCheck.fail: I don't know what sets them off. I just want them to keep it out there.

entries.intro: I tried counting stars last night to fall asleep. Got to about two hundred before I gave up.
details: Every time I thought I'd counted a section, I'd notice more behind the ones I'd already seen.
My eyes started watering from staring up. But it was more peaceful than staring at the ceiling.
reactions: Better than counting sheep. At least the stars don't move.

entries.intro: Woke up freezing last night because the blanket had slid off the bed entirely. Found it crumpled on the floor.
details: I must've kicked it off in my sleep. My legs were so cold they'd gone stiff.
Pulled it back up and curled into it, but it took ages to warm up again.
reactions: I need a heavier blanket. Or smaller feet.

entries.intro: There's a particular star that sits right in the gap between my shutters. I see it every night from bed.
details: It's bright enough to cast a tiny point of light on the wall opposite. Moves slowly across as the night goes on.
I've started thinking of it as mine. Nobody else sees it from the same angle.
reactions: It's a small thing, but it makes the dark feel less empty.

entries.intro: I heard a baby crying somewhere in the settlement last night. Went on for ages.
details: You could hear the parents trying to hush it: walking back and forth, that desperate shushing sound.
It finally stopped around the time I was ready to get up myself.
reactions: I felt for them. That kind of tired is its own punishment.

entries.intro: The guards lit a bonfire near the gate last night. Felt good seeing that glow from my window.
details: I could smell the woodsmoke and hear them talking. Quiet voices and the crackle of the fire.
There's something reassuring about knowing someone's up and alert while you're trying to sleep.
reactions: I slept better than usual. The light was like a promise that someone was keeping watch.

entries.intro: A moth got into the house last night and spent an hour battering itself against my candle. Over and over.
details: I tried to shoo it out three times. Each time it circled right back to the flame.
Eventually I just blew the candle out and sat in the dark until it found the window.
reactions: Something about watching it was exhausting. That kind of pointless determination.

entries.intro: My knees ache worse at night. Something about lying still makes every joint announce itself.
details: During the day I'm moving and don't notice. But the moment I stop, my body starts cataloguing complaints.
I shift position every few minutes trying to find one that doesn't hurt.
reactions: The night is when your body reminds you of everything you did to it during the day.

entries.intro: I spotted a {wildlife_type} crossing the path while I was on watch. It froze when my torch caught its eyes.
details: Two bright spots of reflected light, perfectly still. We stared at each other for a long moment.
Then it blinked and slipped into the dark so fast I almost doubted I'd seen it.
reactions: The night belongs to them more than it belongs to us.

entries.intro: I had a dream about someone I haven't thought of in years. Woke up sad for no reason I could explain.
details: The dream wasn't even bad. Just ordinary: talking, laughing, sitting somewhere warm.
But waking up and realizing none of it was real hit harder than it should've.
reactions: Dreams are cruel when they give you things you can't keep.

entries.intro: Found {mob_type} tracks in the soft mud this morning, right along the fence line.
details: They followed the fence for a ways and then veered off {direction}. Didn't come through, just skirted the edge.
The prints were fresh enough that the mud hadn't dried in them yet.
reactions: Close enough to hear us, probably. That thought sticks with me.

statCheck.pass: The stride was even and unhurried. Not hunting, not fleeing: just moving through. They probably do it every night and we only notice when the mud's soft enough.
statCheck.fail: Tracks are tracks. I can't tell much beyond that they were here.

entries.intro: Woke up in the middle of the night and couldn't remember where I was for a few seconds. Just darkness and confusion.
details: My hand found the wall and I remembered. But for those few heartbeats, I was completely lost.
It's happened before, always in the deepest dark. The room feels wrong until your brain catches up.
reactions: It passes quick, but the feeling lingers. Like the ground shifted under you and shifted back.

entries.intro: I sat outside with a mug of warm water last night because I couldn't sleep. The quiet was actually nice.
details: No wind, just the distant sound of the creek and the occasional shuffle of the guard's boots.
I watched the steam curl off the mug and disappear into the cold air.
reactions: Sometimes giving up on sleep is better than fighting it.

entries.intro: The wind changed direction around midnight and suddenly I could smell the fields. Turned the whole room earthy.
details: Wet soil and cut stalks and something green. Stronger than you'd expect through a closed window.
I lay there breathing it in. Reminded me of working outside as a kid.
reactions: Funny how a smell can take you somewhere else entirely.

entries.intro: I left a window open last night and a {wildlife_type} must have come right up to the house. Heard it sniffing at the sill.
details: This soft, steady snuffling sound inches from my head. I held my breath and listened.
It lost interest after a minute and I heard it pad away into the grass.
reactions: I wasn't scared, exactly. More aware of how thin the wall is between inside and outside.

entries.intro: My fire died faster than I expected last night. Woke up to a room so cold my teeth were chattering.
details: The ashes were completely grey: not even a coal left. Must've gone out hours ago.
I wrapped the blanket around my shoulders and relit it, but my fingers were so stiff I could barely strike a spark.
reactions: I should've banked it properly. I know better.

statCheck.pass: The trick is to bury a hot coal in ash before bed. Insulates it. In the morning you've still got a live ember to work with.
statCheck.fail: Fires go out. I'll try harder tonight.

entries.intro: I dreamed I was swimming in the dark. Not scary, just floating in warm water with no edges.
details: There was no sense of up or down: just drifting. And the water was exactly body temperature.
I woke up gradually and almost didn't want to. The bed felt heavy and solid after all that floating.
reactions: I'd go back to that dream if I could. It was the most peaceful I've felt in a while.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} were howling {direction} last night, one after another like they were taking turns.
details: One voice would rise and fade, then another would pick up from a different spot. Went around like that for an hour.
You could hear them moving position between calls: getting further away, then circling back.
reactions: Hard to sleep when you're tracking something by sound whether you want to or not.

statCheck.pass: They call to keep the group together while they move. The circling means they're covering ground, not closing in. If they go quiet, that's when you worry.
statCheck.fail: Howling is howling. I pulled the blanket up and waited for it to stop.

entries.intro: The lantern outside the {poi_type} throws this warm circle of light you can see from across the settlement.
details: On a dark night it's the brightest thing out there. Like a little island in all the black.
I've caught myself just looking at it from my window. Something about a steady light is calming.
reactions: I'm glad someone keeps it lit.

entries.intro: I had this ringing in my ears last night that wouldn't stop. High-pitched, constant, maddening.
details: It wasn't coming from outside: it was inside my head. Every time I thought it was fading it came back louder.
I pressed my pillow over my ears and that just made the ringing echo.
reactions: It was gone by morning. But those hours in between felt very long.

statCheck.pass: Happens after a loud day of work. The ears keep hearing a sound that isn't there anymore. It fades once they've rested.
statCheck.fail: I don't know what causes it. I just know I hate it.

entries.intro: I woke up and the moonlight was falling right across my hands on the blanket. Bright enough to see every line.
details: I just lay there looking at my own hands in this cold white light. They looked older than I remember.
The moon moved on eventually and the light slid off the bed and onto the floor.
reactions: Strange things you notice when you can't sleep.

entries.intro: Someone was whistling a tune outside late last night. Just a few notes, repeating.
details: It was faint and slightly off-key. The kind of absent whistling you do when you don't know you're doing it.
It faded as they walked further away, then I could barely hear the last few notes before it was gone.
reactions: I've had that melody stuck in my head all morning and I don't even know the song.

entries.intro: I pulled my curtain back last night and saw the most enormous moon sitting right on the horizon. Orange as a ember.
details: It was so low and so big it didn't look real. Like someone had hung a lantern at the edge of the world.
Watched it rise until it shrank and turned white again. Took about an hour.
reactions: Nights like that make me glad I don't always sleep through the dark.

entries.intro: I stepped on the cat on my way to the privy last night. It screamed and I screamed.
details: The cat shot under the bed and knocked something over. I stood there in the dark with my heart hammering.
It took ten minutes of coaxing to get it to come back out. It looked deeply offended.
reactions: We've both agreed to blame the darkness.

entries.intro: The frogs started up all at once last night, like someone gave them a signal. A wall of croaking from every direction.
details: Hundreds of them, all slightly different pitches. It blended into this deep, pulsing drone.
It stopped just as suddenly around midnight. Total silence, then one last croak, then nothing.
reactions: I respect the commitment. They give it everything and then they're done.

statCheck.pass: They sing when the temperature and humidity are just right. If conditions change, they all stop together because the trigger is gone.
statCheck.fail: Frogs do what frogs do. I just wish they'd do it quieter.

entries.intro: I sleep facing the wall now. I used to face the room, but the shapes the dark makes got to me.
details: A coat on a hook, a chair with a blanket draped over it: they all look like someone standing there.
I know they're objects. Knowing doesn't help at two in the morning.
reactions: The wall is boring. Boring is what I want at night.

entries.intro: My neighbor's fire was still smoking this morning: that thin grey line going straight up in the still air.
details: No wind at all. The smoke rose in a perfect column until it dissolved way up above the rooftops.
The whole settlement smelled of it. That cold-morning, woodsmoke smell that sticks to everything.
reactions: There's something honest about a fire that's been burning all night. Means someone kept it going.

entries.intro: I heard {mob_type} close to the walls last night. Not distant: close enough that the guard shouted at them.
details: There was a clatter and some noise, then the guard banged his shield and they moved off.
They didn't go far, though. You could still hear them shuffling around out past the torchlight.
reactions: The guard handled it. But I stayed awake a good while after.

statCheck.pass: They test the perimeter when it's dark enough. A loud response sends them off: they're not looking for a fight, just an easy way in.
statCheck.fail: I don't know what they wanted. I'm just glad the guard was there.

entries.intro: I woke up to birds singing before the sun was even up. The sky was still grey and they were already going.
details: One started and then more joined in, one at a time, until the whole dawn chorus was rolling.
I lay there and listened to it build. Better than any alarm.
reactions: If I have to be awake early, that's the way I'd choose.

entries.intro: I've been grinding my teeth in my sleep. Woke up this morning with a sore jaw and a headache.
details: My partner says I do it when I'm stressed. The sound is apparently awful.
I don't remember any of it. Just the ache when I open my mouth.
reactions: My body handles worry on its own schedule and never tells me about it until morning.

entries.intro: I found a {wildlife_type} sleeping under my porch this morning. Curled up tight in the corner.
details: It must've been there all night. Didn't flinch when I walked past: just tucked its nose in deeper.
I left it alone. There's room enough.
reactions: Nice to know my porch is considered a safe spot by someone.

entries.intro: There's a drip in my ceiling that only appears at night. During the day, nothing. After dark, tap tap tap.
details: I've put a pot under it. The sound of water hitting tin is better than water hitting the floor, at least.
I've checked the roof twice and can't find the source. It's not where the drip lands: the water must be running along a beam.
reactions: I'm learning to sleep through a slow, rhythmic tapping. It's a specialized skill.

statCheck.pass: It's condensation, not a leak. The roof cools faster than the room and moisture collects on the cold wood, then runs to the lowest point and drips.
statCheck.fail: Water comes in. I put a pot under it. That's the extent of my understanding.

entries.intro: The stars seemed closer last night. I know they weren't, but the air was so clear they looked like you could reach up and touch them.
details: I could see the faint ones you normally miss: the gaps between the bright ones were full of tiny points of light.
It made the sky look crowded. More stars than dark.
reactions: Nights that clear make everything else feel sharper too. The cold, the quiet, even the sounds.

entries.intro: I tried sleeping with the window closed last night because of the cold. Nearly suffocated.
details: The room got stuffy within an hour. That thick, stale air that sits on your chest.
Opened it a crack and the cold hit like a slap. But at least I could breathe.
reactions: There's no winning that argument: freeze or suffocate. I chose freeze.

entries.intro: I was on the late watch when a shooting star cut right across the sky. Long trail, bright green at the end.
details: It lasted two or three heartbeats and then it was gone. Just a streak of light from one horizon to the other.
Nobody else saw it. The other guard was looking the wrong way.
reactions: Moments like that belong to whoever happened to be looking up.

entries.intro: A door somewhere in the settlement kept banging in the wind last night. Far off, but perfectly regular.
details: Every ten seconds or so: bang. Then the wind would gust and it'd come faster: bang bang bang.
I counted the intervals for a while. Then I counted how many times I counted the intervals. That's the kind of night it was.
reactions: You can get angry at a door you can't see. I proved that.

entries.intro: I could hear the creek from my bed last night. The water must be high: normally it's too far away.
details: A steady rushing sound, constant and even. Like the night was breathing.
When the wind died down it got louder. When the wind picked up it blended in.
reactions: Running water at a distance is one of the better sounds to sleep to.

entries.intro: My candle threw a shadow of myself on the wall last night that didn't look like me. Too tall, too thin.
details: Every time I moved, it moved wrong. The proportions were all stretched and the edges wavered.
I blew the candle out and sat in the dark instead. That was somehow less unsettling.
reactions: I know how shadows work. That doesn't mean I have to like what they do.

entries.intro: Someone's rooster started crowing at the wrong hour last night. The moon was still high.
details: It went three or four times and then quit, like it realized its mistake.
Half the settlement probably woke up thinking it was dawn. I certainly did.
reactions: I've never wanted to strangle a bird more.

entries.intro: I watched the clouds move across the moon last night from my window. Felt like the moon was sailing and the clouds were still.
details: Each cloud would dim the light, then pass, and the brightness would pour back. Like a slow pulse.
Sometimes the moon vanished completely for a minute, then reappeared with a silver halo.
reactions: I could've watched it for hours. Almost did.

entries.intro: There's a mouse in my walls. I can hear it running back and forth inside the boards at night.
details: Tiny fast scratching sounds that move from one end of the room to the other in a few seconds.
It stops when I knock on the wall, then starts again two minutes later.
reactions: It lives here now. I've accepted that.

statCheck.pass: It's probably nesting. They come inside when it's cold and leave again in spring. Easier to wait it out than seal every gap.
statCheck.fail: I hear it. It hears me knocking. Neither of us can do anything about the other.

entries.intro: I brought a blanket outside and lay on the grass to watch the sky last night. My neighbor thought I'd collapsed.
details: She came running over with a lantern asking if I was hurt. I was just stargazing.
We ended up lying there together for a while. She pointed out shapes in the stars I'd never noticed.
reactions: Good company on a clear night. Sometimes the best things start with a misunderstanding.

entries.intro: I keep a knife by the bed at night. I've never needed it. But it's there.
details: It's not even a good knife: just a dull paring blade from the kitchen.
But knowing it's within reach lets my brain shut off enough to sleep.
reactions: It's not about the knife. It's about having something to reach for.

statCheck.pass: I know it wouldn't help much in a real situation. But the mind doesn't need logic at night: it needs a prop.
statCheck.fail: I keep it close. That's all.

entries.intro: The night smelled different last night. That sharp, mineral smell that comes before snow.
details: It was in every breath: cold and clean and slightly metallic. The air felt thinner.
Sure enough, there was a dusting of white on the ground by morning.
reactions: Some people can smell rain coming. I can smell the cold.

entries.intro: The guard fell asleep at his post last night. I saw him slumped against the fence when I went for water.
details: His torch was guttering and his chin was on his chest. Out cold.
I tapped his shoulder and he snapped awake looking terrified. I told him I hadn't seen anything.
reactions: I'm not going to hold it against him. Those shifts are brutal.

statCheck.pass: The body can only fight sleep for so long. If he'd had a relief shift he'd have stayed awake. We don't schedule enough guards.
statCheck.fail: People fall asleep. He's not the first and won't be the last.

entries.intro: I woke up tangled in my blankets so thoroughly I thought I was trapped. Panicked for a few seconds.
details: One arm was pinned under my body and the blanket had twisted around my legs. I couldn't move for a moment.
By the time I fought free I was wide awake and sweating.
reactions: My own bedding tried to restrain me. I've slept on top of the covers since.

entries.intro: I lit a fire outside last night and just watched it. Sometimes that's all you need.
details: The flames found shapes in the wood: arches and caves and towers that built and collapsed in seconds.
The heat kept the cold back just far enough. My face was warm and my back was freezing.
reactions: Fire is the oldest entertainment there is. Still holds up.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} was calling somewhere {direction} all night. Same sound, over and over, steady as a heartbeat.
details: You'd think it would get tiresome, but after a while it became a kind of rhythm. Almost restful.
It stopped just before dawn, like it knew the sun was coming.
reactions: I might've actually slept better because of it. A steady sound is easier than random silence.

entries.intro: I accidentally knocked my water jug off the table in the dark last night. Sounded like the world was ending.
details: Clay hitting stone in a quiet house at midnight is louder than you'd ever believe.
I stood there barefoot in a puddle of cold water holding the handle, which was the only piece that survived.
reactions: The silence afterward felt judgmental.

entries.intro: I've noticed a pattern in the guard's rounds. The creak of the gate, then footsteps, then quiet. Every quarter hour.
details: It's become my clock at night. Four rounds and I know an hour has passed.
When the pattern breaks, when the footsteps are late, I notice immediately. That's the part I don't like.
reactions: Predictability is comforting until you start depending on it.

statCheck.pass: I've timed it enough to know when the guard changes shift. The new one walks faster. Takes me a round to adjust.
statCheck.fail: I hear boots, I relax. I don't hear boots, I don't. Simple as that.

entries.intro: There was ice on the inside of my window this morning. Fern patterns etched across the whole pane.
details: Each one was intricate: curling fronds and spirals, like someone had drawn them with a needle.
I breathed on it and the patterns melted into a clear spot. The cold filled it back in within minutes.
reactions: Beautiful, but it tells me the room got far too cold while I slept.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} were quieter than usual last night. I didn't hear a thing from them.
details: Normally there's at least some noise {direction} after dark. Last night: nothing.
The guard said the same thing. No sounds, no signs.
reactions: Somehow the silence is worse than the noise. At least when they're loud you know where they are.

statCheck.pass: They go quiet when they're on the move. Noise means they're settled in one spot. Silence means they've shifted somewhere else.
statCheck.fail: Maybe they left. Maybe they didn't. I don't know how to read it.

entries.intro: I talked in my sleep last night, apparently. My partner told me I said something about keys.
details: I don't remember any dreams about keys. But she said I was quite insistent about finding the right one.
Sounded like a whole conversation with someone who wasn't there.
reactions: I live a secret life at night that I know nothing about. Unsettling.

entries.intro: The sunset yesterday was nothing special, but the afterglow hung around for ages. This soft pink light that wouldn't leave.
details: It was still there when the first stars appeared. Pink sky at the bottom, black sky at the top, stars in between.
I sat on the step and watched it fade. Took its time about it.
reactions: Sometimes the quiet endings are better than the dramatic ones.

entries.intro: My bed frame creaks every time I move. Every single time.
details: Roll left: creak. Roll right: creak. Adjust the pillow: creak.
I've tried tightening the joints, wedging cloth in the gaps. Nothing works. The bed has opinions.
reactions: I'm convinced the bed is louder than my snoring. My partner disagrees.

entries.intro: I watched a spider build a web across my window frame last night by candlelight. Took it about an hour.
details: It worked outward from the center in these perfect spirals: one line at a time, never a mistake.
By the time it was done the whole web caught the candlelight and glowed like gold thread.
reactions: I'll knock it down in the morning. But for an hour last night it was the most interesting thing in the world.

entries.intro: I keep waking up on the wrong side of the bed. Literally. I go to sleep on the right and wake up on the left.
details: I don't remember rolling over. It just happens. My partner's given up trying to keep her half.
This morning my pillow was at the foot of the bed. I don't know what I'm doing in my sleep.
reactions: I'm exhausted. Whatever I'm up to at night sounds like hard work.

entries.intro: There was a warm breeze last night: unusual for this time of year. It came through the window like summer had gotten lost.
details: You could smell flowers in it, or something close. Sweet and green and out of season.
It lasted about an hour and then the cold settled back in.
reactions: For a little while, the night felt gentle instead of something to endure.

entries.intro: I heard something scraping against the outside wall of my house last night. Slow and deliberate.
details: I lay there listening, trying to figure out what kind of thing makes that sound. Rough, like bark on stone.
After a while I got up and looked, but there was nothing. Just marks in the dust.
reactions: Probably a branch in the wind. I'm going with probably.

statCheck.pass: There's a vine growing up the wall that I keep meaning to cut back. When the wind catches it, the woody stem drags across the stone.
statCheck.fail: Scraping noises in the dark. I didn't investigate further.

entries.intro: My kid crawled into bed with me around midnight saying she had a bad dream. Took up the whole bed within minutes.
details: She sleeps sideways. One foot in my ribs, one arm across my face. Somehow comfortable enough to snore.
I spent the rest of the night balanced on the edge of the mattress, afraid to move.
reactions: I got maybe two hours of sleep. She got the best night of her life.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} left a smell behind when they passed through last night. You could still catch it on the morning air.
details: A sour, rank smell: like something rotting mixed with wet fur. It clung to the fence and the ground near the path.
Even after hours it hadn't faded. Had to breathe through my mouth while I did the morning chores.
reactions: Now I know what they smell like. I could have lived without that information.

statCheck.pass: They mark territory with scent. If the smell's strong, they spent time here. If it's faint, they were just passing.
statCheck.fail: It smelled terrible. That's all I can tell you.

entries.intro: I used to be afraid of the dark as a child. I thought I'd grown out of it.
details: Most nights I'm fine. But every now and then something shifts in the shadows and I'm six years old again.
My hands go cold and my chest tightens and I have to remind myself that I'm an adult in my own home.
reactions: You never really outgrow it. You just get better at pretending.

statCheck.pass: I keep a tinderbox within arm's reach every night. If the feeling gets bad, having light two seconds away makes all the difference.
statCheck.fail: It's just the dark. Nothing to talk about.

entries.intro: I watched the torchlight flicker on the ceiling last night until it put me to sleep. Best trick I know.
details: The light dances in a pattern that's almost regular but never quite repeats. It holds your eyes just enough to quiet your thoughts.
I woke up with the candle burned to a stub and a warm room. Worth the wax.
reactions: Some people count sheep. I watch light move.

entries.intro: I think there's a bird nesting in my chimney. I can hear it shuffling around when the fire's out.
details: Little scratchy sounds and the occasional soft coo, echoing down the flue.
I can't light a fire until it moves out. So I've been cold, and the bird's been warm.
reactions: It found the warmest spot in my house and claimed it. I respect the strategy.

statCheck.pass: They usually nest for a few weeks and then the young ones fledge and they all leave. Easier to wait than to try to evict them.
statCheck.fail: It's up there and I'm down here. We've reached an arrangement.

entries.intro: The wind howled so loud last night it drowned out everything else. Like the whole sky was angry.
details: Gusts hit the walls hard enough to rattle the dishes on the shelf.
I could hear things blowing down the path: loose boards, someone's bucket, a crash that might've been a fence post.
reactions: I stayed in bed and let the wind have its tantrum. Nothing I could do about it anyway.

entries.intro: I fell asleep reading last night and woke up with the book on my face. Page stuck to my cheek.
details: My candle had burned down to a puddle of wax on the table. Lucky it didn't catch anything.
There was an imprint of the text on my cheek. Took half the morning to fade.
reactions: I don't even remember what I was reading. Must've been riveting.

entries.intro: There's a certain hour of the night when everything goes still. No wind, no animals, no house sounds. Just a held breath.
details: It happens around the middle of the dark: this pocket of absolute silence.
It only lasts a few minutes, but if you're awake for it, time feels like it's stopped.
reactions: I've felt it three or four times now. I can't decide if it's peaceful or unsettling.

statCheck.pass: The nocturnal animals are settling in and the dawn ones haven't started yet. There's a gap where nothing is moving. A natural pause.
statCheck.fail: Sometimes the night just goes quiet. I don't think about why.

entries.intro: I watched a {wildlife_type} hunting in the moonlight near the fence last night. Quick and silent.
details: It moved in short bursts: frozen still, then a sudden rush, then still again.
I couldn't see what it was after. But it was patient: waited ages between each move.
reactions: Watching something hunt at night makes you grateful to be inside.

entries.intro: I overslept this morning because I was up half the night with a cough. Every time I'd drift off, another fit.
details: The dry air at night makes it worse. Each cough triggers the next one in this endless loop.
I drank every drop of water in the house trying to stop it.
reactions: I'm exhausted and my ribs ache from coughing. Excellent start to the day.

entries.intro: The first light this morning came in grey and soft: no color at all, just the dark lifting slowly.
details: For a few minutes everything was the same shade of grey: the walls, the floor, my hands.
Then a line of gold hit the top of the opposite wall and the color crept downward like someone was pouring it.
reactions: Dawn earns itself every morning. That slow reveal is worth being awake for.

entries.intro: I forgot to bring the laundry in and it was out on the line all night. Stiff as boards by morning.
details: The frost had turned each shirt into a flat, rigid shape. My trousers stood up on their own.
Had to drape everything by the fire to thaw. The whole house smelled like cold damp cloth.
reactions: One less chore skipped at bedtime from now on.

entries.intro: I dreamed I was back somewhere I used to live. Everything was exactly right: the smells, the sounds, the light.
details: I was just sitting in a room I haven't been in for years. Nothing happened. I was just there.
Waking up was like being pulled out of a place that doesn't exist anymore.
reactions: I was in a strange mood all morning. The kind that doesn't have a name.

entries.intro: We heard the {mob_type} earlier than sunset yesterday. The days are getting shorter and they know it.
details: The noise started before the guard even lit the torches. Caught everyone off guard.
We had dinner with the windows shut and the door barred while it was still light enough to see.
reactions: Winter's going to be long if the dark keeps stretching.

statCheck.pass: They time their activity to the dark, not the hour. As the days shrink, their window grows. By midwinter they'll be active for twice as long.
statCheck.fail: They come out earlier now. I don't need to know why to not like it.

entries.intro: I opened my eyes in the dark and for a moment everything was that deep blue you only see before dawn.
details: Not black and not grey: a real color. The room, the window, the sky outside, all the same deep blue.
It lasted maybe ten minutes before the light shifted and normal morning started.
reactions: That blue hour is the only time the dark actually looks beautiful instead of just dark.

entries.intro: My stomach woke me up growling last night. Loud enough that I thought it was something in the room.
details: Lay there tense for a few seconds before I realized it was coming from me. I'd skipped supper.
Got up and ate a piece of bread in the dark. Crumbs in the bed were the price.
reactions: Hunger doesn't respect sleeping hours.

entries.intro: I was on watch near the {poi_type} last night when the clouds broke and the stars came out all at once.
details: One moment: black ceiling. The next: a thousand lights. It was like someone had lifted a lid off the sky.
The guard next to me actually stopped mid-sentence. We both just looked up.
reactions: Some nights the sky reminds you it's there.

entries.intro: The {wildlife_type} that's been coming around at dusk was back last night. Same spot, same time.
details: It's getting bolder. Used to stay at the tree line, now it comes right up to where the torchlight ends.
I've been leaving scraps out. Probably shouldn't, but I like knowing it's there.
reactions: It's become a part of my evening. I'd notice if it stopped coming.

statCheck.pass: Once they learn a spot is safe, they claim it as part of their territory. It'll keep coming back as long as nobody scares it off.
statCheck.fail: It comes and goes. I don't know what keeps it here.

entries.intro: I had the most vivid dream about flying last night. Arms out, wind in my face, the settlement shrinking below.
details: I could see everything: the rooftops, the paths, the fields, all tiny and clear.
The air was cold and I could feel it on my skin. I keep thinking about that feeling.
reactions: I woke up face down with my arms spread. Close enough.

entries.intro: There was a warm glow from the {poi_type} at an odd hour last night. Someone was working late.
details: I could see the light through the cracks and hear the faint sounds of activity.
It went on until well past midnight. Whoever it was, they had something that couldn't wait.
reactions: I'm glad I'm not the only one who can't leave work alone.

entries.intro: I woke up with a dead arm last night. Slept on it wrong and it went completely numb.
details: It was like a piece of wood attached to my shoulder. I had to pick it up with the other hand.
The pins and needles when the feeling came back were worse than the numbness.
reactions: My own arm frightened me. It's been a long week.

entries.intro: The smell of night-blooming flowers drifted in through my window. Sweet and heavy, like the dark had a perfume.
details: They only open after sunset: all day they're closed up tight, and then the cool air brings them out.
The scent was so strong I dreamed about a garden I've never been to.
reactions: Some of the best things about the night are the ones that come in quietly.

entries.intro: My neighbor's dog barked at my door last night. Just stood there barking until she came and got it.
details: I don't know what it wanted. It barked, I opened the door, it stared at me, and it kept barking.
The neighbor was mortified. She carried it home under her arm while it barked over her shoulder.
reactions: At least it wasn't something worse at the door.

entries.intro: I woke up and the fire had burned down to a single coal. Just one bright orange eye in the grate.
details: It pulsed when I breathed on it: brighter, dimmer, brighter. Like it was answering me.
I built the fire back up around it and had warmth again in a few minutes.
reactions: There's something satisfying about bringing a fire back from one coal.

entries.intro: A branch was scratching against my window all night. Every gust of wind: screee, screee.
details: I knew what it was. I could see the branch moving in the moonlight. It still set my teeth on edge.
I got up twice to push it away and it swung right back.
reactions: I'm going to cut that branch tomorrow. I've said that three nights in a row.

entries.intro: I was on watch and heard footsteps on the path {direction}, but no one was there when I shone the light.
details: Clear footsteps on gravel: crunch, crunch, crunch. Getting closer. Then nothing.
I stood there with the torch held high and stared into empty dark.
reactions: Sound carries strangely at night. Someone was probably a lot further away than they sounded.

statCheck.pass: Cold air bends sound toward the ground. You can hear footsteps from much further away than normal, and they sound close when they're not.
statCheck.fail: I heard what I heard. Nothing was there. I'm not going to explain it.

entries.intro: I made a pot of tea in the middle of the night because I couldn't sleep. Sat with it in the dark.
details: The warmth of the mug in my hands was enough. I didn't need to see anything.
The taste was different at that hour: stronger, more bitter. Everything's more itself in the quiet.
reactions: I've started looking forward to my sleepless nights. That's probably not a good sign.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} got into a pen {direction} last night. I could hear livestock panicking.
details: Bleating and crashing and that awful growling noise. The guards ran out and the noise scattered.
Lost one animal. The rest were huddled in the far corner, shaking.
reactions: I hate mornings when you can tell what happened before anyone says a word.

statCheck.pass: They go for the pens on moonless nights when the livestock can't see them coming. The animals panic and bunch up, which makes them easier targets.
statCheck.fail: They got in and did damage. That's all there is to say.

entries.intro: I lay awake last night thinking about everything I said during the day and cringing at half of it.
details: The night does that to me: pulls out every awkward moment and plays it back in detail.
One conversation in particular kept circling. I know no one else remembers it, but I can't let it go.
reactions: The dark is when my brain decides to audit my social skills.

entries.intro: I heard music last night: someone playing something stringed, very quiet, somewhere nearby.
details: Just a few notes at a time, with long pauses between. Like they were practicing or just thinking in melody.
It came and went with the wind. I couldn't tell whose house it was from.
reactions: I didn't want it to stop. It made the dark feel inhabited in a good way.

entries.intro: I tried sleeping without a pillow last night because mine's gone flat. My neck had a different opinion.
details: I folded a shirt up under my head instead. Not the same: too thin, too lumpy.
Woke up with a stiff neck and a strong conviction that I need a new pillow.
reactions: It's the small comforts you miss the most.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} was digging in the garden when I went out at dawn. Looked up at me with dirt on its nose.
details: It had made a tidy little hole in the soft patch by the fence. Very thorough work.
It seemed more annoyed at the interruption than scared of me.
reactions: I can't be angry at something that earnest. I filled the hole back in and moved on.

entries.intro: I woke up to the smell of smoke last night. Jumped out of bed before I realized it was just someone's chimney.
details: The wind had shifted and was blowing the smoke low across the settlement. Thick enough to smell indoors.
My heart was still pounding when I got back to bed. The adrenaline took a while to clear.
reactions: Smoke at night is a different kind of smell than smoke during the day. It means something else.

entries.intro: The rain last night sounded like someone throwing handfuls of gravel at the roof. Hard and fast.
details: It came in waves: loud, then louder, then a lull, then all at once again.
I pulled the blanket up to my chin and let the noise fill the room. There's a shelter in that sound.
reactions: Heavy rain at night is one thing that's better listened to than watched.

entries.intro: I thought I heard my name called last night. Just once, from somewhere outside.
details: It was faint and I was half asleep, so maybe I imagined it. But I sat up and listened for a full minute.
Nothing followed. The wind, the usual sounds, but no voice.
reactions: The mind hears what it wants to hear when you're between sleep and waking.

entries.intro: I tried going to bed earlier last night. Was lying there wide awake while it was still light outside.
details: I could hear everyone else still going about their evening: talking, closing doors, finishing work.
I felt like a child put to bed before the party. My body wasn't tired: my brain was, but my body hadn't gotten the message.
reactions: I gave up an hour later and got up again. Two steps forward, two steps back.

entries.intro: The guard's lantern throws long shadows when he walks past. I can see them sweep across my ceiling.
details: The shadows move smoothly: left to right, then gone. Then the room goes dark again until the next pass.
There's a rhythm to it. Light, dark, light, dark. The settlement breathing in and out.
reactions: I find it reassuring. It means someone's still walking the rounds.

entries.intro: Last night was warm enough to sleep with both windows open. A breeze came through the whole house.
details: You could feel the air moving: in one window, across the room, out the other. Like the house was breathing.
I could hear the night insects and the rustle of the trees and the occasional voice far away.
reactions: Open windows on a warm night: that's the peak of summer. I don't take those for granted.

entries.intro: I think I talk more honestly at night. When it's dark and quiet, the walls come down.
details: Had a conversation with my partner last night that we'd been avoiding for weeks. Just came out in the dark.
We didn't solve anything, but we said things that needed saying. That's enough.
reactions: The dark strips away pretense. You can't see each other's faces, so you just say what you mean.

entries.intro: I was watching the {poi_type} from the path last night and noticed the shadows it throws in moonlight. Never looked at it that way before.
details: During the day it's just a building. At night the shape changes: taller, more angular, less familiar.
The moon was behind it and the shadow stretched all the way to the path where I was standing.
reactions: Every building has a night version you only see if you're out at the wrong hour.

entries.intro: I stepped outside at midnight and the air was so crisp it felt like breathing cold water.
details: Every breath stung my nostrils and made my lungs tighten. But it woke me up completely.
The sky was sharp and hard above me: every star a needle point.
reactions: There's a kind of cold at night that makes you feel alive instead of just uncomfortable.

entries.intro: I left a bowl of stew by the door last night for the stray cat. It was licked clean by morning.
details: Could've been the cat. Could've been anything. But the bowl was spotless.
I've been leaving food out for a week now. Whatever's eating it is very punctual.
reactions: I've started looking forward to checking the empty bowl each morning. It's a small connection.

statCheck.pass: There are scratch marks on the bowl rim that are too small for anything but a cat. So at least something feline is getting fed.
statCheck.fail: The bowl is empty and I'm choosing to believe it's the cat.

entries.intro: The moon went behind a cloud last night while I was outside and the darkness was instant. Like someone blew out the sky.
details: One moment I could see everything: the path, the fence, the buildings. Next moment, nothing.
I stood still and waited. Didn't trust my feet on a path I couldn't see.
reactions: You don't realize how much you're relying on moonlight until it's gone.

entries.intro: I caught a {wildlife_type} drinking from the rain barrel at first light. It had its head right down inside.
details: When it pulled its head out, water was dripping off its chin. It shook itself and trotted off.
Bold as anything. Didn't care that I was standing three paces away.
reactions: I admire the confidence. That's someone who knows what they want.

entries.intro: I lay in bed last night and just listened to my house. Every building has its own voice.
details: The timbers settling, the shutters shifting, the hearth stone ticking as it cools. Each one familiar.
When you're still enough to hear them all, it sounds like the house is alive.
reactions: I've lived here long enough that the sounds are comforting. A new house would keep me up for months.

entries.intro: A group of {mob_type} crossed the path {direction} just after sundown. The guard counted them.
details: They weren't hurrying, just moving through. Like the path was theirs and we were the guests.
The guard said there were more than last time.
reactions: They used to only come through in full dark. The fact that they're comfortable at dusk bothers me.

statCheck.pass: Numbers swell in autumn when they're looking to settle before winter. Bigger groups don't mean more aggression: it means they're consolidating.
statCheck.fail: More of them, earlier in the evening. That's the trend, and I don't like it.

entries.intro: I dreamed about water last night. Calm water, still and dark, stretching to every horizon.
details: I was standing at the edge of it, and the surface reflected the stars so perfectly it looked like I could step into the sky.
I woke up before I decided whether to step in.
reactions: Some dreams ask you a question and don't wait for the answer.

entries.intro: The guard on duty last night brought a flask of something hot. I could smell it from my window.
details: Spiced something: cinnamon or clove, I couldn't tell. But the smell carried.
He was standing at the gate with both hands wrapped around the flask, breathing in the steam.
reactions: Smart man. Cold hands and cold feet are the enemy of a good watch.

entries.intro: I couldn't stop yawning last night but I also couldn't fall asleep. My body wanted rest but my brain wouldn't allow it.
details: That awful state where your eyes burn and your limbs are heavy but your thoughts won't stop.
I must've turned over fifty times. Each position felt wrong within a minute.
reactions: Tired and sleepless is its own kind of misery.
