entries.intro: Walked past a puddle last night and it was reflecting the moon so perfectly it looked like a hole in the ground full of sky.
details: I stopped and stared at it longer than a grown person should stare at a puddle.
A leaf fell in and shattered the image. Then it re-formed, piece by piece.
reactions: The night hides things, but it shows you other things you'd never see in daylight.

entries.intro: I woke up to the sound of hail on the roof: sudden and violent, like someone dumping gravel from the sky.
details: It lasted maybe five minutes and then stopped completely. The silence after was almost louder.
This morning the ground was littered with little ice pellets. Some the size of berries.
reactions: Hail in the night is the sky's way of reminding you who's in charge.

entries.intro: I found a {wildlife_type} print in the fresh snow outside my door this morning. One clear track, toes and all.
details: The snow fell sometime after midnight and must've stopped just before dawn. This track was the only mark in it.
One animal, walking right past my door. The prints led from the fence and disappeared around the corner.
reactions: Something was that close while I slept. A quiet visitor I never knew about.

entries.intro: I heard two guards talking in low voices near my window last night. Just fragments of conversation.
details: Something about the schedule, something about being cold. Normal things, but whispered.
Whispering always sounds more serious than it is. They were probably just trying not to wake people.
reactions: Hushed voices at night make everything sound like a secret.

entries.intro: My blanket smelled like woodsmoke last night. Must've been near the fire too long while I was drying it.
details: It's not an unpleasant smell: warm and familiar. But it was strong enough to taste.
I fell asleep wrapped in it and dreamed about campfires.
reactions: Smoke in a blanket is like sleeping next to an old memory.

entries.intro: I noticed the {mob_type} have stopped using the path {direction}. They've moved to a new route.
details: The old tracks are fading and the new ones cut through a different stretch. You can see where the grass is flattened.
The guards shifted a torch to cover the new line, but it means the old spot is darker now.
reactions: Just when you think you know their habits, they change.

statCheck.pass: They rotate paths to avoid predictability. If they always take the same way, it's easier to set traps. Changing routes is survival instinct.
statCheck.fail: They go where they go. I can't predict it.

entries.intro: I watched my breath fog in the moonlight last night. Little clouds that hung in the air and dissolved.
details: Each breath was visible for a second or two before the cold ate it. Like watching thoughts disappear.
The colder it got, the thicker the cloud.
reactions: Something about seeing your own breath makes you aware that you're alive and the night is very large.

entries.intro: The cat woke me up by sitting on my chest and staring at my face in the dark. I opened my eyes and there it was.
details: Two green reflective circles about four inches from my nose. My heart nearly gave out.
It wanted to be fed. At three in the morning. With the urgency of a dying creature.
reactions: I fed the cat. What else can you do?

entries.intro: I slept better on a bedroll outdoors once than I ever do in my own bed. I think about that a lot.
details: Hard ground, open sky, cold air. And I slept like the dead until sunrise.
Something about being out in it, no walls between you and the night, just lets the tension go.
reactions: Maybe beds aren't the answer. Maybe the answer is fewer walls.

statCheck.pass: I think it's the air. A closed room gets stale and your body knows it. Outside, every breath is fresh and the cold keeps you just uncomfortable enough to sleep deep.
statCheck.fail: I slept well outside once. I don't know why. I just know it happened.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} creaks louder at night. You can hear it from the path when everything else is still.
details: It's just the building settling: wood shrinking in the cold, joints shifting.
But at night every sound from an empty building feels intentional.
reactions: I hurry past it after dark. Not because I'm scared. I just prefer to hurry.

entries.intro: I woke up convinced it was morning, got dressed, and walked outside to full darkness. Not even close to dawn.
details: I stood on the path in my boots wondering what time it actually was. The stars said midnight.
Went back inside, got undressed, and went back to bed. Slept through the actual morning.
reactions: My sense of time is completely broken. My body has its own clock and it's wrong.

entries.intro: I could hear a storm building in the distance last night. Thunder rolling closer, one rumble at a time.
details: The first ones were so far off they sounded like furniture being moved in another house.
By midnight it was overhead: bright flashes and cracks that shook the shutters.
reactions: There's nothing to do in a storm but lie there and let it happen.

entries.intro: I caught a {wildlife_type} silhouetted against the dawn sky this morning, perfectly still on a fence post.
details: It was just a dark shape with the pink sky behind it. Like a carving placed there on purpose.
It stayed for a long while, then dropped off the far side and was gone.
reactions: Some mornings start with a small picture worth remembering.

entries.intro: I lay in bed making lists in my head last night. Things to do, things to fix, things I've been putting off.
details: The list grew longer the more I thought about it. Every finished item spawned two new ones.
By the time I gave up, I was more stressed than when I started.
reactions: The night is not the time for planning. Nothing feels achievable in the dark.

entries.intro: I woke up and the pillow was soaking wet. Just one side: the side my face was on.
details: Drool, apparently. I'm a grown adult and I drool in my sleep like a baby.
Flipped the pillow and went back to sleep. My dignity can wait until morning.
reactions: There are things about yourself you only learn at night. Most of them are humbling.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} made no sound at all last night, but we found fresh claw marks on the fence post this morning.
details: Deep gouges in the wood: deliberate, not accidental. Like they were sharpening on it or testing it.
Nobody heard a thing. They were quiet enough to get that close without alerting the dogs.
reactions: The quiet ones concern me more than the loud ones.

statCheck.pass: Claw marks are territorial. They're saying 'we were here' to other groups. It's a boundary marker, not a threat to us specifically.
statCheck.fail: Claw marks on the fence. I don't know what it means and I don't want to guess.

entries.intro: I sat by the open window last night and just felt the air change as the temperature dropped.
details: You can feel the moment the warmth leaves. The air goes from soft to sharp in the space of an hour.
My face was cold but I kept the window open. The sky was too clear to close it.
reactions: Cold air and clear skies: the night's way of offering a trade.

entries.intro: I fell asleep so fast last night I don't remember lying down. Just woke up eight hours later.
details: No dreams, no waking up, no awareness of time passing. Like being switched off and on again.
I was still in the same position. Hadn't moved at all.
reactions: That kind of sleep is a gift. I wish I knew how to make it happen on purpose.

entries.intro: A fox was barking outside the settlement last night. That sharp, ragged sound they make.
details: If you've never heard it, it sounds like someone screaming through a mouthful of gravel.
It went on for ten minutes and then silence. Just making its presence known.
reactions: The first time I heard a fox at night I thought someone was in trouble. You get used to it.

entries.intro: My candle made a popping sound last night and I flinched so hard I almost fell out of bed.
details: Just a bubble in the wax hitting the flame. A tiny sound. But in the quiet it was like a crack of thunder.
I sat there with my hand on my chest feeling my heartbeat slow down.
reactions: The night amplifies everything. Including my reactions.

entries.intro: I noticed a planet last night: one bright steady light that doesn't twinkle like the stars around it.
details: It sat there burning clean and orange while everything else flickered. Felt like it was watching back.
I pointed it out to the guard and he shrugged. Not everyone finds the sky interesting.
reactions: I like knowing it's up there. Something steady in all that shimmer.

statCheck.pass: Stars twinkle because their light bends through the air. Planets are closer, so their light is steadier. That's how you tell them apart.
statCheck.fail: One bright one that doesn't twinkle. I don't know why it's different.

entries.intro: I pulled a muscle in my leg last night: woke up to a cramp that bent me double.
details: That white-hot pain in the calf that locks the whole muscle. I couldn't straighten my leg for a full minute.
By the time it released I was sweating and wide awake. Took an hour to trust the leg enough to relax.
reactions: Night cramps are my body's idea of a practical joke.

statCheck.pass: Means I'm not drinking enough water during the day. The muscles dry out and seize up in the cold. More water, fewer cramps.
statCheck.fail: It cramped, it hurt, it stopped. Not much more to say.

entries.intro: There was a circle around the moon last night. A big, pale ring, like it was wearing a halo.
details: The whole ring was visible: a perfect circle of faint light with the moon sitting in the center.
It means weather coming, my grandmother used to say.
reactions: Sure enough, it was raining by this afternoon. She was rarely wrong about these things.

statCheck.pass: Ice crystals in the high clouds bend the moonlight into a ring. It means there's moisture up there moving in. Rain within a day or two.
statCheck.fail: Ring around the moon means rain coming. That's all I know.

entries.intro: I watched two bats chasing each other around the torch by the gate last night. Dipping and weaving in circles.
details: They were snatching moths out of the air: you could see them fold their wings and snap sideways at the last second.
The moths kept coming to the light and the bats kept picking them off. Efficient.
reactions: Nature running its system right outside the gate. I just happened to be watching.

entries.intro: I sleepwalked last night. Woke up standing in the middle of the room with no idea how I got there.
details: My feet were cold from the stone floor. I was holding a shoe for some reason.
My partner said I got up, walked to the corner, picked up the shoe, and stood there. Then I woke up.
reactions: My sleeping self has plans that my waking self knows nothing about.

entries.intro: The first snow of the season came last night while I slept. I opened the door to a white world this morning.
details: Everything was muffled: the sounds, the edges, even the light. Like the settlement had been wrapped in cloth.
No footprints yet. Just smooth, unbroken white in every direction.
reactions: The first snow always feels like a reset. Everything clean, everything quiet.

entries.intro: I left the shutters open and the moonlight woke me. Brighter than a candle, right across my face.
details: For a moment I thought it was dawn. But the light was silver, not gold, and the house was still.
I lay in it for a while. There's something different about moonlight on skin: cooler, somehow.
reactions: I should've closed the shutters. Instead I lay there and watched the light move. Time well wasted.

entries.intro: I had a nosebleed in the middle of the night. Woke up to a warm trickle and a dark stain on the pillow.
details: Had to sit up and pinch it for ten minutes in the pitch dark. Blood dripping into my cupped hand.
The pillow's probably ruined. I'll flip it and deal with it later.
reactions: The body picks the worst hours for its surprises.

statCheck.pass: The air's been dry. When the inside of your nose dries out, the blood vessels crack. Keeping a damp cloth nearby helps in dry weather.
statCheck.fail: Nosebleed. In the dark. At midnight. Nothing else to say.

entries.intro: There were so many fireflies last night it looked like the fields were full of floating sparks.
details: They blinked in waves: one section would light up and then the next, like the light was rolling across the grass.
Some of them drifted up high enough to mix with the stars.
reactions: For a few minutes the boundary between the ground and the sky disappeared.

entries.intro: I heard an argument through the wall last night. My neighbors, going back and forth in sharp whispers.
details: Couldn't make out the words: just the tone. Sharp, clipped, angry. Then one of them raised their voice for a second and the other shushed them.
It went quiet after that. Too quiet: the kind of silence that means someone's sleeping on the floor.
reactions: I pretended I didn't hear anything this morning. Some things you're better off not knowing.

entries.intro: I woke up and heard rain, but when I looked outside it was dry. The sound was coming from the leaves in the wind.
details: A dry rustling that sounds exactly like light rain on a roof. My ears were completely fooled.
I stood at the window for a full minute waiting for drops that never came.
reactions: The night is full of sounds pretending to be other sounds.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} was making a terrible fuss outside the fence last night, screeching and thrashing.
details: I think it had gotten tangled in something: kept hearing it flap and scratch and cry out.
By the time I got a lantern out there it was gone. Whatever held it must have let go.
reactions: I hope it's all right. That was a desperate sound.

entries.intro: I found a cobweb across my doorway this morning. A spider built the whole thing overnight: corner to corner.
details: The dew had caught on every thread. The whole web was glittering like someone had hung jewelry in my doorway.
I hated to break it, but I needed to get through.
reactions: Something worked all night to build that, and I destroyed it in a second. I felt worse about it than I expected.

entries.intro: I've been having the same nightmare for three nights. Nothing specific: just a feeling of being followed.
details: I can't see anything in the dream. Just darkness and the sense that something is matching my pace behind me.
I walk faster, it walks faster. I stop, it stops. I never turn around.
reactions: I wake up tired every morning. My sleep is working against me.

statCheck.pass: It's anxiety wearing a costume. Whatever I'm worried about during the day puts on a cloak and follows me through the night.
statCheck.fail: Bad dreams. Nothing I can do about them.

entries.intro: I stepped outside at first light and the whole world was covered in spider silk. Every blade of grass, every fence rail.
details: The dew made it visible: millions of tiny threads catching the light. The ground looked like it was dressed in gauze.
It was beautiful in a way that also made my skin crawl.
reactions: Nature does things at night that it never lets you see it doing.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} were close enough last night that the guard lit the signal fire. The orange glow woke everyone.
details: People were at their windows, some with weapons. The fire meant 'be ready,' not 'run,' but the mood was tense.
They moved on after an hour. The fire burned down and we all went back to bed, though I doubt anyone slept.
reactions: The signal fire means it's serious enough to worry about and not serious enough to panic. That narrow space is exhausting.

statCheck.pass: The fire makes the perimeter look occupied and awake. They avoid settlements that look alert. It's as much a deterrent as a warning.
statCheck.fail: The fire went up, they left. That's good enough for me.

entries.intro: I taught myself to tell time by where the moon sits in the sky. Not precise, but close enough.
details: Low in the east: early. Overhead: middle of the night. Low in the west: almost dawn.
It only works on clear nights, which are the nights I tend to be awake anyway.
reactions: Insomnia has given me exactly one useful skill.

statCheck.pass: It tracks roughly an hour for each hand-width across the sky. Hold your fist at arm's length and count the widths from the horizon. That's your clock.
statCheck.fail: The moon moves, and I watch it. Keeps my mind off other things.

entries.intro: I woke up to the sound of the first bird: one note, repeated three times, and then quiet. The night was ending.
details: It was still dark, but that single bird knew. A few minutes later, another one started. Then another.
By the time the sky had any color at all, the whole chorus was rolling.
reactions: The night doesn't end all at once. It ends one bird at a time.

entries.intro: I woke up in the middle of the night and my fire had gone out. The room was so dark I couldn't find the door.
details: I shuffled around with my hands out like a blind person, bumping into furniture I've owned for years.
Took me five minutes to find the tinderbox. My own house, and I was lost in it.
reactions: Darkness turns familiar places into mazes.

statCheck.pass: I keep a tinderbox on the bedside table now, within arm's reach. One dead fire taught me that lesson.
statCheck.fail: The fire went out and I was stuck in the dark. Not much to it.

entries.intro: There's a sound the wind makes at night that it never makes during the day. A low moan through the gaps in the walls.
details: During the day the same wind just feels like weather. At night it sounds like the house is complaining.
I stuffed a rag in the worst gap and it stopped. Now I miss it.
reactions: You get used to the sounds your house makes. Take one away and the silence is louder.

statCheck.pass: The gap is where the old beam split last winter. I could fix it properly, but honestly I've grown fond of the sound.
statCheck.fail: The wind finds its way in no matter what you do.

entries.intro: I heard {mob_type} howling {direction} just before midnight. That sound travels further than you'd think.
details: It echoed off the hills and came back warped: sounded like twice as many as there probably were.
The dogs in the settlement went rigid. Ears up, every muscle tense.
reactions: When the dogs go quiet, I go inside.

statCheck.pass: They howl to mark territory, not to hunt. If they're howling, they're stationary: it's the silent nights you worry about.
statCheck.fail: Howling is howling. I don't pretend to know what it means.

entries.intro: I sat on the step last night and watched a moth circle my lantern for a solid ten minutes.
details: Round and round in the same tight loop, never landing, never leaving. Just kept orbiting.
I moved the lantern to the other side and the moth followed without hesitation.
reactions: I don't understand moths. But I admire the commitment.

entries.intro: Slept with the window cracked last night and a beetle flew in. Spent twenty minutes buzzing around the ceiling.
details: That angry, droning buzz that stops and starts: lands on the wall, takes off again, crashes into the rafters.
I finally caught it in a cup and put it back outside. It immediately flew at the window again.
reactions: Some creatures are drawn to the light and there's no reasoning with them.

statCheck.pass: Beetles navigate by starlight: the window glow confuses their sense of direction. Cover the light and they'll find their way out on their own.
statCheck.fail: Bugs come in, bugs go out. Not much to do about it.

entries.intro: The sky was so clear last night I could see the band of stars that runs across the whole ceiling of the world.
details: Thick and milky, like someone spilled a pale liquid across the dark. Thousands of stars blurred into a river.
I lay on my back in the grass until the cold drove me inside.
reactions: Some nights the sky reminds you how small you are, and it feels like a relief.

entries.intro: I keep a candle burning in the window when I can't sleep. Makes me feel less alone.
details: From outside it probably looks like someone's up late working. Really I'm just sitting there, watching the flame.
The wax drips and pools and I watch it harden. That's the whole evening.
reactions: A candle in a window says someone is awake. Sometimes that's enough.

entries.intro: I woke to the smell of rain. Not the sound: the smell, coming through the window before a single drop fell.
details: That heavy, mineral scent the ground gives off when it knows rain is coming. Warm and earthy.
I lay there breathing it in. By the time the rain actually started, I was already calm.
reactions: The smell of rain before it arrives is one of the best things about an open window.

entries.intro: My neighbor's rooster crowed at three in the morning: three in the morning, and the sun wasn't even close.
details: It went off like an alarm with no cause. One long, confident crow into pure darkness.
Then nothing: back to silence, like it just wanted everyone to know it was there.
reactions: That rooster has no respect for anyone's sleep, including its own.

entries.intro: I noticed a {wildlife_type} sitting perfectly still near the lantern post last night. Just watching the flame.
details: Didn't move, didn't flinch. Just sat there with the warm glow on its face, looking contemplative.
We were both just sitting in the dark staring at the same light. Had more in common than I expected.
reactions: Animals at night always look like they know something you don't.

statCheck.pass: Animals sit near lanterns because the warmth attracts insects and the insects attract them. It's not curiosity: it's dinner.
statCheck.fail: Just an animal doing animal things. Nothing more to it.

entries.intro: The guard changed shifts right under my window last night. That brief exchange of murmurs is the loneliest sound.
details: One voice tired, one voice fresh. A few words about the night so far, and then footsteps going opposite ways.
The tired one always walks faster. Heading home, heading to bed.
reactions: I'm glad someone's out there, even if the shift change wakes me every time.

statCheck.pass: The night guards get one break in an eight-hour shift. That handoff under my window is the only human contact they get for hours.
statCheck.fail: Guards change over. It wakes me, but that's the routine.

entries.intro: I heard {mob_type} scuffling {direction} of the fence line last night. Not loud, but persistent.
details: Crunching through underbrush, snapping sticks. Moving along the edge without coming closer.
They passed within earshot for about ten minutes and then faded. Just passing through.
reactions: I'd rather hear them than not. At least I know where they are.

statCheck.pass: When they move noisily along the edge, they're foraging, not scouting. Scouts are quiet, but foragers don't care if you hear them.
statCheck.fail: They were close and they moved on. That's the best outcome.

entries.intro: I dreamed I was falling and jerked awake so hard I kicked the wall. Toe is still throbbing.
details: That hypnic jolt: your whole body twitches and you're suddenly wide awake with your heart racing.
The dream was already gone by the time I opened my eyes. Just the falling sensation and then pain.
reactions: My body can't even fall asleep without turning it into an event.

entries.intro: I woke up to absolute silence last night: no wind, no insects, no animals, just nothing.
details: It felt heavier than noise. Like the quiet had a weight pressing on my ears.
I almost got up just to make a sound: open a door, drop something, anything.
reactions: Total silence is more unsettling than any noise I've heard.

statCheck.pass: Total silence like that usually means a predator passed through. Everything else holds its breath and waits.
statCheck.fail: It was quiet: unusually quiet, and I don't know why.

entries.intro: The rain on the roof last night was so steady and even it put me to sleep in minutes.
details: A perfect, constant drumming: not too loud, not too soft. Like the sky was singing a lullaby.
Woke up once, heard it was still going, and fell right back under.
reactions: Best sleep I've had in weeks, and all it took was a rainstorm.

entries.intro: I watched a shooting star last night. Quick as a blink: one bright line and gone.
details: If I'd been looking anywhere else I would've missed it. Just happened to be staring at the right patch of sky.
I waited for another one but that was the only show. One performer, one note.
reactions: I don't make wishes on them. But I always feel lucky to see one.

entries.intro: My pillow was too warm last night. Kept flipping it to find the cool side, and the cool side kept warming up.
details: An endless rotation: flip, enjoy three minutes of coolness, feel the warmth creep back, flip again.
By morning I was sleeping on a balled-up shirt because the pillow had become the enemy.
reactions: Summer nights and warm pillows: a small misery that feels enormous at two in the morning.

statCheck.pass: I soaked a cloth in cold water and laid it on the pillow. Stays cool for about an hour: long enough to fall asleep.
statCheck.fail: Hot pillows in summer. No solution that I've found.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} was scratching at the base of the wall last night. I could hear its claws on the stone.
details: Persistent little scraping sound, going back and forth along the foundation. Looking for something.
I tapped the wall from inside and it stopped. Then started again a minute later.
reactions: Stubborn creature. I respect it, but I wish it would be stubborn somewhere else.

entries.intro: I walked outside at midnight and the ground fog was ankle-deep. Like wading through a slow river of smoke.
details: My feet disappeared completely. I could feel the path under my boots but I couldn't see it.
The fog was cold and damp against my skin. Everything above my knees was clear and crisp.
reactions: Ground fog makes the world feel unfinished: like the bottom half hasn't loaded yet.

statCheck.pass: Ground fog forms when the soil is warmer than the air above it: moisture rises and condenses at ankle height. It means tomorrow will be warm.
statCheck.fail: Fog on the ground. I walked through it and came home damp.

entries.intro: I left a cup of water by the bed and drank it in the night without waking up. Found the empty cup this morning.
details: I have no memory of reaching for it. My sleeping self was thirsty and handled it.
At least sleeping-me has good survival instincts, even if waking-me gets the empty cup.
reactions: The body knows what it needs and will get it with or without your participation.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} were louder than usual {direction} last night. Could hear them over the wind.
details: Not just one or two: a whole group, making noise for hours.
The guards doubled the watch on that side. Everyone was on edge until they quieted down.
reactions: Loud nights make for tired mornings. Nobody sleeps well when they're listening.

statCheck.pass: The noise peaks around the new moon. Less light makes them bolder, so they're louder because they feel safer in the dark.
statCheck.fail: They were loud, then they stopped. I don't try to read meaning into it.

entries.intro: I found a toad on my doorstep this morning. Just sitting there in the dew like it owned the place.
details: Squat and warty and completely unbothered by my presence. It blinked once and stayed put.
I had to step over it to get out. It was still there when I came back.
reactions: Something about a toad's confidence is deeply admirable.

entries.intro: I woke up sweating through the sheets last night. Not from a nightmare: just heat, thick and unmoving.
details: The air had gone dead. No breeze, no current, just a warm weight on everything.
I spent an hour lying on top of the blankets, waiting for any movement in the air.
reactions: Hot, still nights feel like the house is holding its breath.

statCheck.pass: The still nights are the hottest because there's no air moving to carry the heat away. If there's even a slight breeze, you sleep better.
statCheck.fail: Hot and still. Nothing to do but wait it out.

entries.intro: The dew was so heavy this morning that everything outside was dripping. Like it had rained, but from below.
details: The grass was soaked. My boots were wet through before I reached the gate.
Every surface was beaded with water: the fence, the shutters, even the rope on the well.
reactions: Heavy dew means the night was cold and the ground was warm. A quiet battle fought while we slept.

entries.intro: I heard singing last night. Someone walking the path, singing quietly to themselves in the dark.
details: Couldn't place the voice or the tune. It drifted past my window and faded out toward the edge of the settlement.
It was a slow, simple melody: not sad, not happy, just someone keeping themselves company.
reactions: A voice in the dark is either comforting or unsettling. Last night it was comforting.

entries.intro: My dog growled at the wall last night. Not barking: a low, sustained growl at nothing I could see or hear.
details: Staring at one spot, hackles up, barely breathing. Whatever he sensed was gone by the time I looked.
He settled down eventually, but he slept with one eye open the rest of the night.
reactions: Dogs hear what we can't. I've learned to take his warnings seriously even when I can't verify them.

statCheck.pass: Dogs hear sounds we can't: higher pitches, fainter volumes. Whatever he noticed was real, just beyond my range.
statCheck.fail: The dog growled at nothing. Maybe nothing isn't always nothing.

entries.intro: I woke before dawn and the light was that deep blue that means morning is close but not here yet.
details: Everything was visible but colorless: shapes without detail, outlines without edges.
It lasted maybe twenty minutes before the first gold crept in and washed it away.
reactions: That blue hour is the quietest time there is. The night is done but the day hasn't started.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} left droppings on my windowsill overnight. I discovered them with my elbow this morning.
details: Reached out to open the shutters, felt something soft, and the day started poorly.
Had to scrub the sill with a rag and hot water. Still not sure it's clean.
reactions: The wildlife around here treats my home like a public latrine.

entries.intro: I counted the seconds between lightning and thunder last night, the way my mother taught me.
details: Started at twelve seconds and kept shrinking. By the end it was nearly instant: flash and crack together.
Then the gap started widening again as the storm moved off. Twelve seconds, fifteen, twenty.
reactions: Counting the storm is something to do besides worry. My mother knew that.

statCheck.pass: Each second between flash and thunder is roughly a thousand paces of distance. Twelve seconds means far off, and instant means it's right overhead.
statCheck.fail: You count the seconds and the number tells you something. I just don't remember what.

entries.intro: I dreamed about a place I've never been. A wide open field under a sky with two moons.
details: The grass was silver and the air tasted like metal. I was walking but I couldn't feel my feet.
I woke up with the image so clear I could've drawn it.
reactions: Some dreams give you a place to visit when you close your eyes. I wouldn't mind going back.

statCheck.pass: I've had that dream three times now: same field, same sky, same feeling of belonging. I almost look forward to it.
statCheck.fail: A strange dream about a strange place. Dreams are dreams.

entries.intro: The lantern outside my door ran out of oil at some point in the night. I woke to a darker street than usual.
details: That one lantern covers more ground than you'd think. Without it, the whole stretch was pitch black.
Had to refill it by feel this morning. Spilled oil on my hands.
reactions: You don't notice a light until it's gone.

entries.intro: I lay awake last night thinking about something embarrassing I said years ago. Just out of nowhere.
details: The kind of memory that makes you wince in the dark. Nobody else remembers it, but my brain won't let it go.
I pulled the blanket over my head as if that would help.
reactions: The night is when your own mind decides to ambush you with things best forgotten.

statCheck.pass: The brain replays old embarrassments at night because it's trying to learn from them. Doesn't help to know that, but at least there's a reason.
statCheck.fail: Bad memories at night. Everyone has them.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} left tracks along the fence line {direction} this morning. Fresh ones, in the mud.
details: A whole line of prints, walking single file. They follow the fence like it's a road.
The guard pointed them out during the morning check. He said the prints were hours old at most.
reactions: They walk closer than I'd like, but at least they keep walking.

statCheck.pass: Single file means a scouting party, not a raiding group. They check the perimeter and report back: no interest in getting in.
statCheck.fail: Tracks along the fence. They were here, and now they're not.

entries.intro: I found myself humming in bed last night without realizing it. Some tune I don't even know the name of.
details: It just started, like my throat decided to make noise on its own. A low, aimless melody.
When I noticed and stopped, the silence felt worse. So I kept humming.
reactions: The body fills silence when the mind won't.

entries.intro: I watched the clouds slide across the moon last night. Slow and deliberate, like curtains being drawn.
details: The light dimmed and brightened as each cloud passed: a slow pulse across the whole landscape.
Some clouds were thin enough that the moon burned through them, hazy and ringed.
reactions: Cloud-watching at night is an underrated occupation.

entries.intro: I slept with socks on last night and still had cold feet. The cold came up through the floor.
details: Stone floors hold the cold like a grudge. Two pairs of socks and I could still feel it seeping in.
I ended up pulling my feet under me and sleeping curled up like a cat.
reactions: Cold feet are the reason half my nights end with me in a ball.

statCheck.pass: Warm stones under the bed hold heat for hours. I heat them by the fire before I sleep and tuck them under the blankets.
statCheck.fail: Cold feet, cold floor. I curl up and endure.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} was calling outside my window for most of the night. Persistent, rhythmic, unwavering.
details: Every few seconds, the same sound, over and over: like a metronome made of wildlife.
I put the pillow over my head and it didn't help. That sound cuts through anything.
reactions: I respect its dedication. But I'm running on no sleep because of it.

statCheck.pass: When a {wildlife_type} calls that steadily, it's establishing territory. It'll keep going until something answers or dawn comes, whichever is first.
statCheck.fail: It called all night. I don't know why and at this point I don't care.

entries.intro: The sunset last night was the color of a forge. Red-orange and glowing, like the sky was full of hot metal.
details: It lasted a long time: the color deepened instead of fading, getting redder and richer.
By the time it finally went dark, the afterimage was still burned into my eyes.
reactions: A sunset that good almost makes up for the night that follows.

entries.intro: I noticed last night that I've been grinding my teeth in my sleep. Woke up with a sore jaw.
details: My back teeth feel like they've been pressing against each other all night. Dull ache all through the jaw.
I didn't know I was doing it until my partner mentioned the noise.
reactions: My body carries stress in my sleep that I don't feel when I'm awake.

statCheck.pass: I clench my jaw when I'm worried about something I won't admit to during the day. The grinding is just the night version of the same tension.
statCheck.fail: Sore jaw, no explanation. The body does what it wants.

entries.intro: I watched the guard's torch make shadows jump on the wall of my room last night. Moving shapes across the ceiling.
details: Every time he walked past, the shadows swung: long and thin, then short and fat, then gone.
It was like watching a puppet show with no puppets. Just shapes and movement.
reactions: Torchlight and darkness play games with each other all night. I just happened to be watching.

entries.intro: I woke up and couldn't remember where I was for a solid five seconds. My own bedroom, and it was foreign.
details: That disorientation where everything is wrong: which way the door is, which side the window is on.
Then it all snapped back, familiar again. But for those few seconds I was nowhere.
reactions: Waking up confused is my least favorite way to start a day.

statCheck.pass: It happens more when I'm overtired: the brain wakes up before it reconnects with where the body is. Five seconds of genuine confusion, then it clicks.
statCheck.fail: I forgot where I was for a moment. It passed.

entries.intro: I left bread on the table last night and something got into the house and ate half of it.
details: Clean bite marks, crumbs scattered. Whatever it was, it helped itself and left.
The door was closed. Must've come in through the window I left cracked.
reactions: My own fault for leaving food out. But I'd like to know what's dining in my kitchen.

statCheck.pass: The bite marks were too clean for a rat. Something with a wider jaw: likely a raccoon or a possum coming in through the gap.
statCheck.fail: Something ate my bread and I don't know what.

entries.intro: There was a thin crescent moon last night. Barely there: just a sliver of light like a closing eye.
details: It hung low in the west and set early, leaving the sky completely to the stars.
The thinner the moon, the brighter the stars seem. Like they've been waiting for their turn.
reactions: I prefer the thin moons. The sky opens up when the moon steps back.

entries.intro: I could hear frogs from the wet ground last night. Dozens of them, all going at once.
details: A thick wall of croaking that rises and falls in waves. One starts, they all join, then they all stop.
The silence between choruses is sudden and total: like someone gave a signal.
reactions: Frogs are terrible musicians and excellent company.

statCheck.pass: When frogs go silent all at once, something spooked them: a snake, a large bird, anything that eats frogs. The silence is the warning.
statCheck.fail: Frogs croak and then they stop. That's the whole show.

entries.intro: I dreamed I was late for something but couldn't figure out what. Just running through corridors with a pounding heart.
details: Every door led to another corridor. I kept thinking the right room was just around the next corner.
Woke up exhausted, with my legs tangled in the sheets like I'd actually been running.
reactions: The worst dreams are the ones where you're failing at something you can't even name.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} set something on fire {direction} last night. We could see the glow on the horizon.
details: An orange flicker against the treeline that burned for maybe an hour before it died down.
The guard watched it the whole time. Said it was too far to be a concern, but he kept watching.
reactions: Firelight on the horizon at night is never a comfortable sight.

statCheck.pass: They burn brush to flush out prey: it's a hunting technique, not aggression toward us. They don't care about the settlement when they're feeding.
statCheck.fail: Something burned, and they were behind it. I don't know the details.

entries.intro: I woke up with one arm completely numb. Slept on it wrong and it was dead from the shoulder down.
details: That horrible pins-and-needles feeling when the blood comes back. Like ants crawling inside the skin.
I had to pick up my own arm with my other hand and move it. Couldn't feel it at all.
reactions: For one groggy second I thought something was seriously wrong. Then: just my arm being dramatic.

statCheck.pass: The numbness means the nerve was compressed. Sleeping with your arm above your head does it: cuts off the blood and deadens the signal.
statCheck.fail: Dead arm, but it came back. Nothing to worry about.

entries.intro: The stars reflected in the puddles after last night's rain. Every puddle was its own little sky.
details: I walked the path and each step passed a different constellation trapped in muddy water.
The reflections shivered when the breeze touched the surface, then went still again.
reactions: The sky below your feet is more interesting than the one above sometimes.

entries.intro: I tried counting sheep last night. Got to two hundred and was more awake than when I started.
details: The problem is I kept losing count and having to restart. Turns out counting is stimulating, not relaxing.
By three hundred I was angry at the sheep.
reactions: Whoever invented counting sheep as a sleep aid was already asleep when they thought of it.

statCheck.pass: The trick isn't counting: it's boring yourself into submission. I've had more luck breathing slowly until my mind gives up and lets go.
statCheck.fail: Counting sheep doesn't work. Nothing works, some nights.

entries.intro: I stepped outside to check the sky and a {wildlife_type} bolted across my path. Nearly tripped over it.
details: Came out of nowhere: a dark shape moving fast at ankle height. I stumbled back and it was gone.
My heart was going like a drum for a full minute after.
reactions: More scared of it than it was of me, probably. Though I doubt that.

entries.intro: I woke up thirsty enough that the water from the jug tasted like the best thing I've ever had.
details: Room temperature water from a clay jug, and it might as well have been the finest drink in the world.
I drank the whole thing standing in the dark and went back to bed feeling reborn.
reactions: Everything tastes better when you need it badly enough.

entries.intro: The neighbor's chimney was smoking last night: I could smell it through my window. Someone was up late.
details: Woodsmoke in the night air has a different quality than during the day. Thicker, sweeter, more present.
I couldn't see it in the dark, but the smell mapped the wind direction for me.
reactions: Chimney smoke at night means someone's awake. That's a small comfort.

statCheck.pass: Smoke sinks in cold air and rises in warm air. If I can smell the chimney at ground level, the air is colder than the smoke: means frost by morning.
statCheck.fail: Smoke in the night air that smelled nice. That's all I've got.

entries.intro: I saw the first frost of the season on the ground this morning. Everything glittering and stiff.
details: Each blade of grass was edged in white. The fence rails had a fine crystal coating that melted under my fingers.
My breath made thick clouds in the air. Winter declaring itself with a gentle warning.
reactions: Frost is beautiful and a threat, all at once. I admire it from the doorway.

statCheck.pass: Frost forms heaviest on metal and stone because they lose heat fastest. Wood stays warmer longer: that's why the fence rails frost first and the posts last.
statCheck.fail: Frost means cold and cold means winter. Not much more to say.

entries.intro: I thought I heard someone knock on the door at three in the morning. Lay there frozen, listening.
details: Three slow taps: I was sure of it, but when I went to the door, no one was there.
Might've been the shutter, might've been in my head. I locked the door anyway.
reactions: A knock in the night is never just a knock. It's a question you don't want to answer.

entries.intro: The guard's dog sat with me on the step for a while last night. We shared a comfortable silence.
details: It leaned against my leg and sighed: that big, full-body exhale dogs do when they settle in.
We watched the empty path together until the guard whistled it back.
reactions: Sometimes the best company at night is someone who doesn't need to talk.

entries.intro: I heard the {mob_type} fighting among themselves {direction} last night. Snarling and crashing.
details: It went on for a while: sharp cries and the sound of things breaking. Then silence.
Better they fight each other than bother us, the guard said.
reactions: I don't enjoy hearing it. But I'd rather they settle things out there than in here.

statCheck.pass: Internal conflict means a dominance dispute: a new leader trying to take over. While they're fighting each other, they leave the area alone.
statCheck.fail: They made a lot of noise and then stopped. Not my business what happened.

entries.intro: I found the pattern of three stars that my father used to point out. Still there, same as when I was small.
details: He'd put me on his shoulders so I could see over the rooftops. I can see them from the ground now.
They haven't moved. The whole world changes and those three points stay fixed.
reactions: There's something steady about a sky that doesn't change. My father knew that.

entries.intro: I spent half the night listening to a drip somewhere in the house. Couldn't find it in the dark.
details: Slow, irregular drips: just when I'd forget about it, another one, then nothing, then tap.
This morning I found it: a crack in the shutters letting rain onto the sill.
reactions: A drip in the dark is louder than a thunderstorm. The brain won't let it go.

statCheck.pass: I tracked the drip to a hairline crack in the shutter frame. The wood swells with moisture and pulls apart at the grain: needs sealing before the real rains come.
statCheck.fail: A drip somewhere: found the source, fixed it, end of story.

entries.intro: I woke up laughing from a dream and couldn't remember what was funny. Just the laughter lingering.
details: I lay there grinning in the dark like a fool, chasing the punchline that was already gone.
Whatever my dreaming mind thought was hilarious, my waking mind will never know.
reactions: Waking up laughing is the best way to start a day, even if you don't know why.

entries.intro: My blankets smelled like lavender last night. My partner must've dried them near the herbs.
details: A faint, clean scent that gets stronger when you bury your face in the fabric.
I fell asleep faster than I have in weeks.
reactions: Small, thoughtful things like that do more for my sleep than any remedy.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} was rummaging through the compost pile at the back of the house before dawn.
details: I could hear it digging and snuffling: nosing through scraps with single-minded enthusiasm.
When I opened the shutter it looked up, paused, then went right back to eating.
reactions: At least someone appreciates my table scraps.

statCheck.pass: If they're eating compost, they'll leave the garden alone. Better scraps than seedlings: I don't mind sharing what I've already thrown away.
statCheck.fail: Something in the compost pile. It helps itself every night.

entries.intro: I slept through the entire night without waking once. I don't think that's happened in months.
details: No noise, no dreams, no cramps, no thirst. Just unbroken rest from dark to light.
I woke up feeling like a different person. Alert, rested, calm.
reactions: One good night's sleep and the whole world looks manageable again.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} were quiet {direction} last night. Quieter than they've been in weeks.
details: No howls, no crashing, no noise at all from that direction. Just the usual night sounds.
The guard said the quiet nights make him more nervous than the loud ones.
reactions: I slept better for it. But I understand what the guard means.

statCheck.pass: Silence after a period of noise usually means they've moved camp. They rotate through areas and the lull can last a week or more.
statCheck.fail: Quiet is quiet. I'll take it and not question it.

entries.intro: I sat by the fire until the last ember died and the room went completely dark. Didn't want to move.
details: Watching a fire die is hypnotic: the glow shrinking, the coals dimming one by one.
The last coal held on for a long time: a single orange point in all that black. Then it was gone.
reactions: There's a strange peace in watching something end quietly.

entries.intro: I woke up with the blankets completely off the bed. All of them, piled on the floor.
details: I must've been thrashing. No memory of it: just woke up cold on a bare mattress.
My partner says I fight the blankets in my sleep like they're an enemy.
reactions: My sleeping self and my waking self have very different ideas about blankets.

statCheck.pass: I thrash when I'm too warm: the blankets come off because my body is trying to cool down, not because I'm restless. A lighter blanket fixes it.
statCheck.fail: Blankets on the floor again. My sleeping self has opinions about bedding.

entries.intro: The first light of dawn hit the frost this morning and the whole world caught fire. Everything was gold and white.
details: The ice crystals on the fence turned into tiny prisms: flashing colors as the angle of the sun shifted.
It lasted maybe ten minutes before the frost melted. I'm glad I was awake for it.
reactions: The best show of the day happens before most people open their eyes.

entries.intro: I could hear an owl last night. That low, rounded call that seems to come from everywhere and nowhere.
details: I tried to find it but the sound bounced off the buildings. Seemed to shift direction every time it called.
Eventually I just sat and listened. Two notes, over and over, spaced evenly apart.
reactions: Owls own the night in a way nothing else does. That sound is authority.

statCheck.pass: The call bounces because the owl shapes the sound deliberately, angling it to confuse prey about the direction. If you can't find the owl, neither can the mouse.
statCheck.fail: I heard an owl but couldn't find it. That's owls for you.

entries.intro: My candle burned down to nothing last night while I was reading. Didn't notice until the flame touched the table.
details: A small scorch mark on the wood now. Not bad, but noticeable.
I was so absorbed I forgot I was even reading by candlelight. The story ate the time.
reactions: A good story is dangerous to candles and tabletops.

entries.intro: I noticed a {wildlife_type} sleeping under the porch when I came home late. Curled up tight, completely still.
details: I could just barely see it breathing: the tiniest rise and fall. Peaceful as anything.
I stepped over it and went inside quietly. Felt wrong to wake it.
reactions: Even the wildlife knows the best spots. Can't blame it for picking the driest corner.

entries.intro: The wind changed direction twice last night. I could tell because the shutter kept banging on different sides.
details: First the east shutter rattling, then quiet, then the west one picked up. Like the wind was circling the house.
I tied both down eventually and the house went quiet.
reactions: Restless wind makes for a restless house.

statCheck.pass: Shifting wind means a weather front moving through: the old air pushing out and the new air pushing in. By morning the wind will settle into the new direction.
statCheck.fail: The wind couldn't make up its mind. Neither could the shutters.

entries.intro: I found a snail trail across the floor this morning. A shining line from the door to the hearth.
details: A perfect silver path drawn on the stone: straight as an arrow, like it knew exactly where it was going.
The snail itself was tucked behind a warm stone by the hearth. Made itself right at home.
reactions: Hard to be angry at something that slow. It worked all night to get that far.

statCheck.pass: Snails follow warmth trails: they can feel the heat radiating from a hearth through the stone floor. That straight line was the warmest path between the cold door and the fire.
statCheck.fail: The snail got from the door to the fire. How it decided on the route is beyond me.

entries.intro: The full moon last night was so bright I could read by it. Held up a page and made out every word.
details: The light was silver-white and steady: no flicker like a candle, just clean, even illumination.
My shadow on the wall was as sharp as it would be at noon. Just colder in color.
reactions: Full moon nights don't feel like night at all. More like a dim, silver day.

entries.intro: I had sleep paralysis last night. Awake, aware, completely unable to move.
details: My eyes were open and I could see the room, but my body was stone. Every limb pinned down.
It lasted maybe thirty seconds, but those seconds stretched out like hours.
reactions: Your own body trapping you inside itself: that's a particular kind of fear.

statCheck.pass: It happens when you wake up before the body lifts its sleep paralysis. The brain is awake but the muscles are still locked for dreaming: harmless, just terrifying.
statCheck.fail: I couldn't move and I don't know why. It passed, and that's all I can say.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} passed close enough {direction} that the guard could smell them. He said the stench was unmistakable.
details: A sharp, acrid smell: like rotting leather and smoke. It hung in the air for an hour after they passed.
Some of us could smell it from our houses with the windows closed.
reactions: You can see them, hear them, and now smell them. They're getting too comfortable.

statCheck.pass: The smell means they've been camping in one spot too long and their refuse is building up. They'll move soon because even they can't stand it after a while.
statCheck.fail: They smelled bad and they were close. That's two things I don't like.

entries.intro: I accidentally left the lantern burning all night and woke to find the room still lit. Disorienting.
details: For a moment I didn't know if it was night or morning. The lantern glow had replaced the darkness I expected.
The oil is expensive enough that I cursed myself for wasting it. But I slept well.
reactions: Maybe I should leave the light on more often. My purse would disagree.

statCheck.pass: I actually sleep better with a dim light than in total dark. Something about knowing I can see if I open my eyes keeps the anxiety at bay.
statCheck.fail: Wasted oil, good sleep. Not the worst trade I've made.

entries.intro: I heard a tree branch crack and fall somewhere close last night. A sharp snap and then a heavy crash.
details: It sounded enormous in the quiet: like a wall collapsing. But it was probably just one dead limb.
This morning I found it across the path. Rotten through: it was going to fall eventually.
reactions: Every sound at night gets multiplied by ten in my head.

statCheck.pass: Dead branches fall on still nights because the temperature drops and the wood contracts. The cold finishes what the rot started.
statCheck.fail: A branch fell. Rotten wood gives way eventually.

entries.intro: I spotted three shooting stars in a row last night. One after another across the same patch of sky.
details: The first was fast, the second slower, and the third left a faint trail that lingered for a breath.
I waited for a fourth but the sky kept its silence after that.
reactions: Three in a row feels like a message, even though I know it's just rocks and air.

entries.intro: I lay in bed listing all the sounds I could hear. Got to nine before I lost track.
details: Wind, insects, a distant dog, the house settling, my own breathing, the shutter, dripping water, a bird, and something I couldn't name.
The unnamed one bothered me the most. A faint hum, just at the edge of hearing.
reactions: The night isn't quiet: it's full, and you just have to stop and count.

entries.intro: I woke up with my hand hanging off the side of the bed, fingers brushing the cold floor.
details: It was numb from the cold and the blood draining out. Took a full minute of shaking to get feeling back.
As a child I was terrified of leaving a hand off the bed. Apparently grown-up me isn't worried.
reactions: My sleeping body arranges itself in ways I'd never choose.

statCheck.pass: Hands lose heat fastest because the blood vessels are close to the surface. Dangling off the bed puts them in the coldest air in the room: right above the floor.
statCheck.fail: Cold hand, numb fingers. I tucked it back under the blanket.

entries.intro: I sat outside with a mug of warm cider and watched the night settle in. Twilight to full dark: the whole transition.
details: The colors drained out of things slowly: green to grey to black. Like watching a painting dry in reverse.
The first star appeared while I was mid-sip. Then a dozen, then hundreds.
reactions: Watching the day end from start to finish is time well spent.

entries.intro: A spider lowered itself onto my face while I slept. I felt the thread on my cheek and woke up swatting.
details: By the time I was awake it was gone. But the feeling of that thread lingered on my skin for an hour.
I lit the candle and searched the ceiling, but nothing was there: as if it never happened.
reactions: Spiders own the night ceiling. We're just guests below.

entries.intro: I went out to check the rain barrel last night and the water was perfectly still. A dark mirror.
details: I could see the stars in it: a tiny rectangular sky captured in old wood.
A leaf dropped in and the stars shattered into rings.
reactions: Still water at night is one of those things that makes you stop and forget what you came out for.

entries.intro: I sneezed in the middle of the night and it was so loud in the silence it scared me more than anything else.
details: Just a sneeze. But in a dead-quiet room at two in the morning, it sounded like an explosion.
I lay there with my heart pounding, embarrassed at being frightened by my own face.
reactions: The body doesn't care about timing. Sneezes arrive when they arrive.

statCheck.pass: Sneezing in your sleep means the air irritated your nose: dust, pollen, dry air. A damp cloth over the face helps some people, but I'd feel ridiculous.
statCheck.fail: I sneezed and it scared me. That's the whole story.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} lit a campfire {direction} last night. First time I've seen their fire from here.
details: Just a pinpoint of orange light, flickering between the trees. Steady enough to be a camp.
The guard watched it for his whole shift. It was still burning when the sun came up.
reactions: Seeing their fire from our settlement means the distance is shrinking.

statCheck.pass: A fire visible from here means they're not hiding. Groups that show fire are resting, not planning: hidden camps are the real concern.
statCheck.fail: Their fire, their business. I just wish I couldn't see it from my window.

entries.intro: I dreamed I could fly last night. Low and slow, just above the rooftops.
details: I could feel the wind on my face and see the settlement laid out below me: every roof, every path.
It felt completely natural: like I'd always been able to do it and just forgot.
reactions: I woke up disappointed that gravity was still in charge.

entries.intro: I woke up to the sound of something heavy being dragged outside. Slow, scraping, deliberate.
details: It went on for a solid minute: a heavy grinding sound along the path.
Turned out it was the guard moving a log to block a gap in the fence. Nothing sinister.
reactions: Every innocent sound at night wears a suspicious costume.

entries.intro: The crickets were so loud last night I could feel the vibration in the walls. A solid wall of sound.
details: It rises and falls in waves: gets louder, holds, drops slightly, then builds again.
Close your eyes and it sounds like the ground itself is humming.
reactions: Cricket song is the sound of a warm night doing what it's supposed to.

statCheck.pass: The louder the crickets, the warmer the night: their chirp rate tracks the temperature almost exactly. You can count the chirps and estimate the heat.
statCheck.fail: Crickets chirp louder some nights. I don't know why.

entries.intro: I woke up tangled in the sheets so tightly I panicked before I figured out what was holding me.
details: My legs were wrapped and my arms were pinned. Took a full ten seconds of thrashing to get free.
The sheets were twisted into ropes. I must've been spinning in my sleep.
reactions: My bed is a wrestling opponent I fight every night and lose.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} was silhouetted on the roof ridge when I looked out. Just sitting there against the stars.
details: A dark shape, perfectly balanced, head turning slowly. It looked like a decoration placed there by a sculptor.
It stayed for a long while, then dropped off the far side without a sound.
reactions: The roof belongs to the wildlife at night. I'm just the tenant below.

entries.intro: I stayed up late mending a shirt and the house got so quiet I could hear the needle going through the fabric.
details: A tiny, intimate sound: the pop of the needle through cloth, the hiss of thread pulling through.
The fire had gone to coals and the candle was low. Just me and the work and the silence.
reactions: There's a particular kind of peace in working late when the rest of the world is asleep.

statCheck.pass: Late-night handwork is the only time I'm completely present: no distractions, no obligations, just the task and the needle. I think that's why I sleep well after.
statCheck.fail: Mending by candlelight. Quiet work for quiet hours.

entries.intro: I keep waking at the same hour every night. I don't know the exact time, but it's always the same darkness.
details: My body has a clock I can't see or change. Some internal alarm with no purpose.
I lie there and wait for sleep to take me back. It always does, eventually.
reactions: Reliable insomnia is almost worse than random insomnia. At least random feels less personal.

statCheck.pass: It usually means something physical: digestion, temperature, the bladder. The body wakes for a reason, even if the mind doesn't register what it is.
statCheck.fail: I wake up and I don't know why. That's the whole story.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} have been keeping their distance {direction} for the last few nights. The guard seems pleased.
details: No tracks near the fence, no sounds after dark. A quiet stretch we haven't had in a while.
Some people think the extra torches are working. Others say they just moved on naturally.
reactions: I'll enjoy the peace while it lasts and try not to wonder when it ends.

statCheck.pass: Extra light on the perimeter does work: they prefer to operate in darkness and avoid lit areas. More torches means less incentive to come close.
statCheck.fail: They backed off. Why doesn't matter as much as how long.

entries.intro: I woke up with the taste of copper in my mouth. No nosebleed, no cut: just the taste.
details: Bitter and metallic, like I'd been chewing on a coin in my sleep.
It faded after a cup of water, but the first few minutes were unpleasant.
reactions: The body does things in the night it refuses to explain in the morning.

entries.intro: The trees were completely still last night. No wind at all: every leaf frozen in place.
details: The branches looked like they were carved from stone. Not a single movement anywhere.
In daylight, still trees are unremarkable. At night, they look like they're listening.
reactions: Stillness at night has a weight to it that stillness in the day never does.

statCheck.pass: Dead-still air usually means a high pressure system sitting overhead. It'll hold for a day or two and then break with wind or rain.
statCheck.fail: No wind at all. Unusual, but I can't explain it.

entries.intro: I burned my fingers on the candle snuffer last night. Forgot the metal gets hot.
details: A quick, sharp sting and the smell of singed skin. Such a small, stupid injury.
I stood in the dark sucking my fingertips and feeling foolish.
reactions: You'd think after years of snuffing candles I'd remember that metal conducts heat.

entries.intro: I woke to my cat kneading my chest with its claws, purring like a small engine.
details: Tiny needle pricks through the blanket, rhythmic and insistent. It was completely content.
I didn't have the heart to push it off. So I lay there being punctured.
reactions: Cats show love in ways that are technically painful.

entries.intro: The sunset bled into the clouds last night and the whole western sky turned purple. Deep, rich, endless purple.
details: It darkened from lavender to violet to near-black, layer by layer. Like watching a bruise form on the sky.
By the time the first stars showed through, the purple was still there at the edges: clinging on.
reactions: Purple sunsets are rare enough that I stop whatever I'm doing to watch.

entries.intro: I got up for water and stepped on something cold and wet on the floor. Stood there frozen trying to decide what it was.
details: Turned out to be a damp cloth I'd left drying on the chair. Must've slipped off.
But those three seconds of cold, wet mystery in the dark were enough to wake me completely.
reactions: The mind fills darkness with the worst explanation first.

entries.intro: I left the back door open by accident last night and the house was full of moths by morning.
details: Drawn to the lantern: a dozen of them, circling the ceiling, bouncing off the walls.
Took me twenty minutes to shoo them all out. Some of them kept coming back.
reactions: An open door at night is an open invitation to everything that flies.

statCheck.pass: Moths navigate by the moon's angle. A lantern confuses them because it's a closer, brighter light source: they spiral toward it thinking it's the sky.
statCheck.fail: Moths and open doors don't mix. Close the door, keep the moths out.

entries.intro: The moon was orange when it rose last night. Huge and low, sitting on the horizon like a coal.
details: It turned from orange to gold to white as it climbed: like a piece of metal cooling after the forge.
By the time it was high it looked normal. But that first hour was something else.
reactions: An orange moon rising is one of those sights that makes you glad you looked up.

statCheck.pass: The orange color comes from the atmosphere bending the light when the moon is low. Same reason sunsets are red: more air between you and the light.
statCheck.fail: Orange moon, low on the horizon. I don't know the science, but it was beautiful.

entries.intro: I heard a {wildlife_type} on the roof last night. Small feet, quick and light, pattering back and forth.
details: It sounded like it was running laps: one end to the other, pause, then back again.
I banged on the ceiling with a broom handle and it stopped. For about thirty seconds.
reactions: My roof apparently doubles as a running track after dark.

entries.intro: I realized last night that I don't know any constellations. I just look at the stars as a whole.
details: People point out shapes and patterns, and I nod along. But honestly I just see a scatter of light.
Maybe that's fine. The sky doesn't need me to organize it.
reactions: I enjoy the stars more without trying to turn them into pictures.

statCheck.pass: My father said the patterns are just stories people told to remember which stars come up in which season. The pictures aren't in the sky: they're in the calendar.
statCheck.fail: Stars are stars. I enjoy them without the labels.

entries.intro: I woke up thinking it was time to get up, then heard the night insects still going. Not even close.
details: That sinking feeling when you realize the night is barely half over and you're already awake.
Tried to guess the hour by the darkness level. Failed completely.
reactions: The worst part of waking early isn't the lost sleep: it's knowing how much night is left.

entries.intro: I watched a bat catch something in the air right in front of me last night. A sudden, sharp turn and snap.
details: One second it was flying straight, the next it folded sideways and caught something invisible. Reflexes like nothing I've seen.
It flew off with its prize. The whole thing took less than a heartbeat.
reactions: Bats are the fastest hunters I've ever watched. They make it look easy.

statCheck.pass: They hunt by sound, not sight: those sharp turns are corrections based on echoes. They shout and listen for the bounce, faster than your eye can follow.
statCheck.fail: It turned and caught something. That's all I saw.

entries.intro: I tried sleeping in a different position last night and woke up feeling like a new person.
details: Always slept on my back, but I tried my side on a whim and the difference was immediate.
Neck doesn't hurt, back doesn't ache. One small change.
reactions: I've been sleeping wrong for years, apparently.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} were dragging something {direction} last night. We could hear it scraping across the ground.
details: A heavy, rhythmic scraping sound that went on for a long time. Getting gradually quieter.
Nobody went to look. The guard said it was better not to know.
reactions: Some sounds you hear at night, you file away and don't ask about.

statCheck.pass: They drag food back to camp: carcasses, foraged material, bulk transport. The stockpiling means they plan to stay in the area a while.
statCheck.fail: They were dragging something, and I didn't see what. I didn't want to.

entries.intro: I made a tea of dried herbs before bed and it actually worked. Slept deep and woke rested.
details: A simple mixture: just what was growing by the fence, dried and steeped. Bitter, but drinkable.
My grandmother swore by it. I always thought she was making it up.
reactions: Turns out my grandmother was right about most things. Especially the things I doubted.

statCheck.pass: The bitter ones are usually the ones that work: something in the leaves relaxes the chest and slows the breathing. Old knowledge, but it holds.
statCheck.fail: I drank it, I slept. Cause and effect or coincidence: I don't care.

entries.intro: I heard water running somewhere last night. Not rain: a steady trickle, like a stream that shouldn't be there.
details: I followed the sound and found a gutter overflow running down the side of the house. Blocked with leaves.
Cleared it with my hands in the dark and the trickle stopped. Simple enough.
reactions: Night sounds that have explanations are the best kind.

statCheck.pass: Running water at night that shouldn't be there is always worth checking. A blocked gutter overflows into the foundation and you'll have rot inside a season.
statCheck.fail: I found the trickle and fixed it. That's about the limit of my plumbing.

entries.intro: I stood at the window and watched a single cloud cross the moon. Took about two minutes.
details: The leading edge dimmed the light first: a slow fade like someone turning down a lamp.
Then the trailing edge cleared and the light came back, sudden and sharp.
reactions: Watching a cloud cross the moon is the slowest entertainment and somehow enough.

entries.intro: I stubbed my toe on the bed frame getting up in the dark. Same toe, same frame, same mistake.
details: That instant flash of pain that goes straight from the toe to the brain with no stops in between.
I sat on the floor holding my foot, saying words I wouldn't say in daylight.
reactions: I've lived in this house for years and the bed frame still wins.

statCheck.pass: I've tried moving the bed, but the room is too small: every position puts a corner in the path to the door. The toe is just the price of a small house.
statCheck.fail: Same toe, same bed frame. You'd think I'd learn.

entries.intro: The dawn chorus was spectacular this morning. Wave after wave of birdsong building from one voice to a hundred.
details: It started as a single thread of sound in the dark and grew until the air was thick with it.
By the time the sky had color, every bird in the area was going at full volume.
reactions: Being awake early enough to hear the whole thing from the start is a rare privilege.

entries.intro: I dreamed about teeth falling out again. One by one, into my cupped hands.
details: They came loose easily: no blood, no pain. Just a gentle tug and then the weight of a tooth in my palm.
I woke up and immediately ran my tongue across my teeth. All still there.
reactions: That dream visits everyone eventually. It's the tax on having teeth, I suppose.

entries.intro: I heard geese flying over in the dark last night. That honking sound high above, moving fast.
details: Couldn't see them at all: just the sound, drifting across the sky from one horizon to the other.
They sounded determined. Whatever their destination was, they weren't stopping here.
reactions: Something about hearing birds migrate at night makes you think about journeys.

statCheck.pass: They fly at night because the air is calmer and the winds are more predictable at altitude. Cooler air means less turbulence for tired wings.
statCheck.fail: Geese in the dark, heading somewhere. I don't know why they fly at night.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} got into the chicken coop last night. More mess than damage: scattered feathers everywhere.
details: The chickens were in a state this morning: puffed up and outraged. None missing, just upset.
I found the gap in the wire where it squeezed through. Patched it with a bit of bent metal.
reactions: The chickens will hold a grudge about this for days.

entries.intro: The air last night smelled like cut hay and warm stone. Like the day was slowly releasing what it had stored.
details: That sun-baked scent that only comes out after dark, when the stone walls give up their warmth.
I sat on the step and just breathed. Each breath tasted like summer.
reactions: Some nights smell so good they make you forgive the heat of the day that caused them.

statCheck.pass: Warm stone releases its heat slowly: the thicker the wall, the longer it holds. My grandmother said you can tell a well-built house by how warm the outside wall is after sunset.
statCheck.fail: The air smelled good and the stone was warm. A pleasant night.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} broke something {direction} last night. A cracking sound, like timber splintering.
details: One sharp crack and then quiet. The guard went out at first light and found a damaged fence post.
Could've been deliberate, could've been an accident as they passed. Hard to say.
reactions: Repairing what they break is just part of the routine now.

statCheck.pass: They test barriers by pushing against posts and leaning on fences. If something gives they remember it: not malice, just assessment.
statCheck.fail: They broke something, and we'll fix it. That's how it goes.

entries.intro: I found a warm spot on my bed last night where the cat had been lying before I got in. Like a gift.
details: That perfect circle of warmth in the cold sheets. I curled up in it immediately.
The cat was annoyed at being displaced, but I was already comfortable.
reactions: Some nights the small comforts are the ones that matter most.

entries.intro: I woke up speaking in the middle of a sentence. Just words coming out of my mouth, mid-thought.
details: I was saying something about bread. Actual words, clearly spoken, to an empty room.
My partner woke up and asked who I was talking to. I had no answer.
reactions: My sleeping mind holds conversations my waking mind isn't invited to.

statCheck.pass: Sleep talking runs in my family: my mother did it, her father did it. The words are nonsense but the delivery is completely convincing.
statCheck.fail: I talk in my sleep sometimes. Can't control it.

entries.intro: The stars were arranged in a way last night that I'd never noticed before. A long, curved line of bright ones.
details: Maybe it's always been there and I just never looked at the right angle. Or maybe the sky rearranged itself.
I traced the line with my finger: from the horizon to nearly overhead. Must've been twenty stars in a row.
reactions: The sky has more patterns than anyone could find in a lifetime.

entries.intro: I couldn't get comfortable last night. Every position had something wrong with it: an ache, a pressure, a draft.
details: On my back the ceiling stared at me, on my side my hip dug into the mattress. On my stomach I couldn't breathe.
I cycled through every option twice and none of them improved.
reactions: Some nights the bed and the body just refuse to cooperate.

entries.intro: The night was warm enough to sit outside without a coat. That brief window when the season is perfect.
details: Not too hot, not too cool: the air soft on bare arms and insects humming but not biting.
I stayed out until I started to doze in the chair. Went to bed feeling grateful.
reactions: Perfect nights are so rare I make a point to notice them.

entries.intro: I dreamed I was back in a place I lived as a child. Every detail was perfect: the smell, the light, the sounds.
details: The kitchen table, the crack in the plaster, the sound of the door. All of it stored somewhere in my head for years.
When I woke up, the room I was in felt like the wrong one.
reactions: Memory is ruthless. It brings things back so clearly that the present feels thin.

statCheck.pass: I think the brain stores places more completely than it stores events. Every detail of that kitchen is still in there, decades later, sharper than last week.
statCheck.fail: I dreamed about a place from long ago. The mind holds what it holds.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} ran across my path while I was walking home in the dark. Just a streak of movement at the edge of the lantern light.
details: Gone before I could even focus on it: there and not-there in the same breath.
My lantern swung and the shadows jumped. For a second, everything was moving.
reactions: Lantern light only shows you a small circle. Everything outside it is a mystery.

entries.intro: I woke up cold because I'd kicked every blanket off in my sleep. My body is its own worst enemy.
details: All three blankets in a heap on the floor. Not one left on the bed.
I gathered them all back, tucked the edges under the mattress, and was warm again in minutes.
reactions: I should tie the blankets to the bed frame. I'm not above it at this point.

entries.intro: The guard told me he saw a deer drinking from the stream in full moonlight last night. Said it was the most peaceful thing he's ever seen on duty.
details: Head down, ears relaxed, just drinking: no fear, no hurry, like the night belonged to it.
He said he stood there and watched it for five minutes before it wandered off.
reactions: The night shift isn't all tension. Sometimes it's just quiet beauty.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} were drumming on something {direction} for most of the night. A dull, rhythmic pounding.
details: Not random: a steady beat, like a heartbeat. It went on for hours.
The guard counted the intervals between pauses. Said the rhythm never changed.
reactions: Hearing them keep a beat like that makes them feel less like animals and more like neighbors I don't want.

statCheck.pass: Drumming is communication: they signal location to scattered groups. The steadier the rhythm, the calmer the camp, so erratic drumming is when you worry.
statCheck.fail: They drummed all night and I don't know why. I just know I didn't sleep.

entries.intro: I slept so hard last night I have a crease on my face from the pillow. Still there hours later.
details: A deep line running from my cheek to my temple. Like a scar made of sleep.
People have been looking at it all morning. I've stopped explaining.
reactions: At least a pillow crease means I slept well. The evidence just lingers longer than I'd like.

entries.intro: I listened to the rain change from heavy to light to mist over the course of the night. Three distinct moods.
