details: Heavy rain is a roar, light rain is a patter. Mist is a whisper you can barely hear.
I lay there and tracked the transitions. The heaviest burst was around midnight.
reactions: Rain tells you everything about its own story if you listen long enough.

statCheck.pass: The heaviest rain comes first because the big drops fall faster. As the storm weakens, the drops get smaller and fall slower: heavy to light to mist is just the storm running out of energy.
statCheck.fail: Rain changes pace through the night. I just listen to the shifts.

entries.intro: I sneaked outside to eat a piece of pie in the dark because I didn't want to share. Worth it.
details: Sat on the step with the plate on my knees, eating slowly so nobody would hear.
The night air made the pastry cold around the edges, but the inside was still good.
reactions: Some pleasures are better stolen in the dark.

entries.intro: A moth the size of my hand landed on the wall above my bed last night. I could see it by candlelight.
details: Pale wings with dark eye-spots that looked like they were staring at me. Beautiful and unsettling.
It stayed there the whole night. This morning it was gone, like it was never there.
reactions: The night brings visitors you never see arrive and never see leave.

entries.intro: I left my boots outside and they were soaked with dew by morning. Cold, wet leather on bare feet: a grim start.
details: The insides were damp too. Every step squelched for the first hour.
I've been told a hundred times to bring them in. Apparently I need to be told again.
reactions: Wet boots are a consequence of laziness that punishes you all day long.

statCheck.pass: Dew condenses on the coldest surface first, and leather cools faster than wood. Boots left outside will be soaked before the porch rails even get damp.
statCheck.fail: Wet boots, my own fault. I'll remember next time, though I know I won't.

entries.intro: The guard lit a new torch and for a moment the flame was so bright it lit up the whole street. Like a burst of daylight.
details: Fresh pitch catches fast: a whoosh of orange that makes you squint and then settles into a steady burn.
My eyes had adjusted to the dark and the sudden brightness felt like an intrusion.
reactions: New torchlight is an announcement. The dark had claimed that space and the fire took it back.

entries.intro: I woke up and immediately remembered a problem I'd been stuck on for days. The answer was just there, clear as morning.
details: My sleeping mind had worked on it without telling me. Laid the solution out like it was obvious.
I got up and wrote it down before it could evaporate.
reactions: The brain does its best work when you stop pestering it.

entries.intro: I heard a {wildlife_type} calling from somewhere close, then an answering call from far off. A conversation across the dark.
details: Back and forth, back and forth. The close one loud and insistent, the far one faint but steady.
It went on for twenty minutes. Then the close one stopped, and the far one fell silent a moment later.
reactions: I don't know what they were saying, but it sounded important to them.

entries.intro: I woke up to the floor being ice cold. The kind of cold that seeps up through your soles and into your bones.
details: Had to hop across the room to get my boots. Every step a small agony.
The fire had been dead for hours and the stone had given up every trace of warmth.
reactions: Stone floors are honest: they tell you exactly what the temperature is.

statCheck.pass: Stone floors follow the outside temperature with about a four-hour delay. The coldest floor is around dawn, when the night's cold finally reaches the inside.
statCheck.fail: Cold floor, cold feet. Not much mystery to it.

entries.intro: I noticed my shadow moving differently than me when I walked past the torch. A half-second delay, stretched and bent.
details: Torchlight flickers, so the shadow shudders and shifts: it looked alive in a way that sunlight shadows don't.
I waved my arm and the shadow waved back, but slower, and from a different angle.
reactions: Shadows at night feel like separate creatures borrowing your shape.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} retreated {direction} after the guard blew the warning horn last night. The sound spooked them.
details: Two short blasts and they scattered. You could hear them crashing through underbrush for a full minute.
The guard looked pleased with himself. Said the horn works better than torches most nights.
reactions: At least they still respect the horn. The day they don't, we have a problem.

statCheck.pass: Loud, sudden noise disrupts their coordination. They rely on hearing each other, so a blast of horn drowns out their signals and they scatter by instinct.
statCheck.fail: The horn went off, they ran. Simple and effective.

entries.intro: I tried reading by moonlight last night. Held the page up to the window and squinted.
details: I could make out the largest letters but the rest was just dark marks on a grey page.
Gave up after two paragraphs and just stared at the sky instead.
reactions: Moonlight is good for looking, not for reading. I should know the difference by now.

statCheck.pass: Moonlight looks bright to the eye but carries almost no useful detail. Your pupils open wide for it, which makes everything look lit, but the actual light is too dim for sharp edges.
statCheck.fail: Moonlight looks brighter than it is. You think you can see until you try to read.

entries.intro: I woke up genuinely confused about what day it was. Not just the hour: the entire day.
details: Lay there trying to reconstruct the week from memory. The days had blurred into a single smear.
Eventually gave up and decided it didn't matter. Whatever day it is, I still have to get up.
reactions: Losing track of days is either freedom or exhaustion. Lately it's been the second.

entries.intro: The guard let me hold his torch for a minute last night while he adjusted his belt. The heat on my face was immediate.
details: You don't realize how hot a torch is until you're holding one. The warmth pushes into your skin from a foot away.
The light made a perfect circle around us. Outside it: nothing.
reactions: Holding a torch at night makes you feel both powerful and exposed.

entries.intro: I woke to the most vivid dream dissolving like smoke. I could feel it leaving: pieces dropping away.
details: Colors, faces, a feeling of urgency: all of it fading faster than I could grab hold.
Within a minute it was gone. Just a residue of emotion with no story attached.
reactions: Losing a dream the moment you wake is like watching a letter burn before you can read it.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} knocked over the empty bucket by the door sometime before dawn. A metallic crash that woke the whole house.
details: Sounded like someone dropped an anvil. I was on my feet with my fists up before I knew what happened.
Found the bucket on its side and fresh tracks in the mud. The culprit was long gone.
reactions: I've moved the bucket inside. My nerves can't handle another morning like that.

entries.intro: I sat by the window and watched the dew form on the glass, one tiny bead at a time.
details: They grew from nothing: appearing, swelling, merging with their neighbors, then rolling down in a line.
By morning the whole pane was a map of rivers I'd watched being born.
reactions: Watching something happen that slowly either means insomnia or patience. Last night it was both.

statCheck.pass: Dew forms when the glass temperature drops below the dew point. The beads grow because each one acts as a seed for more moisture: the bigger they get, the faster they collect.
statCheck.fail: Water on the glass: I watched it form and it kept me busy.

entries.intro: I fell asleep in the chair by the fire and woke up with a stiff neck and a pattern pressed into my cheek from the armrest.
details: My neck wouldn't turn to the left for an hour. Had to rotate my whole body to look at anything.
The chair was comfortable enough to fall asleep in but not kind enough to let me wake well.
reactions: There's a tax on falling asleep in chairs, and you pay it in your neck.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} made an odd noise {direction} last night: a high keening sound none of us had heard before.
details: Not a howl, not a growl: something between a whine and a screech, thin and wavering.
It stopped after a few minutes. The guard marked it in the log but couldn't name it.
reactions: New sounds from them are unsettling. I prefer the ones I've learned to ignore.

statCheck.pass: That keening usually means injury: one of them was hurt and calling for the group. It's a distress signal, not a threat.
statCheck.fail: A strange noise from a strange direction. I filed it away and tried to sleep.

entries.intro: I woke to the first warm breeze of spring coming through the window. Soft and damp, smelling like thawed earth.
details: After months of cold air, this felt like a promise. The kind of warmth that carries seeds and plans.
I lay there with the blanket half off, just letting it wash over me.
reactions: One warm night doesn't mean winter is done. But it means winter is thinking about leaving.

entries.intro: I heard the guard talking to himself on his rounds last night. Just a low murmur, keeping himself company.
details: Couldn't make out the words. Just the cadence of someone narrating their own evening.
He paused under my window, said something about the cold, and moved on.
reactions: Talking to yourself on a long night watch seems entirely reasonable.

entries.intro: I made the mistake of drinking water right before bed. Woke up three times to deal with the consequences.
details: Each time: stumble out of bed, navigate the dark, come back, try to sleep. Three times.
By the third trip I just lay there angry at my own bladder.
reactions: No water after sundown. I've learned this lesson before and I'll forget it again.

statCheck.pass: Drinking anything within an hour of sleep is the mistake. The body takes about that long to process it, so you wake up right as you're finally getting deep rest.
statCheck.fail: Water before bed, bad idea. I've been told before.

entries.intro: I found a hedgehog curled up in my boot this morning. Must've crawled in overnight for warmth.
details: I reached in without looking and got a handful of spines. A sharp, prickly surprise.
Had to tip the boot upside down and wait for it to uncurl and waddle away.
reactions: I can't even be angry: it's a good boot, warm and dry and sheltered. I'd pick it too.

entries.intro: The last thing I heard before falling asleep was the wind, and the first thing I heard waking up was the same wind. Like nothing had changed.
details: Same pitch, same direction, same steady push against the shutters. The whole night compressed into a single sound.
I could've slept for minutes or hours: the wind gave no clue.
reactions: The wind doesn't sleep. I find that either comforting or exhausting, depending on the night.

statCheck.pass: A steady wind that blows all night means a large weather system parked over the region. It won't change until the system moves, which could be days.
statCheck.fail: The wind blew all night, same direction, nothing more to add.

entries.intro: I woke up and the house was so quiet I could hear my own heartbeat. Just that and nothing else.
details: No wind, no insects, no creaking. Just a silence so complete it felt like pressure.
I held my breath to test if even that was too loud. It was.
reactions: True silence is rarer than people think. Most nights have a soundtrack.

entries.intro: I heard {mob_type} arguing with each other {direction} last night. Snarling and barking back and forth.
details: Two distinct voices going at it: one high and sharp, the other low and steady.
It ended suddenly, like one of them won the argument and the other just stopped.
reactions: Even they have disagreements. I find that oddly reassuring.

statCheck.pass: Squabbles over food or territory are common after dark. The louder they argue with each other, the less attention they're paying to us.
statCheck.fail: They were fighting about something. I didn't understand and didn't need to.

entries.intro: I dreamed I could fly, but only about two feet off the ground. Just hovering there, useless.
details: Not soaring over mountains: just floating above the floor like a bad magic trick.
I kept trying to go higher and couldn't. Two feet was the limit.
reactions: Even in dreams my ambitions come with restrictions.

entries.intro: My pillow was too warm last night. Flipped it, and the cool side lasted about thirty seconds.
details: I flipped it four times. Each time the relief got shorter until there was no cool side left.
Eventually I just lay on the bare mattress and accepted the heat.
reactions: A hot pillow on a warm night is a small torture that lasts for hours.

statCheck.pass: A pillow absorbs body heat faster than it releases it. Feather pillows are the worst: they trap warmth in the down and won't let it go.
statCheck.fail: Hot pillow, no solution. I've tried everything.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} was sitting in the middle of the path when I came home after dark. Just sitting there, calm as you please.
details: My lantern caught its eyes: two bright points staring at me without fear or hurry.
We looked at each other for a long moment, then it moved off at its own pace.
reactions: At night the paths belong to them. I'm just passing through.

statCheck.pass: Animals freeze in lantern light because the brightness ruins their night vision. They're not calm: they're blind for a moment, deciding whether to bolt.
statCheck.fail: It just sat there and stared. I don't know what it was thinking.

entries.intro: I slept with the window open and woke up to the smell of wet grass. Best alarm I've ever had.
details: The air was cool and green-smelling: that rich, earth-and-leaf scent that only comes before dawn.
I lay there breathing it in and didn't want to close the window or get up.
reactions: Some mornings arrive gently enough to make you grateful for them.

entries.intro: I lay awake counting the intervals between the guard's footsteps. Every thirty seconds, like clockwork.
details: Step, step, step, pause: the rhythm was hypnotic, almost like a lullaby made of footsteps.
I tried to use it as a sleep aid. It almost worked.
reactions: The guard's routine is the most reliable thing in my night.

entries.intro: I had a dream where I was late for something important but couldn't figure out what it was.
details: Running through familiar streets, heart pounding, checking a clock that had no numbers on it.
Woke up in a panic and then realized I had nowhere to be.
reactions: The feeling of being late sticks around long after the dream ends.

entries.intro: I watched a spider build a web in the corner of the window by candlelight. Patient, precise work.
details: It anchored each thread and tested it before moving on. No wasted motion at all.
By the time my candle burned down, the web was done: a perfect spiral catching the last light.
reactions: The spider finished more in one night than I managed all week.

statCheck.pass: They rebuild every night: tear down the old web at dusk, spin a new one in the dark. The silk loses its stickiness after a day, so a fresh web catches more.
statCheck.fail: A spider building a web. Kept me company while I couldn't sleep.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} lit a fire {direction} last night. You could see the glow through the trees.
details: An orange shimmer low on the horizon, steady and constant. Not a wildfire: too contained.
The guard counted the fires as a habit. Just the one, same spot as last week.
reactions: Seeing their campfire from here makes the distance between us feel smaller than it is.

statCheck.pass: One fire means a small camp: a dozen at most. When you see multiple fires, that's when the numbers are growing.
statCheck.fail: They had a fire going. That's all I could tell from here.

entries.intro: I woke up with my arm completely numb. Couldn't move it, couldn't feel it: just dead weight.
details: I'd slept on it wrong and now it was hanging there like it belonged to someone else.
Took a full minute of shaking and flexing before the tingling came back.
reactions: That pins-and-needles rush when the feeling returns is the worst reward for waking up.

entries.intro: There was a fog last night so thick I couldn't see my own hand at arm's length.
details: Opened the door to check the weather and the fog just poured in like something alive.
Sound carried strangely: close things sounded far, and far things sounded close.
reactions: Fog turns every familiar place into somewhere you've never been.

statCheck.pass: The thickest fogs come on still, warm nights after a cold day. The ground cools the warm air and the moisture just condenses right there at face level.
statCheck.fail: Thick fog: made everything strange, and that's all I can say about it.

entries.intro: I fell asleep to the sound of frogs and woke up to birdsong. The night shift changed without me noticing.
details: Frogs own the first half of the night: a low, rhythmic chorus that pulses in waves.
Somewhere around dawn the birds take over and the frogs go quiet, one by one.
reactions: The handoff between the night chorus and the morning chorus is the most reliable clock I know.

statCheck.pass: Frogs stop calling when the temperature drops below a certain point, and the birds start when the light hits a certain level. The gap between is the quietest part of the night.
statCheck.fail: Frogs at night, birds at dawn. Different voices, same schedule.

entries.intro: I woke up tangled in the sheets so tight I couldn't move my legs. My sleeping self apparently fights invisible enemies.
details: Both legs wrapped in a spiral of linen, pinned together like a cocoon.
Took a solid minute of kicking and pulling to get free.
reactions: I go to bed as a person and wake up as a wrapped package.

entries.intro: I sat outside last night and watched the moon rise. It came up huge and orange, right on the horizon.
details: It looked close enough to touch: swollen and warm, like it was melting into the ground.
Within an hour it had shrunk to its normal size and gone white. The trick was over.
reactions: The moon is never more beautiful than in the first ten minutes after it rises.

statCheck.pass: The moon's size doesn't change: it's a trick of the eye. Near the horizon your brain compares it to trees and buildings and thinks it's bigger, but overhead there's nothing to measure it against.
statCheck.fail: The moon looked enormous when it rose. It always does.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} was scratching at the garden fence in the dark. Persistent, unhurried scratching.
details: I could hear claws on wood, a steady rasp like someone filing down a plank.
By morning there were fresh marks on the bottom rail. Shallow grooves in the grain.
reactions: My fence is apparently an acceptable scratching post for the local wildlife.

statCheck.pass: They scratch to sharpen claws or mark territory: the scent glands in the paws leave a trace on the wood. If the marks are in the same spot, the same one keeps coming back.
statCheck.fail: Scratching on the fence. It'll stop when it gets bored.

entries.intro: I woke up thirsty in the middle of the night. That dry, papery feeling in your mouth and throat.
details: Got up for water and drank two full cups standing in the dark kitchen. Immediate relief.
The first sip hit my dry throat like rain on packed dirt.
reactions: Middle-of-the-night water is the best water. Nothing has ever tasted so necessary.

entries.intro: The wind picked up around midnight and the shutters started banging. Every few seconds: bang, bang.
details: I got up and latched them, but the latch is loose and they kept rattling in the frame.
Ended up wedging a boot against the window to keep them still.
reactions: A loose shutter in the wind is the most persistent sound a house can make.

statCheck.pass: Wind shifts direction around midnight when the land cools faster than the air above it. The shutter that faces the wrong way at sunset faces the wind by midnight.
statCheck.fail: Shutters banging: I fixed it with a boot. Good enough.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} were moving through the brush {direction} in a long line last night. You could track them by the sound.
details: Branches snapping one after another: a slow wave of noise moving from left to right.
The guard counted the disturbances and said at least eight or ten passed through.
reactions: Hearing them file past is like listening to a storm that decides not to stop here.

statCheck.pass: Single-file movement means they're traveling, not hunting. When they spread out and go quiet, that's when they're looking for something.
statCheck.fail: They passed by in a line. We watched and waited, and they kept going.

entries.intro: I dreamed I was eating the best meal of my life and woke up chewing on my own pillow.
details: My jaw was working away at the pillowcase like it was a piece of bread. Embarrassing even with no witnesses.
The dream was so convincing I could taste the food. Then just fabric.
reactions: The gap between dream food and pillow is the cruelest distance there is.

entries.intro: I noticed one particular star last night that was brighter than all the rest. Just blazing there, unwavering.
details: Every other star twinkled but this one held steady: a fixed, burning point.
I watched it for half an hour and it didn't flicker once.
reactions: Some stars just refuse to be part of the crowd.

statCheck.pass: The ones that don't twinkle aren't stars: they're planets, closer and bigger, so the air doesn't scatter their light the same way. Steady light means you're looking at a wanderer.
statCheck.fail: It was bright and steady. That's all I know about it.

entries.intro: I slept perfectly for the first time in weeks. No waking, no tossing, no strange dreams.
details: Closed my eyes and opened them to sunlight. The whole night passed in what felt like one breath.
My body felt like it had been rebuilt overnight: every joint loose, every muscle rested.
reactions: One good night of sleep makes you realize how badly you've been managing without it.

entries.intro: I heard an owl right outside the window last night. That low, two-note call, over and over.
details: It was so close I could hear the pause between each call: a deliberate, patient silence.
Every few minutes it would shift to a different perch, and the sound would come from a new direction.
reactions: An owl calling from your own wall is either a compliment or a claim. I'm not sure which.

statCheck.pass: The repeated two-note call is territorial: it's telling other owls this patch is taken. If you hear an answer there's a boundary dispute, and if silence: it won.
statCheck.fail: An owl outside the window. Kept at it for a while.

entries.intro: My neighbor's dog howled at nothing for an hour last night. At nothing visible, anyway.
details: A long, mournful sound that just went on and on. No apparent cause, no response.
I put the pillow over my head and it didn't help at all.
reactions: A howling dog at night is the loneliest sound in the world, and also the most annoying.

statCheck.pass: Dogs howl at sounds we can't hear: pitches too high or too low for our ears. To the dog there's always something out there, even if we can't tell what.
statCheck.fail: The dog howled at nothing. Dogs do that.

entries.intro: I woke up laughing: no idea why, just mid-laugh with tears in my eyes.
details: Whatever was funny is completely gone. Just the physical aftermath: sore cheeks and a fading grin.
My partner asked what was so funny and I had absolutely nothing to say.
reactions: Waking up happy for no reason is a gift you can't explain or repeat.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} was digging in the garden again last night. I could hear the soft scrape of earth being moved.
details: Found fresh holes this morning: neat, shallow pits where the vegetable rows used to be straight.
It's not malicious. It's just doing what it does, and what it does ruins my garden.
reactions: I've accepted that the garden is a shared resource, whether I agreed to it or not.

entries.intro: The thunder woke me around midnight. Not close: a distant, rolling grumble that went on for ages.
details: Each rumble lasted ten seconds or more, echoing off the hills like a conversation with itself.
I counted the gap between flash and sound. Miles away, but the sound filled the valley.
reactions: Distant thunder is the most comfortable kind. All the drama, none of the danger.

statCheck.pass: You can estimate the distance by counting the seconds between flash and thunder: about three seconds per mile. Anything over fifteen seconds is far enough to enjoy.
statCheck.fail: Thunder in the distance. Nothing more to say.

entries.intro: I woke up convinced someone was in the room. Sat up fast, heart hammering: nobody there.
details: The feeling was so strong I checked behind the door and under the bed. Nothing.
Took half an hour for my pulse to settle and the certainty to fade.
reactions: The mind invents company in the dark, and then scares itself with its own invention.

entries.intro: The stars were so thick last night the sky looked like spilled flour on a dark table.
details: Not just the bright ones: thousands of tiny ones behind them, a fine dust of light stretching to every horizon.
I tried to find a patch of sky with no stars in it. Couldn't.
reactions: Nights like that make you feel small in a way that's actually pleasant.

entries.intro: I got up to close the window and the floorboard creaked so loud it woke the whole house.
details: One step on one board and suddenly everyone's calling out asking what's wrong.
I stood frozen in the dark, one foot in the air, like I'd triggered a trap.
reactions: I know exactly which board it is now. I'll step over it next time.

entries.intro: I watched fireflies drifting through the yard last night. Tiny sparks floating in the dark, unhurried.
details: Each one blinked at its own rhythm: some fast, some slow, none in unison.
I tried to count them but they kept winking in and out. Could've been ten or a hundred.
reactions: Fireflies make the dark feel friendly. Light without heat, without hurry.

statCheck.pass: Only the males flash, each species with its own pattern like a code. The females sit in the grass and respond only to the right rhythm.
statCheck.fail: Fireflies blinking. I just watched them.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} knocked over something heavy {direction} during the night. A single, loud crash, then silence.
details: The kind of sound that makes you freeze mid-breath and listen. Then nothing for the rest of the night.
The guard found a toppled marker stone at first light. Pushed clean over.
reactions: One crash is almost worse than constant noise. You spend the rest of the night waiting for the next one.

entries.intro: I lay awake thinking about a conversation I had three days ago. Replaying it, editing my responses.
details: All the things I should have said, arriving perfectly formed at two in the morning.
By dawn I'd rehearsed a version of that conversation so good it'll never happen.
reactions: The night is when my brain decides to be clever. Too late every time.

entries.intro: There was a ring around the moon last night. A perfect, faint circle of light.
details: Pale and ghostly: like the moon had drawn a boundary around itself.
It lasted most of the night. By dawn it had faded to nothing.
reactions: When the moon wears a halo, the weather is thinking about changing.

statCheck.pass: The ring is ice crystals high in the air bending the moonlight. It means a warm front is coming: rain within a day or two, almost always.
statCheck.fail: A ring around the moon: some say it means rain, but I just thought it looked interesting.

entries.intro: I dreamed about water. Just standing in a stream, feeling the current push against my shins.
details: The water was cold and clear and I could see every stone on the bottom. I didn't want to leave.
I woke up to dry sheets and warm air, and for a moment the absence of water felt wrong.
reactions: Some dreams leave you homesick for a place that doesn't exist.

entries.intro: I heard a {wildlife_type} moving through the undergrowth near the house. Slow, deliberate steps.
details: Not sneaking: just walking at its own pace, with the confidence of something that isn't hunted here.
I watched from the window but the moonlight wasn't strong enough to see more than a shape.
reactions: There's something calming about animals that go about their business without caring that you're there.

entries.intro: I slept so long my body was stiff when I finally got up. Every joint protesting the first movement.
details: My knees cracked when I stood. My shoulders needed a full stretch before they'd cooperate.
It took ten minutes of moving around before I felt like a person instead of a statue.
reactions: Too much sleep is almost as bad as too little. The body has opinions about both.

statCheck.pass: Oversleeping makes you groggy because the body cycles through deep sleep and light sleep. If you wake mid-cycle, the deep sleep chemicals are still in your blood and take time to clear.
statCheck.fail: Slept too long and felt worse for it. The body is contrary.

entries.intro: There was a brief, bright flash across the sky last night. A streak of light that burned out in a heartbeat.
details: I almost missed it: a blink-and-gone line of white that started at the top of the sky and ended nowhere.
If I'd been looking the other way I'd never have known.
reactions: Seeing a falling star feels like catching the sky doing something it didn't expect you to notice.

entries.intro: The cat brought something in through the window and dropped it on the bed. I felt it land near my feet.
details: I didn't check until morning: turns out it was a leaf, a very large and very important leaf, apparently.
The cat sat next to it looking proud. I thanked it because what else do you do.
reactions: Nighttime cat deliveries are either horrifying or hilarious, and you don't know which until sunrise.

entries.intro: I stepped outside to cool off and the night was warmer than the house. No escape from the heat.
details: The air outside was thick and still: not a breath of wind, not a hint of relief.
I stood there sweating in the dark and went back inside feeling betrayed.
reactions: When the night can't even offer cool air, you know the season has you cornered.

statCheck.pass: When the ground retains the day's heat, it warms the air above it after sundown. The house's stone walls release their stored heat slower: that's why inside stays warm while outside gets worse.
statCheck.fail: Hot inside, hot outside. No escape.

entries.intro: I watched the shadow of the fence creep across the yard as the moon crossed the sky. Slow as an hour hand.
details: At midnight the shadow was by the door. By dawn it had reached the far wall.
I sat there and tracked it moving: visible if you were patient, invisible if you blinked.
reactions: Watching shadows move is a sure sign of insomnia, but at least it's a peaceful one.

entries.intro: I had a nightmare about falling. Not from a height: just the ground opening up and me going down.
details: That stomach-dropping feeling that jerks you awake with your arms grabbing at nothing.
My hands were gripping the mattress when I came to. White-knuckled, like it could save me.
reactions: Falling dreams leave your body convinced of something your mind knows wasn't real.

entries.intro: The night air carried the smell of someone's cooking. Onions and something rich: a late stew, maybe.
details: Whoever was cooking at that hour had the whole street smelling like a kitchen.
My stomach woke up before the rest of me did. Just lying there, hungry, at midnight.
reactions: Smelling food you can't have in the middle of the night is a specific kind of torment.

statCheck.pass: Cooking smells travel farther at night because the cool, still air holds the scent low to the ground. During the day, rising warm air disperses it upward.
statCheck.fail: Someone was cooking late. My stomach noticed before I did.

entries.intro: I woke up to a mouse running across my chest. Light feet, surprisingly fast.
details: I felt the tiny pressure points of four feet crossing my ribs, left to right, and then it was gone.
I lay there very still, waiting to see if it would come back. It didn't.
reactions: A mouse on your chest is harmless but deeply unwelcome at three in the morning.

entries.intro: The stars were out early last night. Visible before the sky had even fully darkened.
details: The brightest ones appeared in a sky that was still blue at the edges: competing with the last light.
By the time the blue was gone, the sky was already full.
reactions: Stars arriving before night does always feels like they're eager.

entries.intro: I heard the {mob_type} {direction} testing the fence line again. Pushing, rattling, checking for weak spots.
details: Post by post, working along the line: you could hear each one get shoved and then released.
The guard said they do this every few nights. Methodical about it.
reactions: The fact that they check the fences like that is the part that keeps me awake.

statCheck.pass: They probe for weak points but rarely commit to breaching: if a fence holds the first two pushes, they move on. Reinforcing the weakest posts is more effective than building higher walls.
statCheck.fail: They tested the fence and it held. That's all I need to know.

entries.intro: I fell asleep in sunlight and woke in complete darkness. The disorientation was total.
details: For a moment I didn't know where I was, what time it was, or how long I'd been out.
The transition from afternoon nap to night darkness with no memory of the gap is deeply strange.
reactions: Sleeping through dusk feels like the day was stolen while I wasn't looking.

entries.intro: I woke up to the sound of hail on the roof. Sharp, rapid tapping like a thousand small stones.
details: Each piece hit the thatch with a distinct crack. The noise built from scattered taps to a solid roar.
It lasted ten minutes and then stopped as suddenly as it started.
reactions: Hail at night sounds like the sky is throwing things at your house.

statCheck.pass: Hail forms when rain gets pushed high enough to freeze, then falls and gets pushed back up again. Each trip adds a layer of ice, so the longer the storm lasts, the bigger the stones get.
statCheck.fail: Hail on the roof. Sounded bad, didn't seem to damage anything.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} startled me on the path home and I dropped my lantern. The glass cracked but the flame survived.
details: It darted out from behind a post and I jumped sideways. The lantern swung, hit the ground, and rolled.
I picked it up with shaking hands and walked the rest of the way faster.
reactions: My heart rate takes longer to recover from surprises than it used to.

entries.intro: I lay in bed listening to the house settle. Creaks and pops as the wood cooled down from the day.
details: Each noise comes from a different joint: the beam above the door, the corner post, the window frame.
I've heard them so many times I could map the house by its night sounds alone.
reactions: A house that creaks is a house that's lived in. The quiet ones are the ones to worry about.

statCheck.pass: Wood contracts as it cools, and the joints resist, so they pop. The louder the pop the tighter the joint: it's the silent gaps that mean something has worked loose.
statCheck.fail: The house creaks at night, always has. I'd worry if it stopped.

entries.intro: I left a candle burning and fell asleep watching the flame. Woke to a pool of cold wax and darkness.
details: The candle burned down to nothing while I slept. A whole evening's worth of light, gone.
The wax had run off the holder and frozen in a long drip down the table.
reactions: Falling asleep with a candle lit is wasteful and risky. I do it at least once a week.

statCheck.pass: A candle burns about four to five hours per inch of length. Once you know how tall it was when you lit it, the stub tells you how long you slept.
statCheck.fail: The candle burned out while I slept. Wasteful.

entries.intro: The moonlight through the window made a perfect rectangle on the floor. I watched it slide across the room.
details: It moved like something alive: slow, steady, inching from the wall to the foot of the bed.
When it reached the blanket it lit up the fabric like a pale, cold spotlight.
reactions: Moonlight indoors always feels like the outside is reaching in.

entries.intro: I woke up grinding my teeth so hard my jaw ached all morning.
details: The muscles on both sides of my face were tight as rope. Opening my mouth hurt.
I didn't even know I was doing it until the pain woke me.
reactions: My sleeping self carries tension my waking self doesn't even know about.

statCheck.pass: Teeth grinding usually means unfinished worry: the jaw muscles work through whatever the mind wouldn't let go of during the day. It's the body's way of chewing on a problem.
statCheck.fail: I grind my teeth in my sleep sometimes. Can't help it.

entries.intro: The night was so clear I could see the line where the sky met the hills. A razor-sharp edge of black against stars.
details: Every ridge was visible: a dark, jagged outline against the bright field of the sky.
It looked like a paper cutout: the land as a silhouette, the stars as the backdrop.
reactions: Clear nights give you back the shape of the world that daytime takes for granted.

entries.intro: I tried counting sheep to fall asleep. The sheep kept wandering off in different directions.
details: I'd get to twenty and then start worrying about whether the imaginary fence was high enough.
Eventually the sheep became a full flock and I was managing a farm instead of sleeping.
reactions: Counting sheep only works if your brain doesn't start problem-solving for the sheep.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} set something on fire {direction} last night. We could see the smoke by moonlight.
details: A plume of grey rising against the stars, drifting slowly. It burned for about an hour.
The guard said it looked like a brush pile: nothing structural, nothing important.
reactions: Knowing they make fires is something you learn to live with. You just hope they stay small.

statCheck.pass: They burn refuse and clear ground around camps. A short fire that dies on its own means housekeeping, not signaling.
statCheck.fail: Something burned and it stopped. That's all we know.

entries.intro: I realized at midnight that I'd left the washing on the line. Went out in my nightclothes to bring it in.
details: Standing in the yard pulling cold, damp shirts off the line in bare feet. Not my finest moment.
A neighbor's light flickered on and then off. I hope they didn't see me.
reactions: Midnight laundry retrieval: the chore you do when you remember the chore you forgot.

entries.intro: I heard a sound like singing far off last night. Faint, melodic, carried on the wind.
details: It drifted in and out: clear for a moment, then gone, then back. Too far to make out the words.
Could've been someone on a late walk, or the wind playing tricks on tired ears.
reactions: Music at a distance, at night, is one of those sounds that makes you stop everything and just listen.

statCheck.pass: Sound carries farther at night because cool air bends the waves downward: they follow the ground instead of spreading up into the sky. That singing could've been a mile away or more.
statCheck.fail: Singing in the distance. Could've been anything.

entries.intro: I woke up sweating from a dream I can't remember. Just the heat and the pounding pulse.
details: The pillow was soaked, the sheets were twisted, and my heart was going like I'd been running.
Whatever it was, my body remembers even if my mind doesn't.
reactions: A forgotten nightmare is a debt your body pays without telling you what it's for.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} was sleeping under the porch when I came out this morning. Curled up tight, completely at ease.
details: It didn't even flinch when the door opened. Just blinked once and tucked its head back in.
I stepped over it and went about my morning. It was still there an hour later.
reactions: Apparently my porch is comfortable. I'll take that as a compliment.

entries.intro: I woke up in the exact position I fell asleep in. Didn't move once the entire night.
details: Same arm under the pillow, same leg bent, same angle. Like I'd been frozen and thawed.
The side I slept on was stiff but the rest of me felt oddly refreshed.
reactions: Either that was the deepest sleep of my life or I briefly stopped being alive.

entries.intro: I could smell rain coming before I heard it. That mineral scent, sharp and clean, pushing ahead of the clouds.
details: A good five minutes between the smell arriving and the first drops hitting the roof.
I closed the window just in time. The rain came in hard right after.
reactions: The smell before rain is the only reliable weather forecast I trust.

statCheck.pass: That scent is the ground releasing its oils when the humidity spikes: the rain hasn't arrived yet but the moisture in the air pulls it out of dry soil and stone. You're smelling the earth getting ready.
statCheck.fail: I smelled rain before it came. Some noses are better than others at that.

entries.intro: I had a dream where everyone I know had a slightly different face. Close, but wrong.
details: Like someone had described each person from memory and rebuilt them with small mistakes.
I recognized them by voice and by how they moved, but the faces were just off enough to be unsettling.
reactions: Dreams get faces wrong more often than anything else. The feelings are always right, though.

entries.intro: I heard a branch snap outside and then nothing. Just the one crack, then silence.
details: Not a creak or a slow break: a clean, sharp snap, like something stepped on it with weight.
I waited by the window for ten minutes and saw nothing. The dark doesn't explain itself.
reactions: A single unexplained sound at night is worse than a hundred. At least a hundred tells you a story.

statCheck.pass: A branch that snaps clean at night usually means weight, not wind. Wind bends and creaks, but a sharp snap means something put pressure on it from above.
statCheck.fail: A branch snapped. Could be anything.

entries.intro: I watched the last embers of the fire fade out. Orange to red to grey, one by one.
details: Each coal held its color until it didn't: a slow surrender from glow to ash.
The room got darker in stages, losing its warmth in the same order.
reactions: Watching a fire die is melancholy in a way that's hard to explain. It's just coals.

entries.intro: I made the mistake of looking at the time when I woke up. Should've just gone back to sleep blind.
details: Once you know it's three hours until dawn, every remaining minute stretches. Ignorance was the better option.
I lay there calculating rest time like it was a budget. That never helps.
reactions: Knowing the time in the middle of the night is a trap disguised as information.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} went quiet {direction} around midnight. After hours of noise, just sudden silence.
details: The guard said the quiet is more concerning than the noise. Noise means they're settled.
We all listened harder for about an hour, then they started up again.
reactions: You get so used to their sounds that the absence of them is what keeps you up.

statCheck.pass: Sudden silence usually means something else entered their territory: a predator, a rival group, or just a patrol they're listening to. They go quiet to assess before reacting.
statCheck.fail: They went quiet, then started again. The guard seemed relieved when the noise came back.

entries.intro: I woke up warm and comfortable and spent twenty minutes not moving because it was too perfect to break.
details: The blanket was at the right weight, the pillow was at the right angle, the room was the right temperature.
I lay there knowing I'd have to get up and destroy the whole arrangement eventually.
reactions: Perfect comfort in bed is a trap because every second you enjoy it brings you closer to being late.

entries.intro: I heard raindrops hitting leaves outside the window. Each drop a different note: plink, plop, tap.
details: Big leaves made a hollow sound, small leaves made a sharp one. The whole garden was an instrument.
It was irregular enough to be interesting and steady enough to lull me back toward sleep.
reactions: Rain on leaves is the kind of music nobody composes. It just happens.

entries.intro: I dreamed I was late for my own wedding, but I'm not getting married. The dream didn't care about the details.
details: Running down corridors in formal clothes that didn't fit, looking for a hall I couldn't find.
Everyone was waiting and I didn't even know who I was marrying.
reactions: Dreams borrow the shape of anxiety without bothering with the facts.

entries.intro: I noticed how different the air smells at three in the morning compared to midnight. Cooler, wetter, more raw.
details: Midnight air still has some warmth from the day. By three, all of it has bled away.
There's a mineral edge to the late-night air: stone and damp earth and nothing else.
reactions: The deep hours of the night have their own smell, and it's the smell of a world that forgot about people.

statCheck.pass: The coldest hour is usually an hour before dawn, not midnight. The ground keeps losing heat all night: by three or four, the accumulated loss hits bottom.
statCheck.fail: The air changes as the night goes on. That's all I know.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} bumped against the door in the dark. One solid thud, then silence.
details: I opened the door a crack and it was already gone: just a scuff mark and a faint smell.
Probably just lost its bearings in the dark. Not the first time it's happened.
reactions: Nighttime visitors who leave before you can see them are the most common kind.

entries.intro: I woke up hugging the pillow like it was a person. Arms wrapped around it, face pressed into it.
details: My sleeping self apparently needed something to hold onto. The pillow was the nearest option.
I let go and it had a dent in the exact shape of my face.
reactions: We're all more honest when we're asleep. The pillow knows my secrets.

entries.intro: The guard changed shifts at midnight and the new one has a heavier tread. I can always tell who's on.
details: The first one walks light: barely a sound. The second one sounds like a loaded cart.
Same route, same pace, completely different weight on the ground.
reactions: I've memorized the guards by their footsteps. I couldn't pick them out by face in a crowd.

statCheck.pass: You learn a lot about a guard by how they walk: a heavy tread means confidence or fatigue, and a light one means they're listening.
statCheck.fail: Different guards, different footsteps. That's all I notice.

entries.intro: I smelled woodsmoke at two in the morning. Someone was up late, stoking their fire.
details: The smoke drifted low in the still air: you could trace it by smell alone, one house to the next.
It mixed with the damp night and became something almost pleasant.
reactions: Woodsmoke at night means someone else can't sleep either. There's comfort in that.

statCheck.pass: Smoke hangs low on still nights because there's no rising air to carry it up. A low blanket of smoke means the air is stable: no storms coming.
statCheck.fail: Woodsmoke in the air. Someone was up late.

entries.intro: I slept through a thunderstorm and feel cheated. Everyone else is talking about how incredible it was.
details: Apparently there was lightning every few seconds for half an hour. I missed the entire show.
People are describing it like it was the event of the season, and I was in bed the whole time.
reactions: Sleeping through something everyone else experienced is a strange kind of loneliness.

entries.intro: The clouds broke just before dawn and flooded the room with moonlight. Like someone lit a lamp.
details: I was half-asleep and the sudden brightness pulled me awake. The room was glowing.
Everything had a silver edge to it: the table, the chair, the water jug. Beautiful and startling.
reactions: Unexpected moonlight at the end of a dark night feels like a reward for enduring it.

entries.intro: I heard something tapping on the wall outside. Regular, persistent: tap, tap, tap.
details: Went out with a light and found a loose bit of bark knocking against the house in the breeze.
Pulled it off and the tapping stopped. Solved in ten seconds after an hour of wondering.
reactions: Most night sounds have boring explanations. The boring ones are always the best.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} were howling {direction} last night. Long, drawn-out howls that echoed off the hills.
details: One would start and then two or three would join in: overlapping, rising, then fading together.
It went on for maybe ten minutes, then stopped all at once.
reactions: You can almost get used to the howling. Almost.

statCheck.pass: Group howling is a headcount: each voice is checking in with the others, confirming position and numbers. When they all stop at once, the count is done.
statCheck.fail: They howled for a while and then stopped. I don't know what it means.

entries.intro: I woke up freezing because I'd kicked the blanket to the foot of the bed. My toes found it first.
details: It was bunched up in a cold lump at the very bottom: as far from useful as it could possibly be.
I fished it back up with my feet, rearranged it, and was warm again in minutes.
reactions: My sleeping self sabotages my comfort on a nightly basis.

statCheck.pass: You kick blankets off when your body overheats in deep sleep: the blood vessels in your feet dilate to release heat, and the feet push the blankets away as a reflex.
statCheck.fail: Kicked the blanket off again. Can't control it.

entries.intro: The crickets were deafening last night. A solid wall of chirping from every direction.
details: Close your eyes and it was like being inside the sound: no direction, no source, just noise.
They all stopped at once when I opened the door, then started again the moment I went inside.
reactions: Crickets make more noise than seems possible for something so small.

statCheck.pass: The speed of their chirps follows the temperature: count the chirps in fifteen seconds and the number is close to the temperature in the shade. Faster means warmer.
statCheck.fail: Loud crickets. They do that every warm night.

entries.intro: I had a dream I was walking through my house, but every room had an extra door that shouldn't be there.
details: Each door opened into a room I'd never seen: same furniture, different arrangement.
I kept going through doors and the house kept getting bigger. It was fascinating and wrong.
reactions: Dreams love to take familiar places and break the rules of their geometry.

entries.intro: The warmest part of the bed last night was the spot where the cat had been lying all evening.
details: I curled around it like finding a warm stone in cold sand. The heat lingered for a surprisingly long time.
The cat watched me take its spot and left the room in silent protest.
reactions: I owe the cat a debt of warmth that it collects in stolen food.

entries.intro: I lay awake listening to two cats yowling at each other somewhere in the dark. That awful, rising screech.
details: Back and forth, each one louder than the last: a competition nobody wins.
It ended with a hiss, a thump, and silence. One of them ran past my window at full speed.
reactions: Cat fights at night sound worse than they are, but they sound terrible.

statCheck.pass: Cat fights are almost always territorial: they scream to establish dominance before fighting. The louder the yowl, the more likely one will back down without contact.
statCheck.fail: Cats screaming at each other. Nothing new.

entries.intro: I stepped outside and the sky was so bright with stars I could see my own shadow. A faint, grey outline on the ground.
details: Not moonlight: starlight alone, making shadow. I didn't think that was possible.
I moved my hand and the shadow moved with it. Barely visible but definitely real.
reactions: Casting a shadow by starlight felt like a privilege reserved for especially clear nights.

statCheck.pass: It takes a truly clear sky with no moon: the combined light of the brightest stars is just barely enough. Most people never notice because they carry a lamp outside.
statCheck.fail: I saw my shadow in starlight. Might've been my imagination, but I don't think so.

entries.intro: I woke up because my foot was hanging off the edge of the bed. The cold air found it immediately.
details: One bare foot dangling in the dark, getting colder by the second while the rest of me was warm.
I pulled it back under the blanket and tucked the edges tight. Not losing that foot again.
reactions: An exposed foot at night is a tiny betrayal by your sleeping body.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} left tracks across the windowsill this morning. Tiny muddy prints, perfectly spaced.
details: It must have walked the full length of the sill: a neat line of prints from one end to the other.
The window was shut, so it walked up, looked in, and walked away. Apparently I wasn't worth the effort.
reactions: I've been assessed and dismissed by something that left footprints the size of my thumbnail.

statCheck.pass: Windowsills attract visitors because they radiate heat from the room inside: on cold nights, the sill is the warmest outdoor surface near a house.
statCheck.fail: Tracks on the sill. Something visited overnight.

entries.intro: The wind changed direction three times during the night. I could track it by the chimney sound.
details: North wind whistles, east wind moans, south wind barely makes a sound. Three different voices before dawn.
The last shift brought warmer air: I could feel the room temperature change in my sleep.
reactions: A chimney is the most honest weathervane. It tells you exactly what the wind is doing.

statCheck.pass: Multiple wind shifts in one night usually mean a front is passing overhead. The sequence of directions tells you which way the weather system is moving.
statCheck.fail: The wind shifted around. Different sound, different feel each time.

entries.intro: I dreamed I was back at work, doing the exact same tasks I'd done all day. No escape, even in sleep.
details: The same motions, the same routine, the same tired arms. My dream couldn't even bother to be creative.
Woke up feeling like I'd worked a double shift.
reactions: Dreaming about work is the cruelest trick the sleeping mind plays.

entries.intro: I found a snail climbing the inside of the window this morning. It must have come in overnight.
details: A silver trail from the sill to about halfway up the glass: steady progress, no detours.
I put it outside on a leaf and it immediately started climbing back toward the house.
reactions: That snail has more determination about where it wants to be than I do.

entries.intro: The first rooster went off way too early. Still pitch dark, not even a hint of dawn.
details: I swear that bird starts an hour earlier every week. No sense of timing at all.
Every other rooster in the area took the cue and started up. A chain reaction of premature crowing.
reactions: A rooster with a bad sense of timing punishes the entire neighborhood.

statCheck.pass: Roosters respond to any light, not just dawn. A torch, a bright moon, even a lamp in a window: any of them can set off the first crow, and then they all follow.
statCheck.fail: The rooster crowed too early, again. Nothing new.

entries.intro: I sat outside and counted satellites. Tiny points of light moving in straight lines across the stars.
details: Some moved fast, some crawled. One crossed the whole sky in about two minutes.
I counted seven before my neck got sore from looking up.
reactions: Something about watching things move through a still sky is deeply satisfying.

entries.intro: My partner talked in their sleep last night. Full sentences, completely serious, about absolutely nothing.
details: Something about needing more rope and a complaint about the color of a boat. Very urgent delivery.
I answered once and they said 'good' and went quiet. Apparently that resolved it.
reactions: Sleep conversations are the funniest and most baffling things you'll ever hear.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} were throwing rocks at each other {direction} last night. You could hear the impacts.
details: Clack, clack, clack: stone on stone, with pauses between like they were taking turns.
The guard said it might be a game. I said I didn't care as long as they weren't throwing them at us.
reactions: When your nightly concern is whether they're throwing rocks at each other or at your fence, you've adjusted.

entries.intro: I woke up from a dream where I could speak every language. Fluent, effortless, understanding everyone.
details: The feeling of comprehension was overwhelming: like a door opening into a room full of meaning.
Then I woke up monolingual and the door shut.
reactions: That dream left me feeling both enriched and cheated at the same time.

entries.intro: I discovered last night that if you lie very still, you can feel your own pulse in your fingertips.
details: Each beat a tiny throb: there, gone, there, gone. The whole body keeping time with itself.
I lay there counting beats for a while. Oddly calming.
reactions: You're never really alone at night. Your heartbeat keeps you company whether you ask it to or not.

statCheck.pass: The pulse in your fingertips is strongest when you're lying flat and still: the heart pushes blood to the extremities with nothing working against it. It's always there, just usually drowned out.
statCheck.fail: I could feel my pulse. Odd but calming.

entries.intro: I woke up to frost on the inside of the window. The glass was cold enough to freeze the condensation.
details: Feathery crystals spreading out from the edges, each one branching into smaller patterns.
I traced a line through the frost with my finger and my fingertip went numb.
reactions: Frost on the inside means the night won the argument with the house.

statCheck.pass: Inside frost means the air in the room cooled below freezing while you slept. The window is always the first surface to go: thinnest barrier between the warm and the cold.
statCheck.fail: Frost on the inside of the glass. Cold night.

entries.intro: I sat out on the step until my tea went cold, just watching the dark. Didn't even drink it.
details: Started warm, ended cold, and I barely noticed. The sky had all my attention.
When I finally took a sip it was room temperature and I drank it anyway.
reactions: Cold tea and a clear sky: proof that I've been out there long enough.

statCheck.pass: Tea cools to room temperature in about forty minutes on a still night. If it was warm when I sat down and cold when I noticed, that tells me how long I was staring at the sky.
statCheck.fail: The tea went cold. I wasn't paying attention.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} was making a nest somewhere in the eaves. I could hear the quiet rustling above me all night.
details: Tiny scraping sounds and the soft drag of material being arranged. Industrious, even in the dark.
By morning it had gone quiet, and I assume the nest is now complete.
reactions: I've got a tenant in the roof. I wasn't consulted, but I'm not evicting anyone who works that hard.

entries.intro: I had a dream that was just a color. No people, no places: just a deep, warm red that filled everything.
details: Not an image of red: the experience of it. Like being inside a feeling that had no other content.
I woke up calm, which is unusual for me.
reactions: The simplest dreams are sometimes the most restful. My mind took the night off.

entries.intro: I woke up to a moth batting against the last of the candlelight. Round and round in smaller circles.
details: Its wings made a soft ticking against the glass chimney: insistent, relentless.
When the candle finally died, the moth vanished. Its whole world switched off.
reactions: Watching a moth chase a flame is the most committed pursuit I've ever witnessed.

statCheck.pass: Moths navigate by the moon, holding it at a constant angle. A candle close by overwhelms that instinct and they spiral inward because they keep correcting toward the brighter light.
statCheck.fail: A moth and a candle. The moth never learns.

entries.intro: The first bird of the morning woke me. One single note, repeated: testing whether dawn was real.
details: It sang alone for a full five minutes before any others joined. A cautious, solitary voice.
Then a second bird answered, and within moments the whole chorus was running.
reactions: That first bird takes a risk every morning: singing before it can see.

entries.intro: I tried sleeping on the floor last night because the bed felt too soft. The floor was worse.
details: Hard, cold, and every bone in my body found a new complaint. I lasted about forty minutes.
Crawled back into the too-soft bed and it felt like a cloud by comparison.
reactions: Sometimes you have to try the wrong thing to appreciate the right one.

statCheck.pass: The floor feels harder because your weight concentrates on fewer pressure points without a mattress. A bed distributes load across the whole body: the floor picks favorites, and they're always the ones that hurt.
statCheck.fail: The floor was hard. That's about all I learned.

entries.intro: I heard the well rope creaking in the wind all night. A slow, rhythmic groaning.
details: The bucket swinging on the end of it, back and forth, making the pulley squeak with each pass.
By morning the sound was so familiar I barely noticed it. But the first creak had woken me.
reactions: Night amplifies the smallest mechanical sounds into something that fills the whole house.

statCheck.pass: Well ropes creak in the wind because the hemp fibers swell with damp night air and then dry out as the wind passes. The swelling and shrinking makes the fibers rub against each other.
statCheck.fail: The rope creaked. Wind does that.

entries.intro: The moon set before I went to bed, and the darkness after was total. Like someone had drawn a curtain.
details: Not dim: gone. The kind of dark where your hand in front of your face is invisible.
I moved by memory: six steps to the bed, turn left, two more steps, sit.
reactions: True dark teaches you how well you know a room without your eyes.

statCheck.pass: Your eyes take about twenty minutes to fully adjust to moonless dark. Most people give up and light something in the first two: that's why true dark feels rare even though it happens every night.
statCheck.fail: No moon, no light. Dark is dark.

entries.intro: I found mushrooms growing in a damp corner of the bedroom this morning. Overnight, just like that.
details: Tiny white caps, barely taller than my thumb, clustered where the wall meets the floor.
I scraped them off but the damp is still there. They'll be back.
reactions: When your bedroom grows its own food overnight, that's a moisture problem.

statCheck.pass: Overnight mushrooms mean the spores have been there for days: they only fruit when the moisture is right. The mushrooms are the symptom: the damp in the wall is what needs fixing.
statCheck.fail: Mushrooms in the bedroom. I scraped them off.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} left footprints near the fence line {direction} overnight. Deep ones: they were moving with weight.
details: The guard measured the stride. Long steps, evenly spaced: they were in no particular hurry.
The tracks went along the fence and then away. They came close and chose not to cross.
reactions: Footprints in the morning are the diary of a night I slept through.

statCheck.pass: Deep prints with long strides mean loaded: carrying supplies or moving equipment. Unladen they leave lighter marks and shorter gaps.
statCheck.fail: Tracks by the fence: they came and went, and that's all we know.

entries.intro: I woke up convinced it was morning, got dressed, and opened the door to a sky full of stars. Not even close.
details: Fully clothed, boots on, ready for the day: at what turned out to be around one in the morning.
I stood in the doorway for a moment, sighed, and went back to undress.
reactions: Getting fully dressed for a dawn that's four hours away is a special kind of defeat.

entries.intro: I leaned out the window and felt the night dew already forming on the sill. Cool and slick under my palms.
details: The air was heavy with moisture: you could feel it on your skin like a fine mist.
Breathing it in felt like drinking: thick, wet air that coated your lungs.
reactions: Dew nights are the quiet ones. Heavy air muffles everything.

entries.intro: I dreamed about someone I haven't thought of in years. Completely vivid, completely unexpected.
details: Their voice, their gestures, the way they laughed: all stored somewhere I didn't know about.
When I woke up, the memory settled back into whatever drawer it came from.
reactions: The mind keeps files on people you've forgotten, and opens them without permission.

statCheck.pass: I think dreams pull from a deeper shelf than waking memory. People you haven't thought of in a decade are still in there, complete, waiting for a quiet night to surface.
statCheck.fail: I dreamed about someone from long ago. The mind does what it wants at night.

entries.intro: I watched a bat circling the torch outside for ten minutes. Tight, fast loops, never landing.
details: It was hunting the insects the light attracted: swooping, catching, banking, coming back for more.
Each pass was closer to the flame than I'd have dared. No fear at all.
reactions: That bat had the best night of its life. I had a show.

statCheck.pass: Bats can eat hundreds of insects in a single night, and a good torch draws enough moths and beetles to keep one fed for hours. Wherever there's a light, there's a bat making a living.
statCheck.fail: A bat chasing insects around the torch. Good entertainment.

entries.intro: I lay awake because my mind decided to catalog every mistake I've made this month. One by one, in detail.
details: Things said wrong, tasks done badly, promises forgotten: all queued up and presented in order.
Each one came with a full replay and a sharp pang. Very thorough, my brain.
reactions: The dark is when the mind settles its accounts, and the interest rate is terrible.

entries.intro: I noticed a cobweb in the corner catching the moonlight. It looked like a piece of lace hung in the air.
details: Each strand was a fine silver thread, trembling slightly in a draft I couldn't feel.
The spider that built it was nowhere in sight: just the web, glowing, abandoned or asleep.
reactions: Cobwebs are uglier by day and beautiful by night. Same web, different light.

entries.intro: I went to bed with a tune stuck in my head and woke up with the same tune. It survived the whole night.
details: Six hours of sleep and the song didn't budge. Same three bars, looping endlessly.
I hummed it while making breakfast and finally: mercifully, it left.
reactions: A song that survives a full night's sleep has earned my grudging respect.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} {direction} were banging on hollow logs again last night. That deep, resonant thumping.
details: You could feel it in your chest more than hear it: a vibration that came up through the ground.
It stopped when the rain started. Apparently they don't drum in the wet.
reactions: At least rain shuts them up. Something to be grateful for.

entries.intro: I woke up with my hands clasped behind my head like I'd been relaxing on purpose. Comfortable, actually.
details: My sleeping self apparently found the ideal position and stayed there. Arms linked, elbows out, completely at ease.
I tried to fall back asleep in the same pose but it wasn't the same. You can't recreate accidental comfort.
reactions: The best sleeping positions are the ones your body finds without your help.

entries.intro: There was a streak of orange near the horizon before dawn. Not sunrise: too early and too narrow.
details: A thin band of color between the black land and the dark sky: like a crack in the night leaking light.
It lasted twenty minutes and then the real dawn overtook it.
reactions: That first false light is the night admitting it's almost done.

entries.intro: I woke up to the smell of the hearth. Ash and old smoke: the ghost of yesterday's fire.
details: The scent is stronger at night when nothing else is in the air to compete with it.
Not unpleasant: just a reminder that the fire is out and the room is cooling.
reactions: Cold ash has a honesty to it. The day is done and nothing is pretending otherwise.

entries.intro: I had a dream where I was swimming in warm water. No destination: just floating, weightless, at peace.
details: The water held me up without effort. No current, no shore, just warmth and suspension.
I woke up and the bed felt heavy. Gravity reasserting itself after the dream let me go.
reactions: Floating dreams are the ones I wish I could choose. They leave you rested.

entries.intro: I heard a door slam somewhere in the dark. Once, hard, and then nothing.
details: Could have been the wind or could have been someone angry. No way to tell from bed.
I waited for a second slam that never came.
reactions: A single door slam at night tells a story you'll never hear the rest of.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} was silhouetted on the roof opposite when I looked out the window. Just sitting there against the sky.
details: A dark shape on the ridgeline, perfectly still: it could've been a weathervane if I didn't know better.
I watched it for five minutes and it never moved. Then I blinked and it was gone.
reactions: Night gives animals a dignity that daylight takes away. Everything looks composed in silhouette.

entries.intro: I couldn't sleep because my mind kept planning tomorrow. Tasks, conversations, routes: all of it on a loop.
details: I organized the whole day three different ways and still wasn't satisfied with any of them.
By the time I stopped planning, half the night was gone.
reactions: The mind rehearses at night what the body does during the day. Unfortunately, it charges the same hours.

entries.intro: The rain on the roof started gentle and ended heavy. A slow build that took most of the night.
details: It started as a whisper and grew into a drumroll. Each stage lasted about an hour.
By dawn it was a solid downpour: the kind that makes you pull the blanket up and refuse to move.
reactions: A rain that builds slowly is the best sleeping weather. It teaches you to stay.

statCheck.pass: Rain that builds instead of starting heavy means the storm is approaching, not overhead. Heavy-first storms are directly above: building storms are still moving toward you.
statCheck.fail: The rain got heavier through the night. Just the weather doing what it does.

entries.intro: I woke up on the wrong side of the bed. Literally: head where my feet should be, facing the wall.
details: No memory of turning, no explanation: just a full rotation sometime during the night.
I lay there trying to figure out the mechanics. My sleeping self is more athletic than my waking one.
reactions: Waking up backwards is the body's way of keeping life interesting.

entries.intro: I heard a cart on the road well after midnight. Wheels creaking, horse plodding, someone traveling late.
details: It moved slowly: the kind of pace that says the driver is too tired to push the horse.
The sound faded gradually over a long time. They were heading somewhere with no urgency.
reactions: A late traveler on the road always makes you wonder about the journey and never about the destination.

statCheck.pass: Night travelers usually prefer the cool hours to save the horse. A loaded cart in summer heat needs to move at night or risk overheating the animal.
statCheck.fail: Someone on the road late. Not my business where they're going.

entries.intro: I slept with a headache and woke up without one. The night took it from me like a gift.
details: I went to bed with my temples throbbing and my eyes aching. Morning came and it was just gone.
No remedy, no treatment: just sleep doing what nothing else could.
reactions: Sleep fixes things that nothing else can touch. I'll never understand how.

statCheck.pass: Sleep resets the pain signals: the nerves that fire during a headache fatigue and go quiet during deep rest. Most headaches that survive the day won't survive a full night's sleep.
statCheck.fail: I slept it off. Headaches do that sometimes.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} threw something against the fence {direction} that shattered on impact. Glass or pottery.
details: A sharp, splintering crash that cut through the quiet. The guard found shards at the base of the post.
No way to know what it was or why they threw it. Could be testing, could be amusement.
reactions: They throw things and we clean up. The relationship, in summary.

entries.intro: I noticed the shadow of the chimney falling across the yard like a sundial in reverse. A moon-dial.
details: It crept along the ground, marking the moon's path the same way a shadow marks the sun's.
If I'd been patient enough, I could've tracked the whole arc.
reactions: The moon casts its own clock on the ground. You just need insomnia to read it.

entries.intro: I dreamed about falling rain, and when I woke up it was actually raining. The dream was a forecast.
details: Same sound, same rhythm: the dream blended into reality so smoothly I couldn't find the seam.
For a moment I wasn't sure which side I was on: still dreaming or finally awake.
reactions: When the dream matches the world, waking up is the strangest transition.

entries.intro: I tried sleeping with earplugs last night. Blocked the outside noise and amplified everything inside my own head.
details: My breathing sounded huge, my pulse sounded like footsteps, and every swallow was an event.
Pulled them out after twenty minutes and the normal night noise felt like silence by comparison.
reactions: Earplugs don't bring silence: they bring a different, more personal kind of noise.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} was drinking from the water trough before dawn. I could hear the quiet lapping from my window.
details: Delicate, unhurried sips: like it had all the time in the world and knew it.
When it finished it shook its head once and walked off into the grey light.
reactions: Sharing water with the wildlife was never the plan, but I don't mind the company.

entries.intro: The night was so warm the wax candle on the table bent. Didn't melt: just softened and leaned.
details: I found it this morning listing to one side like a tired guard at the end of his shift.
Still usable, but crooked. The heat reshaped it overnight.
reactions: When your candles wilt, the night is too warm for anything upright.

statCheck.pass: Beeswax holds its shape better in heat than tallow does: if your candles bend on warm nights, they're tallow. Beeswax costs more but it'll stand up to a summer night.
statCheck.fail: The candle bent. Hot night.

entries.intro: I woke up to a child crying somewhere nearby. Not mine: a neighbor's, I think.
details: A thin, tired cry that went on for a while, then settled into hiccupping quiet.
I felt the sympathy in my chest: that parental instinct that fires whether it's your child or not.
reactions: A child crying at night makes the whole street lie awake, even if they won't admit it.

statCheck.pass: A child's crying carries further at night because the high pitch cuts through still air. It's the frequency: lower sounds get absorbed, but a child's cry is sharp enough to reach every house on the street.
statCheck.fail: A child was crying. I hope they settled.

entries.intro: The sky had layers of cloud at different heights last night. Each one moving in a different direction.
details: The low ones drifted left while the high ones went right: two currents in the same air, ignoring each other.
It made the sky look alive: not one thing moving, but many.
reactions: Looking up at night and seeing the sky move in two directions at once makes you feel the scale of the air.

statCheck.pass: Clouds at different heights catch different wind layers, and the direction they're moving tells you what the weather is doing at each level. If they're going opposite ways, the weather is about to argue with itself.
statCheck.fail: Clouds going different directions. I just watched and didn't try to understand.

entries.intro: I woke up because I heard my own name. Clear as day, spoken right beside me: nobody there.
details: A voice I couldn't place, speaking my name like they were trying to get my attention.
I sat up fast and the room was empty. Just the dark and my hammering heart.
reactions: Hearing your name in an empty room is the kind of thing you don't mention to people.

statCheck.pass: The brain sometimes fires recognition patterns on the edge of sleep: familiar sounds from random noise. Your name is the most deeply wired word you know, so it's the most likely hallucination.
statCheck.fail: I heard something, probably nothing. I'm not going to think too hard about it.

entries.intro: I found the perfect sleeping temperature last night. Not too warm, not too cold: one blanket, window cracked.
details: The air coming in was just cool enough to balance the blanket's warmth. An equilibrium.
I didn't wake once. The formula was: one blanket, two-inch gap in the window, no socks.
reactions: I should write down the exact conditions. I'll chase this combination for months.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} got tangled in the clothesline overnight. Found it this morning hanging from a shirt sleeve.
details: Not hurt: just confused, twisting slowly, wrapped in linen with one leg hooked on the rope.
I untangled it carefully and it bolted the second it was free. No gratitude.
reactions: My clothesline is apparently an obstacle course that the local wildlife has not mastered.

entries.intro: I lay awake worrying about nothing specific. Just a general, shapeless anxiety that wouldn't land on a cause.
details: No particular fear, no particular problem: just a feeling of wrongness with no address.
I tried to name it and couldn't. The worry outlasted my patience and eventually I just fell asleep.
reactions: The worries without names are the worst kind. You can't argue with something that won't identify itself.

entries.intro: The sunset last night was still glowing after I went to bed. A deep red line at the horizon that wouldn't give up.
details: I watched it from the pillow: a thin seam of color that held on for an hour past its time.
It faded so slowly I couldn't tell when it ended and the dark began.
reactions: Long sunsets are the sky's way of not quite being ready for the night either.

statCheck.pass: The longest twilights happen when the sun sets at a shallow angle: it grazes below the horizon slowly instead of dropping straight down. The closer to midsummer, the longer the glow holds.
statCheck.fail: A long sunset. Nice to watch.

entries.intro: I woke up to the sound of my own snoring. Loud enough to startle myself awake.
details: One great rattling snore that cut off the instant I woke, like evidence being destroyed.
I lay there embarrassed in the dark, even though no one heard it.
reactions: Snoring yourself awake is a betrayal of trust between the sleeping self and the waking one.
