statCheck.pass: Snoring happens when the throat muscles relax and the airway narrows: sleeping on your back is the worst for it because gravity pulls the tongue down. Side sleeping usually fixes it.
statCheck.fail: I snored myself awake. Embarrassing, but harmless.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} were scraping tools against stone {direction} in the small hours. A grinding, metallic noise.
details: Rhythmic and steady: the sound of sharpening or shaping. It went on for a long time.
The guard made a note of it. Said it's something they do every few nights.
reactions: Hearing them work on tools is one of those sounds you file away and try not to think about.

statCheck.pass: Tool maintenance is routine for them: they sharpen blades, repair hafts, and reshape stone edges. The fact that they maintain their equipment means they're settled in, not passing through.
statCheck.fail: They were grinding something on stone. I didn't like the sound.

entries.intro: I sat outside and the only sound was my own breathing. The whole world had gone still.
details: No wind, no animals, no voices: just me and the dark and the sound of air moving in and out.
It felt less like quiet and more like the world was listening.
reactions: Perfect stillness is rare enough to feel like a secret you stumbled into.

entries.intro: I dreamed I was walking somewhere familiar, but every landmark was in the wrong place.
details: The road I knew, the buildings I recognized, but all rearranged: like someone had shuffled a deck of places.
I kept walking because dream logic said it was fine. Waking logic strongly disagrees.
reactions: Dreams take the places you know and remix them without permission.

entries.intro: My eyes adjusted to the dark last night and I was surprised how much I could see. Shapes, outlines, distances.
details: It took about ten minutes, but then the room went from black to grey and I could navigate without a lamp.
Even the yard was visible: fences, the path, the shape of the roof next door.
reactions: We reach for lights so fast we forget our eyes can handle the dark on their own.

statCheck.pass: Full dark adaptation takes about twenty minutes: the first five give you shapes, and the next fifteen give you detail. Most people give up and light a candle after two.
statCheck.fail: My eyes adjusted and I could see more than I expected. Takes patience.

entries.intro: I heard two guards talking at the corner in low voices. Just a murmur, no words, but the tone was relaxed.
details: One laughed quietly and the other shushed him. A normal night, apparently.
Their voices drifted in through the window like a distant, friendly hum.
reactions: Hearing the guard laugh means things are quiet. That's all the report I need.

entries.intro: I got a leg cramp in my sleep so bad it woke me up shouting.
details: The calf muscle locked solid: a knot of pain that seized the whole lower leg.
I had to stand on it and push against the floor until it slowly, grudgingly released.
reactions: A night cramp is a betrayal by your own body at the worst possible time.

statCheck.pass: Night cramps usually mean you're low on water or salt: the muscles don't have enough of either to relax properly. A cup of water before bed prevents most of them.
statCheck.fail: A cramp in my sleep. It hurt, it stopped, I went back to bed.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} was calling from somewhere high up last night. A clear, carrying sound that cut through everything.
details: It echoed off the buildings and made it impossible to tell the direction. The sound just was, everywhere.
It called three times, then went silent. Like it said what it needed to and was done.
reactions: Some animal calls at night feel like they're meant for you, even though they're not.

entries.intro: I tried leaving a window open for fresh air and a beetle the size of my thumb flew in and hit the wall.
details: A heavy, buzzing impact: thwack, then silence, then buzzing again as it took off for another pass.
I chased it around the room with a cup for five minutes before it found the window again.
reactions: Fresh air comes with passengers. I should've expected that.

statCheck.pass: Beetles fly toward light because they navigate by the moon, trying to keep the brightest point at a fixed angle. A lamp confuses the system and they spiral inward, same trick that catches moths.
statCheck.fail: A beetle flew in. Took forever to get it out.

entries.intro: The last thing I saw before sleep was the moon through the curtain. Bright enough to glow through the fabric.
details: The curtain turned into a soft, luminous rectangle: the moon's shape blurred but its light was clear.
I fell asleep facing that glow. It was like a second, gentler lamp.
reactions: Moonlight through fabric is the softest light there is. Enough to see by, not enough to keep you up.

entries.intro: I woke up to a puddle on the floor under the window. Rain had come in through the gap I forgot to close.
details: Stepped right in it on the way to get water. Bare foot, cold puddle, no warning.
Mopped it up with a shirt and closed the window. The damage was done.
reactions: An open window in the rain is a lesson I keep learning and keep forgetting.

statCheck.pass: Rain comes through a gap at an angle: the wind pushes it sideways, so even a small opening can let in a surprising amount of water. A one-inch gap in a crosswind might as well be an open window.
statCheck.fail: Rain got in through the window. My own fault.

entries.intro: I lay awake counting the drips from the eaves. Seventeen per minute: I counted twice to be sure.
details: Each drop hit the same spot on the stone below. A tiny, precise percussion that refused to vary.
I tried to ignore it, but once you've counted, you can't stop counting.
reactions: A dripping eave at night turns you into a mathematician against your will.

entries.intro: I woke up with the blanket on the floor and no memory of kicking it off. Must've happened hours ago.
details: My feet were cold, my arms were cold, but apparently my sleeping self preferred freedom over warmth.
I pulled it back up and was asleep again in seconds. The cold hadn't fully registered yet.
reactions: The sleeping body makes choices the waking body would never approve.

entries.intro: There was a {wildlife_type} on the fence post when I went out to check the sky. Just perched there, watching the dark.
details: It didn't startle when I opened the door. Just turned its head slightly, acknowledged me, and went back to watching.
We stood there together for a few minutes, both looking at nothing in particular.
reactions: Nice to know I'm not the only one who sits up at night for no reason.

entries.intro: I dreamed I was eating a meal but couldn't taste anything. Every bite was blank.
details: The food looked right, the texture was right, but the flavor was simply absent. Like chewing warm nothing.
I woke up genuinely hungry, which I suppose was the point.
reactions: A dream that makes you hungry is the cruelest kind of advertisement.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} were singing something {direction} last night. Not words I could make out: just a low, droning chant.
details: It rose and fell in waves, almost musical if you didn't think too hard about who was singing.
Went on for about an hour and then stopped all at once, as if someone gave a signal.
reactions: Hearing them sing is stranger than hearing them shout. Shouting I understand.

statCheck.pass: The chanting usually happens around the same time each week. It's rhythmic and structured, not random noise: they're keeping some kind of schedule with it.
statCheck.fail: They were making noise. Not much else to say.

entries.intro: I watched a shooting star last night. A bright scratch across the sky that lasted half a breath.
details: It moved faster than anything I've seen on the ground: a white line from one horizon to the other.
I blinked and the afterimage was still there, burned into my eyes.
reactions: A shooting star rewards you for looking up at the right moment and punishes you for blinking.

entries.intro: My pillow was damp when I woke up. Not sweat: just the humidity settling on everything overnight.
details: The sheets, the pillow, even the wooden headboard felt clammy. Like the air had sweated.
I turned the pillow over and the other side was the same. No escape.
reactions: Waking up damp in a dry bed is the most confusing kind of discomfort.

statCheck.pass: Humidity condenses on the coldest surfaces first: skin, linen, anything that radiates heat slowly. A damp pillow means the room temperature dropped below the dew point while you slept.
statCheck.fail: Everything was damp. Humid night, I suppose.

entries.intro: I noticed the constellation that looks like a ladle was directly overhead last night. Brighter than usual.
details: The stars at the corners were crisp and distinct: no haze, no clouds, just clean points of light.
I used to stare at it as a child, convinced it was meant for me personally.
reactions: Some constellations feel like old friends. You look up and there they are, exactly where you left them.

statCheck.pass: The brightness depends on how clear the air is, not the stars themselves. On dry, windless nights the atmosphere thins out and stops scattering the light: same stars, less interference.
statCheck.fail: The stars were bright. Clear night, I suppose.

entries.intro: I woke up mid-sentence, talking to someone who wasn't there. My own voice startled me more than the dream.
details: I was saying something earnest, mid-argument by the sound of it, and the room was empty.
Lay there with my heart going fast, embarrassed by a conversation I can't remember.
reactions: Talking in your sleep means your mouth has its own schedule and doesn't consult you.

entries.intro: The frogs started up around midnight. A thick, overlapping chorus from every direction.
details: One would call and a dozen would answer, then silence, then the whole thing again.
There's a rhythm to it that's almost predictable: call, chorus, pause, repeat.
reactions: Frogs arguing at midnight is either soothing or maddening, depending on how tired you are.

statCheck.pass: The chorus gets louder when the air is damp: the moisture carries the sound further and the frogs know it. A loud frog night means rain is close.
statCheck.fail: Frogs were noisy. That's what frogs do.

entries.intro: I kept waking up every hour like I had an appointment to keep. Eyes open, check the window, close them again.
details: Each time the darkness had shifted a little: the shadows in different places, the moon further along.
By morning I'd seen the full rotation of the night in installments.
reactions: Sleeping in one-hour shifts is worse than not sleeping at all. You get the fatigue with none of the rest.

statCheck.pass: Waking every hour usually means your body is stuck in light sleep cycles and never drops deep enough. Stress or noise or temperature can all hold you at the surface.
statCheck.fail: I kept waking up. Bad night, that's all.

entries.intro: I stepped outside to cool off and the grass was silver with frost. Beautiful, and it's not even winter.
details: Each blade was outlined in white, glinting under the moon like someone had dusted the yard with sugar.
My bare feet lasted about three seconds before I retreated back to the doorstep.
reactions: Frost at night before winter is the cold testing the boundaries.

statCheck.pass: Ground frost can form on clear nights even when the air is above freezing. The ground loses heat faster than the air, and dew freezes on the surface while everything a foot up stays liquid.
statCheck.fail: Frost on the grass. Cold night.

entries.intro: I heard a single, distant bell ring once in the middle of the night. No follow-up, no explanation.
details: One clear tone that came from somewhere far off and then nothing. Not an alarm: just one ring.
I waited for a second one for at least ten minutes before giving up.
reactions: A bell that rings once at night is a question with no answer.

entries.intro: I had a nightmare where I was late for something but didn't know what. Just the dread of lateness, no destination.
details: I was running through familiar streets but every turn led somewhere wrong. The urgency never let up.
Woke up sweating with my heart pounding, then realized it was the middle of the night and nothing was due.
reactions: Being late in a dream is the purest form of anxiety: all the panic, none of the reason.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} lit a fire {direction} last night. I could see the orange glow from my window.
details: It flickered for hours: a small, steady light that didn't move. A campfire, probably.
The glow died down before dawn. Whatever they were doing, they finished.
reactions: Seeing their fire from my window makes the distance feel smaller than it is.

statCheck.pass: A fire that burns steady and low for hours is a cooking fire or a warming fire: short fuel, controlled. Signal fires burn high and bright, but this was domestic.
statCheck.fail: They had a fire going. I could see the glow.

entries.intro: I lay in bed listening to a cricket that had gotten inside the house. Loud, insistent, and somewhere behind the wall.
details: The chirping came in bursts: ten seconds on, five seconds off, perfectly regular.
I tried to find it but the sound seemed to come from everywhere at once.
reactions: A cricket indoors is a tiny roommate who pays no rent and keeps terrible hours.

statCheck.pass: Crickets chirp faster when it's warm: count the chirps in fifteen seconds, add the number to a rough base, and you have a decent guess at the temperature. The warmer the night, the faster they go.
statCheck.fail: A cricket in the wall. Loud, persistent, and impossible to find.

entries.intro: I watched my breath cloud in the lamplight when I got up for water. The cold had crept in while I slept.
details: Each exhale made a little ghost that drifted upward and dissolved. The air in the room had teeth.
The water from the jug was ice-cold. It woke me up more than the chill.
reactions: Seeing your breath indoors means the fire went out and the night won.

statCheck.pass: You see your breath when the air drops below a certain point and your lungs are still warm enough to make steam. If you can see it indoors, the room is near freezing.
statCheck.fail: Cold enough to see my breath. The fire was dead.

entries.intro: I couldn't sleep so I reorganized the shelf by the bed. Everything is now in a different wrong order.
details: Moved things around for half an hour by lamplight. Felt productive at the time.
This morning I can't find anything. Nighttime organization has its own logic that doesn't survive sunrise.
reactions: Doing chores at midnight feels like solving problems. In the morning, you've just moved the mess.

entries.intro: The moon was behind clouds all night, but every now and then it broke through. Brief, silver moments.
details: Each break lasted maybe thirty seconds: enough to light the yard, then gone again.
It felt like the moon was checking on things and then pulling the curtain back.
reactions: Moonlight through gaps in the clouds is the night's version of a flickering lamp.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} was nosing around the compost pile in the dark. I heard it before I saw it.
details: Soft rustling, a few quiet scrapes, and then the silhouette of it pulling at scraps.
It ate its fill and left without drama. Polite, as night visitors go.
reactions: I can't begrudge an animal for eating what I threw away.

entries.intro: I slept so deeply I didn't move once. Woke up in the exact position I fell asleep in.
details: Same arm under the pillow, same leg bent, same crease in the blanket. Like I'd been preserved.
The only difference was six hours had passed and my arm was completely numb.
reactions: The deepest sleep is the one your body commits to fully and regrets in the morning.

entries.intro: I tried reading by candlelight until I got drowsy. Three pages in, the words started swimming.
details: My eyes would track a line, reach the end, and realize they'd absorbed nothing. The same sentence, four times.
The candle was guttering by the time I gave up. Fell asleep with the book on my chest.
reactions: Reading yourself to sleep is a negotiation between your mind and your eyelids. The eyelids always win.

statCheck.pass: Reading by low light makes your pupils dilate fully, and dilated pupils trigger the same relaxation response as darkness. The book doesn't bore you to sleep: the dim light does.
statCheck.fail: Reading made me sleepy. The usual effect.

entries.intro: I heard geese overhead in the dark. Honking, a whole flock, moving fast.
details: Couldn't see them, but the sound crossed the sky from one side to the other in about a minute.
They were high up: the honking was thin and distant, like voices heard through a wall.
reactions: Geese migrating at night always sound like they know exactly where they're going. I envy that.

statCheck.pass: Geese fly at night to avoid predators and heat: the cool air is denser, which actually gives their wings better lift. Most long-distance flights happen between dusk and dawn.
statCheck.fail: Geese flying over in the dark. Heading somewhere.

entries.intro: I woke up thirsty and the water jug was empty. I'd forgotten to fill it before bed.
details: Lay there for ten minutes debating whether the thirst was worth getting up for. It was.
The walk to the well in bare feet on cold stone woke me up completely. Took an hour to fall back asleep.
reactions: Forgetting to fill the water jug before bed is a small mistake with outsized consequences at midnight.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} were moving through the trees {direction} around midnight. Branches snapping, steady and unhurried.
details: Not running: just walking through the underbrush like they owned the forest.
The sound passed from left to right over about twenty minutes, then faded.
reactions: Listening to them pass is like watching a storm: you wait, it moves through, and you breathe again.

statCheck.pass: When they move slowly through cover at night, they're traveling, not hunting. Hunting is quiet and fast, but this was a column on the move.
statCheck.fail: They were walking through the trees. Moving somewhere.

entries.intro: I found a spider had built a web across the window while I slept. A perfect spiral catching the first light.
details: It must have worked all night: the web was huge, filling the whole frame from corner to corner.
The spider was sitting in the center, absolutely still, like a craftsman admiring their own work.
reactions: A spider builds more in one night than I manage in most days.

statCheck.pass: A spider can build a full orb web in under an hour: they eat the old one each morning and spin a new one each night. What looks like all-night labor is actually quick, efficient work.
statCheck.fail: The spider built a web overnight. Impressive work.

entries.intro: I had the most vivid dream about a color that doesn't exist. I could see it clearly, but I can't describe it now.
details: Everything in the dream was this impossible shade: the sky, the ground, the light itself.
Woke up trying to hold onto it, but it slipped away like water through fingers.
reactions: The mind invents things at night that the waking brain can't even hold.

entries.intro: I woke up to the smell of rain before it started falling. That heavy, mineral scent that means it's coming.
details: The air changed first: thick and electric, like the sky was holding its breath.
The first drops hit the roof about ten minutes later. My nose beat the clouds.
reactions: Smelling rain before it arrives is one of those small powers everyone has and nobody talks about.

statCheck.pass: That smell is dust and oils from the ground being lifted by the pressure drop ahead of the rain. The storm pushes air downward in front of it, which stirs up the earth's scent before the first drop falls.
statCheck.fail: I smelled rain, and then it rained. Hard to explain.

entries.intro: The floor creaked in the hallway and I held my breath. Nothing: just the wood settling in the cold.
details: A single pop, like a knuckle cracking, and then silence. The house adjusting to the temperature.
I know the sound, I've heard it a hundred times, but it still gets me every time.
reactions: A house cooling down at night sounds exactly like someone walking, and you can never quite believe it isn't.

statCheck.pass: Wood shrinks as it cools: the joists contract unevenly, and the ones that pull free make that popping sound. It's always worst on nights that cool fast after a warm day.
statCheck.fail: The floor creaked. Just the house settling.

entries.intro: I sat on the step and watched the stars slowly rotate. An hour passed and I barely noticed.
details: The whole sky turned a fraction, like a wheel with a very slow hand on it.
I tracked one bright star near the horizon and watched it drift: proof the world is spinning.
reactions: You can watch the Earth move if you're patient enough and have nothing better to do.

entries.intro: My neighbor's dog barked at something around two in the morning. Three barks, then quiet.
details: The kind of bark that says 'I heard something' not 'something is wrong.' Short and investigative.
I heard a door open briefly, a hushed command, and the dog went quiet.
reactions: A dog that barks three times and stops is reporting, not panicking. That's a well-trained animal.

entries.intro: I tried sleeping with the window shut for warmth and the room went stale by midnight. Had to open it.
details: The air got thick and close, like breathing through a cloth. My lungs wanted something fresh.
Opened the window an inch and the cold rushed in like it had been waiting.
reactions: You can have warmth or fresh air at night, but not both. That's the rule.

entries.intro: I woke up and for a moment didn't know where I was. Just blackness, confusion, and the wrong side of the room.
details: My hand reached for a wall that wasn't there. Took a few seconds for the room to make sense again.
That moment between sleep and recognition: everything familiar becomes strange.
reactions: Forgetting where you are for three seconds in your own bedroom is the most disorienting feeling.

entries.intro: The night sky was hazy last night. The stars looked like they were behind gauze.
details: Only the brightest ones punched through: the rest were smeared into a general glow.
The moon had a ring around it, wide and pale. I remember my mother saying that means weather.
reactions: A hazy sky is less dramatic than a clear one, but it has its own softness.

statCheck.pass: A ring around the moon means high, thin ice crystals in the atmosphere. It's moisture at altitude, and it usually means rain within a day or two.
statCheck.fail: The moon had a ring. Some say it means rain.

entries.intro: I heard a {wildlife_type} scratching at something under the porch. Persistent little scraping sounds for half an hour.
details: Whatever it found down there was worth the effort: it worked at it with single-minded determination.
Eventually the scratching stopped and I heard it trot off. Mission accomplished, apparently.
reactions: Animals have their own midnight errands, and they're just as busy about them as we are.

entries.intro: I fell asleep by the fire and woke up when the last log broke in half. A loud crack and a shower of sparks.
details: The embers rearranged themselves with a soft hiss, settling into new positions.
The room had cooled considerably since I dozed off. The fire was almost done.
reactions: Falling asleep by the fire and waking up to embers is the full arc of an evening in miniature.

statCheck.pass: A log breaks when the last fibers holding the charred halves together fail at once. The crack sounds loud because the gases trapped inside release in a burst with the structure.
statCheck.fail: The log cracked and I woke up. Fire stuff.

entries.intro: I woke up with a song in my head that I haven't heard since I was a child. A lullaby, half-remembered.
details: Just the melody: the words were gone, but the tune was complete and clear.
I hummed it into the dark and it felt like borrowing something from a long time ago.
reactions: The oldest songs surface in the quietest hours. They know when you're not guarding the door.

entries.intro: The thunder last night was so far away it was just a low rumble. No lightning visible: just sound.
details: It came in slow waves, like something heavy being dragged across the sky miles off.
The storm never got closer. It stayed on the horizon and grumbled to itself.
reactions: Distant thunder is the most comfortable kind of storm: all the drama, none of the rain.

statCheck.pass: Thunder you can hear but lightning you can't see means the storm is more than ten miles away. Light travels further than sound only when there's a line of sight: beyond the horizon, the curve blocks the flash but the rumble gets through.
statCheck.fail: Thunder in the distance. The storm went somewhere else.

entries.intro: I dreamed I could fly, but only a few feet off the ground. Enough to clear fences, not enough to be impressive.
details: I floated over hedgerows and low walls, barely skimming the tops. Dream flying on a budget.
Woke up feeling lighter than usual. The feeling lasted until I tried to get out of bed.
reactions: Even in dreams, my ambitions are modest.

entries.intro: I woke up because my arm had gone completely dead. Couldn't feel it at all: had to pick it up with the other hand.
details: It hung there like someone else's arm, heavy and useless. Took a full minute of shaking to get the feeling back.
The pins and needles were brutal: a thousand tiny complaints as the blood returned.
reactions: Losing an arm to sleep and getting it back by shaking it is the smallest kind of resurrection.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} had a lookout posted on the ridge {direction} last night. I could see the silhouette against the sky.
details: It stood there for hours, barely moving. Just a dark shape on the skyline, watching.
Near dawn it was gone. Replaced or relieved: I didn't see it leave.
reactions: Knowing they post sentries means they think about us the same way we think about them.

statCheck.pass: They rotate their lookouts roughly every four hours: you can track the pattern if you watch the same ridge on consecutive nights. It's disciplined, not random.
statCheck.fail: They had someone watching from the ridge. I saw the shape.

entries.intro: I heard what I thought was music: very faint, very far. Could've been the wind through something hollow.
details: A sustained note that wavered and shifted pitch, almost melodic. It came and went with the breeze.
I listened for twenty minutes trying to decide if it was real or if my ears were inventing things.
reactions: The night makes musicians out of wind and pipes out of fences.

statCheck.pass: Wind through a hollow post or pipe makes a tone based on the length of the tube. Change the wind speed and the pitch shifts: it's the same physics as a flute, just accidental.
statCheck.fail: Could have been music, could have been wind. No way to tell.

entries.intro: I slept well for the first time in a week. Didn't wake once, didn't dream, just pure unconsciousness.
details: Seven hours of nothing. No sounds registered, no position changes, no middle-of-the-night thoughts.
Woke up feeling like a different person: lighter, sharper, actually glad to be awake.
reactions: A truly good night's sleep is so rare it feels like an event.

entries.intro: I lay awake thinking about a conversation from earlier in the day. Replaying it, changing my lines.
details: The things I should've said were obvious by midnight. Clear, concise, devastating: hours too late.
I rehearsed the improved version twice before admitting I'd never use it.
reactions: Midnight is when your brain writes the dialogue it should have delivered at noon.

entries.intro: A cloud drifted across the moon and the whole yard went dark. Then it passed and everything was silver again.
details: The transition was slow: like someone dimming a lamp, holding it, and then brightening it again.
Shadows appeared, disappeared, and reappeared. The same yard, three different moods in two minutes.
reactions: The moon and the clouds put on a show every night and charge nothing for the ticket.

entries.intro: I sneezed at three in the morning and scared myself. The sound was enormous in the silence.
details: It echoed off the walls and probably carried through the window to half the street.
Lay there afterwards feeling guilty, like I'd broken something fragile.
reactions: A sneeze at night in a quiet room is a cannon shot. There's no discreet version.

entries.intro: I noticed a planet near the moon last night: bright, steady, no twinkling. Just a calm, fixed point.
details: It stayed in the same spot relative to the moon all evening: a companion, keeping pace.
The stars around it flickered and shifted, but that one light held perfectly still.
reactions: There's something reassuring about a light that doesn't flicker. Steady things are rare at night.

statCheck.pass: Planets don't twinkle because they're close enough that their light comes from a disc, not a point. Stars are so far away their light is a single ray that the atmosphere bends: planets are near enough that the bending averages out.
statCheck.fail: A bright light near the moon. A planet, I think.

entries.intro: I couldn't get comfortable last night. Every position had a problem: too hot, too cold, arm wrong, neck wrong.
details: I must've tried ten arrangements before settling on one that was merely tolerable.
Woke up in a completely different position anyway. The body negotiates its own deals.
reactions: The search for a comfortable sleeping position is a nightly quest with no guaranteed reward.

entries.intro: I heard a cat yowling somewhere in the dark. Long, wavering cries that sounded almost human.
details: It went on for fifteen minutes: that rising, falling wail that makes your skin prickle.
Then another cat answered from a different direction and the two of them went back and forth.
reactions: Cats arguing at night sound like they're performing a tragedy for an audience of one: me, trying to sleep.

statCheck.pass: Cats yowl at night during mating season: the female announces availability, the males compete for attention. The sound is designed to carry as far as possible.
statCheck.fail: Cats yowling. They do that.

entries.intro: I dreamed about water: a still lake under a clear sky, with every star reflected perfectly.
details: The reflection was so exact I couldn't tell which way was up. Two skies, one on each side of the surface.
I stood at the edge and didn't want to disturb it. Some things are better left untouched.
reactions: The most peaceful dreams are the ones where nothing happens and everything is still.

entries.intro: The wind changed direction around midnight. I could tell because the shutters went from rattling to still.
details: The wind had been pushing at the west side all evening, and then it just stopped: a moment of silence before it picked up from the south.
The new wind brought a different smell: damp earth instead of dry grass.
reactions: When the wind changes at night, the whole character of the dark changes with it.

statCheck.pass: A wind shift at midnight usually means a front passing through: the old air mass gives way to the new one. The smell change is real: you're literally breathing a different body of air.
statCheck.fail: The wind changed direction. It does that.

entries.intro: I woke up laughing from a dream I can't remember. Just the tail end of a laugh, dying in the dark.
details: Whatever was funny is completely gone. All that's left is the feeling: warm, amused, fading.
I lay there smiling at nothing, waiting for the joke to come back. It didn't.
reactions: Laughing awake from a forgotten dream is the best kind of mystery.

entries.intro: A loose shutter was banging in the wind all night. Somewhere down the street: not mine, but close enough.
details: Bang, silence, bang, silence: never regular enough to tune out, never frequent enough to confront.
By morning I'd developed strong opinions about whoever owns that shutter and their maintenance habits.
reactions: Someone else's loose shutter is everyone's problem after midnight.

statCheck.pass: A loose shutter bangs irregularly because the wind gusts aren't steady: if it banged on a rhythm, you could tune it out. The random spacing is what keeps you alert.
statCheck.fail: A shutter banging somewhere. Couldn't sleep through it.

entries.intro: I sat outside and tried to count the stars. Lost count around two hundred and started over, then lost count again.
details: Every time I thought I'd covered a section, I'd notice more between the ones I'd already counted.
The sky kept producing stars I hadn't seen: deeper, dimmer ones that appeared the longer I looked.
reactions: Counting stars is a lesson in how much there is beyond what you see at first glance.

statCheck.pass: You can see about three thousand stars with bare eyes on a clear night, but your eye only resolves a few hundred at a time. The faint ones take longer to register: your eye builds up light slowly, like a cup filling.
statCheck.fail: More stars than I can count. That's all I know.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} were quiet last night {direction}: not a sound. That's unusual for them.
details: Most nights you hear something: movement, voices, tools. Last night was silence.
The guard said a quiet night from them makes her more nervous than a loud one.
reactions: I almost missed the noise. At least when they're loud, you know where they are.

statCheck.pass: Quiet nights usually mean they've moved camp or they're resting before a long march. It's worth paying attention to what follows a silent night.
statCheck.fail: They were quiet. Could mean anything.

entries.intro: I dreamed about my hands doing work: mending, sorting, stacking. Busy hands with no face attached.
details: I watched them from somewhere outside myself, working quickly and precisely on tasks I don't remember.
Woke up and flexed my fingers. They were stiff: as if they really had been working.
reactions: When your hands are busy in your dreams, your body hasn't stopped working even though the day is done.

entries.intro: I heard an owl calling from near the {poi_type} last night. A deep, steady call that carried across everything.
details: It called every thirty seconds or so: patient, unhurried, as if time meant nothing to it.
Nothing answered. It called into the dark alone, and seemed perfectly content with that.
reactions: An owl that calls and gets no answer doesn't seem bothered. It just keeps calling.

statCheck.pass: Owls call from elevated positions to maximize range: the higher the perch, the further the sound carries. A call from near the {poi_type} means it's using the highest point available.
statCheck.fail: An owl near the {poi_type}. Nice sound if you're not trying to sleep.

entries.intro: I woke up too early and couldn't get back to sleep. Lay there in the grey light watching dawn build.
details: The room went from black to grey to pale blue, one shade at a time. A slow reveal with no hurry.
I could've gotten up and been productive. Instead I watched the ceiling change color.
reactions: Watching dawn arrive from bed is the most peaceful form of wasted time.

entries.intro: I burned my finger on the candle snuffer before bed. A small, sharp sting that made me curse into the quiet.
details: The metal was hot from the wick. I grabbed it without thinking and paid immediately.
It's a tiny burn, barely there this morning. But it kept me awake for twenty minutes while it throbbed.
reactions: A small burn at bedtime is the night's way of reminding you to pay attention.

entries.intro: The stars were so clear last night I could see the faint band of light that stretches across the whole sky.
details: A pale river of stars, edge to edge, dimmer than any single star but huge in scope.
I stared at it until my neck ached and I still hadn't taken in the full width.
reactions: Some nights the sky shows you how small everything is, and somehow that's comforting.

statCheck.pass: That band is the densest part of our own sky: you're looking along the flat plane of it, through the most stars at once. It's brightest in summer when the night side faces the thickest part.
statCheck.fail: A band of light across the sky. Beautiful and impossible to understand.

entries.intro: I tossed and turned for an hour before realizing the sheet was bunched up under my back. One wrinkle: an hour of misery.
details: Smoothed it out and fell asleep in five minutes. One crease, the size of my fist, was the entire problem.
I'm annoyed at myself for not checking sooner.
reactions: The smallest discomfort becomes enormous when you're trying to sleep.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} left tracks across the windowsill overnight. Tiny prints in the dust.
details: Came in through the gap, crossed the sill, and left again: a complete journey in miniature.
The tracks were delicate and precise. It knew exactly where it was going.
reactions: I share my house with visitors I never see. The evidence is there every morning.

entries.intro: I lay awake listening to the silence between sounds. The pauses are longer than the noises.
details: A creak, then two minutes of nothing: a rustle, then five minutes of nothing. The silence is the main event.
When you really listen, the quiet has a texture: thick, layered, almost physical.
reactions: Night silence isn't empty. It's full of all the things that aren't making noise.

entries.intro: I had a dream where I was talking to someone and everything I said was absolutely wise. Just flowing brilliance.
details: Eloquent, clear, convincing: the kind of thing you wish you could say awake. Pure dream confidence.
I tried to remember even one sentence when I woke up and couldn't. All gone.
reactions: The dream version of me is significantly more articulate. I'd like to borrow him for meetings.

entries.intro: The candle flame turned blue for a moment before it went out. One flicker of blue and then darkness.
details: I wasn't sure I'd really seen it: the kind of thing that could be imagined after a long day.
But the afterimage was there, blue and brief, burned into my eyes.
reactions: Candles do their most interesting work right before they die.

statCheck.pass: A candle turns blue when it's burning the last of the wax gases near the wick: the temperature drops and the flame shifts color. It's the candle running on fumes.
statCheck.fail: The flame turned blue and went out. Just the candle dying.

entries.intro: I woke up freezing because the fire had burned down to nothing. Just a faint orange eye in a bed of grey ash.
details: I knelt there in the dark trying to coax it back with kindling. Cold hands, cold floor, cold air.
It caught after a few tries and the warmth started creeping back. A small rescue.
reactions: Relighting a dead fire at three in the morning is a survival skill disguised as a chore.

statCheck.pass: A fire that's down to one ember still has heat stored in the ash bed: if you pile the ash around the ember and add kindling on top, it catches faster because the ash holds warmth like insulation.
statCheck.fail: I got the fire going again. Took a few tries in the cold.

entries.intro: I watched a moth the size of my palm rest on the window. Wings spread flat, perfectly still, like a leaf pinned to glass.
details: Its wings had patterns I could barely see in the moonlight: circles and lines, a whole design.
It stayed there until dawn and then it was gone. A night visitor with a schedule.
reactions: The biggest moths are also the quietest. They land and wait like they've arrived somewhere on purpose.

entries.intro: I heard rain start while I was in bed. The first drops hit the roof like thrown pebbles, then it steadied.
details: The steady part was soothing: a soft drumming that filled the room with sound without being loud.
I pulled the blanket up and let it take me. Best sleeping conditions in weeks.
reactions: Rain on the roof at night is the world's oldest lullaby. It still works.

entries.intro: I stepped on a cold, wet thing in the dark on my way to the privy. Turned out to be a leaf blown in under the door.
details: The moment of not knowing what it was lasted about two seconds, but those two seconds were vivid.
My foot still remembers the sensation: wet, flat, unexplained.
reactions: Stepping on anything unknown in the dark is a test of character. I did not pass quietly.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} had drums going {direction} for about an hour after sunset. Slow, heavy beats.
details: Not fast or aggressive: a steady pulse, like a heartbeat. Measured and deliberate.
The rhythm didn't change once in the whole hour. One beat every few seconds, exactly the same.
reactions: Slow drums are worse than fast ones. Fast drums mean excitement: slow drums mean patience.

statCheck.pass: Slow, steady drumming is how they mark time for their work shifts. The beat changes pace when they're done: a quick burst means the next group is starting.
statCheck.fail: Drums in the dark. I tried not to think about it.

entries.intro: I dreamed I was walking down a staircase that kept going. Step after step, never reaching the bottom.
details: Each step was identical: same height, same width, same grey stone. An infinite descent into nothing.
I wasn't afraid, just tired. In the dream, my legs ached from walking stairs that went nowhere.
reactions: Endless staircases in dreams feel like the mind processing something it can't finish.

entries.intro: I noticed the shadow of a tree on my wall last night, its branches swaying. Like a puppet show with no puppeteer.
details: The wind moved the branches and the shadows danced: long, grasping shapes that reached and retreated.
When the wind died, the shadows went still and it was just a normal wall again.
reactions: Shadows on the wall are the night's entertainment. Free, silent, and endlessly restless.

statCheck.pass: Tree shadow movement looks exaggerated on a wall because the light source is far away and the wall is close: small movements at the branch become large swings at the shadow. The amplification makes gentle wind look dramatic.
statCheck.fail: Shadows on the wall from a tree. The wind made them dance.

entries.intro: I woke up to absolute stillness: no wind, no animals, no creaking. Like the world had paused.
details: I held my breath to listen harder and the silence only deepened. My own heartbeat was the loudest thing.
It lasted maybe two minutes, and then a gust of wind broke it and the night resumed.
reactions: Total silence in the middle of the night feels deliberate, like something chose to stop.

entries.intro: I left a cup of water by the bed and by morning it was warm. Room temperature: the most disappointing temperature.
details: I took a sip anyway and it tasted stale: flat and lifeless. Water ages overnight like nothing else.
Poured it out and got fresh water. The difference was immediate.
reactions: Water that's been sitting all night has given up on being refreshing.

entries.intro: I watched two stars that seemed to be close together: a pair, almost touching, brighter than the rest.
details: They were a matched set, same brightness, same color, sitting side by side in the dark.
I don't know if they're actually near each other or just look that way from down here.
reactions: Two stars sitting together in the sky look like companions. Maybe they are: who would know?

statCheck.pass: Some paired stars are genuinely close: they orbit each other, locked together by gravity. Others just happen to line up from our angle, and without a telescope you can't tell which.
statCheck.fail: Two bright stars next to each other. Pretty.

entries.intro: I woke up with a stiff neck so bad I couldn't turn my head. Slept wrong, apparently.
details: It felt like someone had hammered a pin into the joint. Any rotation was met with a sharp objection.
Had to turn my whole body to look sideways this morning. Like a fence post with legs.
reactions: A stiff neck from sleeping is the body punishing you for resting wrong, which feels deeply unfair.

statCheck.pass: Neck stiffness from sleep usually means you were in a draft: cold air on exposed muscles causes them to seize. A closed window or a higher blanket usually prevents it.
statCheck.fail: Slept wrong, neck hurts. The usual.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} was drinking from the puddle by the door when I got up before dawn. It looked up, unbothered.
details: We made eye contact for a long moment. It went back to drinking like I wasn't there.
I stood in the doorway and waited until it was done. Seemed rude to interrupt.
reactions: An animal that isn't afraid of you before dawn has decided you're part of the scenery.

entries.intro: I lay in bed and tried to hear the farthest sound I could. Concentrated, really focused on the distance.
details: Past the house sounds, past the street sounds: out there, a stream, or maybe wind through trees.
The further I listened, the more layers I found. Every distance had its own sound.
reactions: Listening hard at night peels back layers of the world you don't notice during the day.

entries.intro: I dreamed about teeth falling out. Always the same dream: they loosen, they drop, I catch them in my hands.
details: No pain, just the wrongness of holding something that should be in your mouth. Cold, smooth, small.
Woke up and ran my tongue along my teeth: all there. The relief was unreasonable.
reactions: The teeth dream visits everyone eventually. Nobody likes it and nobody knows what it means.

entries.intro: The night was so warm I slept on top of the blankets with the window wide open. A luxury.
details: The air moving across my skin was warm and slow: like breathing in summer itself.
I could hear every sound outside with nothing between me and the night.
reactions: Warm nights with open windows are what summer is for. I store them up against January.

entries.intro: I noticed the floorboards near the hearth have a different creak than the ones by the door. Two completely different sounds.
details: The hearth boards moan: low, sustained. The door boards pop: sharp, quick.
I've walked on both a thousand times and only noticed the difference last night, barefoot in the quiet.
reactions: A house has its own language in its floorboards. You just need silence and bare feet to hear it.

statCheck.pass: The hearth boards are dried out from years of heat: they've lost their moisture and shrunk, so they groan when they flex. The door boards are colder, stiffer: they snap because they won't bend.
statCheck.fail: Different parts of the floor make different noises. That's all I noticed.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} were arguing {direction} around midnight. Raised voices, sharp tones, then silence.
details: I couldn't make out words, but the cadence was unmistakable: two voices, one louder than the other.
It ended abruptly. Whatever the disagreement was, one of them won it decisively.
reactions: Nice to know they argue with each other too. Not just a problem they save for us.

entries.intro: I lay awake adding up numbers in my head. Prices, quantities, debts: a ledger audit at midnight.
details: My brain decided this was the time for accounting, and I couldn't shut it off.
The numbers kept changing every time I recounted. Midnight arithmetic is not reliable.
reactions: The mind does its bookkeeping when you least want it to, and the sums are always wrong.

statCheck.pass: The brain defaults to problem-solving when it's understimulated: lying awake in the dark with no input, it grabs the nearest unresolved task. Numbers and debts are common because they're open-ended.
statCheck.fail: My brain decided to do accounting at midnight. Not helpful.

entries.intro: I woke up to the rooster crowing in the dark. It was at least two hours early.
details: Full-throated, confident, absolutely wrong about the time. It crowed three times and stopped.
The silence after was thick with disapproval from every house on the street.
reactions: A rooster that crows at the wrong time has too much confidence and not enough information.

statCheck.pass: Roosters crow in response to light, not time: a bright moon or a lamp left burning can fool them into thinking it's dawn. The false crows are always shorter, like they're not fully committed.
statCheck.fail: The rooster crowed early. Not the first time.

entries.intro: I noticed the constellation that looks like a crown was low on the horizon. It won't be there much longer this season.
details: It's sinking a little lower each night: dipping toward the edge of the sky like it's bowing out.
In a few weeks it'll be gone until next year. One of those slow goodbyes the sky does.
reactions: Watching a constellation leave for the season is like saying goodbye to a neighbor who travels.

entries.intro: I was half asleep when I felt something brush my face. Probably a strand of hair: could've been anything.
details: Light, barely there, and then gone. My hand went to my face instantly but found nothing.
I lay perfectly still for five minutes, alert, waiting for it to happen again. It didn't.
reactions: Something touching your face in the dark is the fastest way from drowsy to wide awake.

entries.intro: I heard a stream I've never noticed before. The quiet was deep enough to let it through.
details: A constant, soft rushing: water over stone somewhere beyond the houses.
It must be there every night, hidden under the daytime noise. The dark is what finally lets it speak.
reactions: The night reveals the sounds that the day buries. The water was always there: I just couldn't hear it.

entries.intro: I dozed off sitting up and woke with my chin on my chest. Neck folded forward like a wilted stem.
details: The angle couldn't have been good for anything: my neck protested immediately on lifting.
Fell asleep upright and paid the posture tax in full.
reactions: Sleeping sitting up is something the body allows once and punishes for hours.

entries.intro: I watched a cloud pass in front of the moon and turn gold at the edges. Lit from behind like a lamp through cloth.
details: The center was dark but the rim glowed: a ring of amber light around a dark core.
It drifted slowly across and then the full moon emerged on the other side, untouched.
reactions: The moon behind a cloud is one of those small spectacles that no one else was awake to see.

entries.intro: I dreamed about a room I've never been in, but it felt like home. Warm, familiar, and completely imaginary.
details: The furniture, the light, the smell of it: all invented, all perfect, all mine.
I woke up missing a place that doesn't exist. That's a particular kind of homesickness.
reactions: Dreams build places you've never been and make you miss them. The mind is a cruel architect.

entries.intro: There was a fox bark somewhere close around midnight. That sharp, hoarse cry that stops your heart for a second.
details: One scream, then silence: like it had shouted once and moved on.
If you've never heard one before, a fox at night sounds like something much larger and much worse.
reactions: A fox cry at midnight is nature's jump scare. Once you know it, it's still unnerving.

statCheck.pass: Foxes bark to mark territory: a single sharp cry tells other foxes the area is claimed. If you hear one bark and then silence, it means nothing contested the claim.
statCheck.fail: A fox screamed in the dark. Always startling, even when you know what it is.

entries.intro: I tried counting backwards from a hundred to fall asleep. Got to about sixty before I forgot what I was doing.
details: The numbers started drifting, mixing with half-thoughts: sixty-three became something about a door, then stairs.
I think I fell asleep somewhere around fifty. The technique works, but not in the way I expected.
reactions: Counting backwards to sleep works by boring your own mind into surrender.

statCheck.pass: Counting backwards works because it occupies the part of the brain that narrates your thoughts. Once that channel is busy with numbers, the worrying voice can't get a word in.
statCheck.fail: Counting backwards made me sleepy. Somehow.

entries.intro: I woke up with the taste of something metallic in my mouth. No cause, no explanation, just copper on the tongue.
details: I drank water to wash it out, but it lingered: a faint, flat tang that took ten minutes to fade.
This morning it's gone entirely. A night flavor with no origin.
reactions: Waking up to a taste that shouldn't be there is the body's way of being mysterious.

entries.intro: I went outside to look at the stars and the dew had already started forming. My shoes were wet in thirty seconds.
details: The grass was beaded with it: thousands of tiny lenses, each one catching the moonlight.
I stood there getting cold feet, literally, while the sky gave me nothing I hadn't seen before.
reactions: Stargazing in dew season means wet feet and cold ankles. Worth it, but just barely.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} were dragging something heavy {direction} before dawn. A slow, scraping sound.
details: Wood on stone, or maybe a laden sled: a long, grinding noise that lasted several minutes.
It stopped, started again, stopped again. Whatever it was, it was too heavy to move smoothly.
reactions: Heavy things being moved in the dark hours makes you wonder what they're building out there.

statCheck.pass: They tend to move supplies before dawn when the air is cool and the ground is damp: both make dragging easier. It's practical, not secretive.
statCheck.fail: They were dragging something heavy. That's all I know.

entries.intro: I woke up warm and content and lay there enjoying the feeling. No rush, no noise, just comfort.
details: The blankets were the right weight, the pillow was the right shape, the room was the right temperature.
I kept my eyes closed and stretched like a cat. Five more minutes turned into twenty.
reactions: A morning where everything is comfortable enough to delay getting up is a morning well spent.

entries.intro: I heard a flute or a whistle somewhere far off in the quiet hours. Just a few notes, then nothing.
details: High, clear, and far away: someone playing softly, or the wind finding the right shape to sing through.
I waited for more but it didn't come. A fragment of music with no context.
reactions: A few notes of music at night, from nowhere, are better than a full song. They leave room to imagine.

entries.intro: I couldn't sleep because my thoughts kept circling the same worry. Round and round, never landing.
details: The worry wasn't even urgent: just a low-grade concern that refused to resolve or leave.
I tried to argue it down logically but logic doesn't work at two in the morning.
reactions: A worry at midnight is three times its daytime size and twice as persistent.

entries.intro: I noticed the moon was a perfect half last night. A clean, straight edge dividing light from dark.
details: Half the face bright, half in shadow: like a coin sliced in two and one half put away.
The terminator line was razor-sharp. No gradient, no blur, just light ending and dark beginning.
reactions: A half moon is the most honest phase: it shows you exactly how the light works.

statCheck.pass: The sharp edge is the part where the sun hits the surface at the shallowest angle: craters and mountains cast long shadows right at that line, and on a clear night you can almost see them.
statCheck.fail: Half moon. Clean line between bright and dark.

entries.intro: I woke up with my jaw clenched so tight my teeth ached. Must've been grinding all night.
details: The muscles on both sides were sore: like I'd been chewing rocks in my sleep.
My teeth felt tender and my head had a dull ache behind the ears.
reactions: Grinding your teeth at night means your body is working through something your mind won't address.

statCheck.pass: Night grinding is the jaw muscles clenching and releasing under stress: the force is much stronger than conscious chewing because there's no pain feedback while you're asleep. It wears the teeth down over time.
statCheck.fail: My jaw hurts. Ground my teeth again.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} was sitting in the moonlight on the garden wall. Just grooming itself, completely serene.
details: It moved through its routine with slow precision: a lick here, a scratch there, thorough and unhurried.
When it was done, it stretched once, jumped down, and vanished into the dark.
reactions: An animal grooming by moonlight is the picture of someone with no appointments and no worries.

entries.intro: The wind sounded like voices last night. Conversational murmurs just below the threshold of understanding.
details: I kept almost catching words: the rhythm was right, the tone was right, but the meaning stayed just out of reach.
After a while I stopped trying to understand and just let it be wind.
reactions: The wind talks to itself at night and we're not meant to follow the conversation.

entries.intro: I slept through a thunderstorm and only learned about it in the morning. Didn't hear a thing.
details: The ground was soaked, branches were down, and I slept through the whole performance.
My neighbor says the lightning was spectacular. I have to take his word for it.
reactions: Sleeping through a storm is either a sign of deep exhaustion or a talent I didn't know I had.

statCheck.pass: Sleeping through a storm usually means you were in deep sleep when it started: the brain suppresses unfamiliar sounds during deep phases. If you'd been in light sleep, the first thunderclap would've woken you.
statCheck.fail: I slept through a storm. Must've been tired.

entries.intro: I tried sleeping on my stomach and woke up with my face pressed into the pillow. Breathing through one nostril for who knows how long.
details: The pillow had a perfect impression of my face: eyes, nose, mouth, all stamped into the linen.
My neck was twisted at an angle that shouldn't be possible. I'm paying for it now.
reactions: Stomach sleeping is comfortable for the first ten minutes and a punishment for the rest.

entries.intro: I noticed a bright star near the horizon that was red. Not orange, not amber: distinctly red.
details: It pulsed slightly, brightening and dimming: like a heartbeat made of light.
I watched it for twenty minutes and the color never changed. Just that steady, ancient red.
reactions: A red star feels old. Everything else in the sky is white or blue, and then there's this: glowing like an ember.

statCheck.pass: Red stars are cooler and older than the white ones: they've burned through their fuel and expanded. The pulsing you see is the atmosphere bending the light: the star is steady, but the air between you and it isn't.
statCheck.fail: A red star near the horizon. Different from the others.

entries.intro: I woke up to a scratching sound in the ceiling. Something small, moving quickly, then stopping.
details: Little feet, fast and light, crossing above my head: then silence, then another burst of movement.
It went on for ten minutes and then whatever it was settled down or left.
reactions: Anything living in your ceiling has its own schedule, and midnight is apparently peak hours.

statCheck.pass: The stopping and starting is how they move: short bursts to avoid predators, even when there are none. It's instinct built deeper than safety.
statCheck.fail: Something in the ceiling. Small, fast, and I don't care to meet it.

entries.intro: The first light of dawn caught the dew and the whole yard glittered. Like someone had scattered glass.
details: Every surface was sparkling: the fence, the grass, the roof edges. A brief, total shine.
It lasted maybe ten minutes before the sun climbed enough to burn it off.
reactions: The moment between night and day is the prettiest part, and most people sleep through it.

entries.intro: I had a dream where I was trying to read a book, but the words kept rearranging themselves. Just letter soup.
details: Every time I focused on a word it dissolved into nonsense. The sentences swam away from my eyes.
I woke up oddly frustrated, like the book owed me something.
reactions: Your brain can build an entire world in a dream but can't hold five words on a page. Strange priorities.

entries.intro: I heard two cats fighting on the roof above my room. A brief, violent burst of hissing and yowling.
details: The scrambling of claws on tile, a screech, and then one of them fled across the roof in a skittering retreat.
The whole encounter lasted maybe fifteen seconds. Brief and decisive.
reactions: Cat fights on the roof at night are mercifully short but impossibly loud.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} had torches lit along their perimeter {direction} last night. A line of small orange dots.
details: You could count them: eight or nine, evenly spaced, flickering in the wind.
They burned all night. By dawn they were out, but the smoke still hung in the air.
reactions: Counting their torches at night is a habit I wish I didn't have.

statCheck.pass: The number of torches usually matches their sentry posts. If the count changes, they've either added a post or pulled one back, but it's been steady for weeks now.
statCheck.fail: They had torches lit. I could see them from the window.

entries.intro: I went to bed early and couldn't sleep because it was still too light. The dusk was hanging on.
details: I could see every detail in the room: the grain of the wood, the stitching on the blanket. Too bright for sleep.
Pulled the blanket over my head and made my own darkness. Worked well enough.
reactions: Going to bed while the sky is still light is admitting defeat, and the sun knows it.

entries.intro: I could hear every joint in the bed frame tonight. Creak when I shifted, creak when I breathed, creak when I didn't move at all.
details: The frame protested every change in weight like it was personally offended by movement.
I tried lying perfectly still and it creaked anyway. The house cooling down must shift the joints.
reactions: A creaky bed turns you into a hostage: every movement announced to the whole room.

statCheck.pass: Bed frames creak when the joints have dried out and lost their tight fit: the wood shrinks with age and the tenons move in the mortises. A few drops of oil in the joints quiets them for months.
statCheck.fail: The bed creaks. Old wood does that.

entries.intro: I woke up and the stars had completely changed position. The sky had rotated while I slept.
details: The constellations I'd seen before bed were gone, replaced by ones I only see in the small hours.
It felt like the sky had redecorated while I wasn't looking.
reactions: Falling asleep under one sky and waking under another is the quietest proof that time passes.

entries.intro: I tried warming my feet by the fire before bed. They were still cold when I got under the blankets.
details: The heat lasted about three steps from the hearth to the bed. Bare feet on cold floor: instant undo.
I tucked them under the blanket and waited. Took a full fifteen minutes before they felt human again.
reactions: Cold feet in bed is a problem that defies every solution except patience.

entries.intro: I saw a falling star that left a trail. A green streak that hung in the sky for a full second after the star was gone.
details: The trail glowed and then faded: a bright line drawn across the dark and slowly erased.
I've seen plenty of falling stars but never one that left a mark like that.
reactions: A falling star with a trail feels like the sky writing something and then changing its mind.

statCheck.pass: The green color comes from the metals burning up: iron and nickel glow green at those temperatures. A trail that lingers means the object was larger than usual and burned slower.
statCheck.fail: A green streak across the sky. Beautiful and gone.

entries.intro: I dreamed I was packing a bag but couldn't decide what to bring. Kept taking things out and putting them back.
details: Every item seemed important and unnecessary at the same time. The bag was never right.
Woke up with the feeling of not being ready for something I can't identify.
reactions: Packing in a dream is the mind rehearsing for a departure it hasn't scheduled.

entries.intro: The temperature dropped so fast after sunset that the windowpane fogged over. Couldn't see out by bedtime.
details: I wiped a circle in the condensation with my sleeve and watched it fog up again in seconds.
By morning there was actual water running down the glass. The night had wrung the air dry.
reactions: A fogged window is the house breathing. Warm inside, cold outside, and the glass catching the difference.

statCheck.pass: Condensation forms when the glass surface drops below the dew point of the air inside. If the temperature falls fast outside, the glass chills before the room does, and the moisture in the air gives up on the first cold surface it finds.
statCheck.fail: The window fogged up. It gets cold fast out there.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} was making a commotion in the garden before dawn. Digging at something with real purpose.
details: Dirt flying, low grunting sounds, the kind of dedication you only see when food is involved.
I watched from the window until it finished. Whatever it found, it ate it on the spot and trotted off.
reactions: My garden serves dinner nightly to wildlife that never asked permission and never pays.

entries.intro: I lay in bed and felt my heartbeat in my ear against the pillow. A steady, muffled thumping.
details: You only notice it in perfect silence: the pillow amplifies it, turns it into a drum.
I switched ears and it was there on the other side too. No escape from your own pulse.
reactions: Hearing your heartbeat in a quiet room is reassuring for the first minute and annoying for the next ten.

statCheck.pass: You hear your heartbeat through the pillow because bone conducts sound better than air does. Press your ear against something solid and your own pulse gets amplified through the skull.
statCheck.fail: My heartbeat in the pillow. It's always there if you listen.

entries.intro: I went outside at midnight and the air smelled like cut grass. Someone had been mowing in the evening.
details: The scent was thick and sweet: green, alive, lingering hours after the work was done.
Mixed with the cool night air, it was almost pleasant enough to stand in.
reactions: The smell of cut grass at midnight is a daytime scent in the wrong setting. Strangely comforting.

entries.intro: I lay awake composing a letter I'll never write. Perfect sentences, addressed to no one in particular.
details: The words came easily in the dark: clear, honest, exactly right. The kind of writing you can't do by daylight.
By morning the sentences were gone. Not one survived the crossing into wakefulness.
reactions: The mind writes its best work at midnight and destroys it by dawn.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} howled {direction} right as I was falling asleep. One long, rising cry that snapped me awake.
details: Others joined in: a chorus that built for a few seconds and then cut off all at once.
The silence after was almost worse. Just the ringing in my ears and the dark.
reactions: Falling asleep and being yanked back by a howl is the worst kind of reset.

entries.intro: I found a perfectly preserved leaf on the windowsill this morning. Blown in during the night, placed there by the wind.
details: It was centered on the sill like someone had arranged it: a single red leaf, veined and whole.
The wind must have threaded it through the gap in the shutter. A lucky throw.
reactions: When the wind delivers something beautiful to your windowsill, it feels intentional.

entries.intro: I had a dream where everyone I know spoke a language I couldn't understand. Familiar faces, foreign words.
details: They spoke to me earnestly, expecting responses. I nodded along, understanding nothing.
The frustration was real even though the situation was nonsense.
reactions: Being lost in your own dream is a loneliness that follows you for an hour after waking.

entries.intro: I noticed how different the sky looks in different directions. Stars thick in one quarter, sparse in another.
details: Looking one way was like staring into a field of light. Looking the other was almost empty: deep, dark blue.
The sky isn't uniform at all. It has its own geography.
reactions: We talk about 'the sky' as one thing, but it changes every time you turn your head.

statCheck.pass: The thick part is where the most stars are clustered along the central band. Look ninety degrees away and you're looking out the thin edge: fewer stars, deeper darkness, same sky.
statCheck.fail: More stars in one direction than the other. Not sure why.

entries.intro: I stubbed my toe on the bed frame in the dark. The pain was instant and absolute.
details: A sharp crack of bone on wood, and then that wave of agony that radiates outward from one small toe.
I sat on the edge of the bed holding my foot and breathing through clenched teeth for five minutes.
reactions: Stubbing a toe in the dark turns you into a philosopher of suffering. One toe: all that pain.

entries.intro: I sat outside and smelled woodsmoke from someone's chimney. Warm, familiar, drifting across the rooftops.
details: You could trace it: the scent came and went with the breeze, arriving in waves.
Someone was still up, still warming themselves. The smoke was the only sign of life on the street.
reactions: Woodsmoke at night is proof that someone else is awake. You're not the last one.

entries.intro: I kept almost falling asleep and then jerking awake. That twitch: sharp, involuntary, every single time.
details: Right at the edge of sleep: a sudden jolt through my whole body like I'd stepped off a ledge.
It happened four times in a row before I finally passed the threshold.
reactions: The body tests its own reflexes at the worst possible moment. I was almost there.

statCheck.pass: The jolt happens because your muscles relax faster than your brain expects: the brain misreads the sudden looseness as falling and fires a reflex to catch you. It's harmless, but your heart doesn't know that.
statCheck.fail: The twitching thing. Happens sometimes when I'm about to fall asleep.

entries.intro: I heard the guard change shifts around two in the morning. Brief voices, footsteps, then quiet again.
details: A murmured exchange: the handoff of information in low tones meant not to carry.
One set of footsteps departed, another took position. The whole transition took thirty seconds.
reactions: Hearing the guard change is the night's way of marking the hour. Better than any clock.

entries.intro: I had the strangest dream about finding a door in a room I know well. It opened to somewhere I'd never seen.
details: A bright, warm space behind a door that shouldn't exist: impossible but inviting.
I walked through and woke up immediately, as if the dream couldn't sustain what I'd found.
reactions: Dreams that end when they get interesting are the mind's way of teasing.

entries.intro: The rain woke me up because it changed: went from gentle to hammering in about ten seconds flat.
details: Like someone turned the volume up all at once. The roof went from whispering to roaring.
I lay there listening to the assault and hoping the patches I put on last month were holding.
reactions: Heavy rain at night makes you think about your roof in ways you never do during the day.

statCheck.pass: A sudden shift from light to heavy rain means you've hit the core of the storm: the heaviest rainfall is always in a narrow band, and it passes quickly. The worst of it rarely lasts more than twenty minutes.
statCheck.fail: The rain got heavy fast. All you can do is hope the roof holds.

entries.intro: I woke up smiling and couldn't figure out why. No dream I could recall, just a lingering warmth.
details: The feeling was there before my thoughts were: a baseline contentment with no source.
By the time I got up, it was fading. But for a few minutes the world was lighter than usual.
reactions: Waking up happy for no reason is the body giving you a gift it won't explain.

entries.intro: I noticed how the moonlight makes everything one color. No reds, no yellows: just shades of silver and blue.
details: My hands looked pale and foreign. The blankets, the walls, the furniture: all the same cool tone.
It's like seeing the room through someone else's eyes, in a world with a simpler palette.
reactions: Moonlight strips the color from everything and replaces it with something quieter.

statCheck.pass: Moonlight is actually sunlight reflected off grey rock: it's the same spectrum but so dim your color-seeing cells can't use it. The blue-sensitive ones work better in low light, so everything shifts blue.
statCheck.fail: Moonlight makes everything silver. I like the way it changes the room.

entries.intro: I woke up with my pillow on the floor. No memory of throwing it: just woke up flat on the mattress, pillow gone.
details: Found it three feet from the bed, face down. My sleeping self apparently has opinions about pillows.
I put it back, lay down, and immediately felt how much better the mattress was without it.
reactions: Maybe my sleeping self knew something. The pillow might be the problem.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} were laughing {direction} last night. A harsh, barking sound that didn't sound like joy.
details: It came in bursts: several voices at once, then silence, then another round.
Hard to tell if it was laughter or something else entirely. The distance distorts everything.
reactions: Their laughter sounds nothing like ours. It makes the night feel longer.

entries.intro: I slept through the night without dreaming. Just closed my eyes and opened them to daylight.
details: No gaps, no interruptions, no memory of anything between then and now. Clean sleep.
It felt like five minutes but the whole night had passed.
reactions: Dreamless sleep is the most efficient rest. Your mind finally shut up and let you have it.

statCheck.pass: Dreamless sleep means you woke up during a deep phase, not during a dream phase. You probably did dream: you just don't remember because you didn't wake up at the right moment to catch it.
statCheck.fail: No dreams I can remember. Just good sleep.

entries.intro: I could hear a loose tile somewhere on the roof, tapping in the wind. A rhythmic click, all night.
details: Every gust lifted it and set it down: tap, tap, tap. Regular as a clock but infinitely more irritating.
I know which tile it is. I've been meaning to fix it for weeks.
reactions: A loose tile at night is a reminder of a chore you keep postponing.

statCheck.pass: A loose tile taps because it lifts on the updraft and drops on the lull: the wind creates suction on the exposed edge. A single nail in the right spot would fix it permanently.
statCheck.fail: A tile tapping on the roof. I should fix it.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} walked right across the path while I was outside stargazing. Close enough to touch.
details: It passed within arm's reach without pausing: calm, purposeful, unbothered by me.
I stood completely still and let it go. Felt like a privilege, somehow.
reactions: When a wild animal passes that close and isn't afraid, the night feels safe for everyone.

entries.intro: I woke up to a cobweb stretched across my face. A single strand, from pillow to wall, right across my nose.
details: Invisible, but the tickle was unmistakable. I wiped it away and spent five minutes checking for the builder.
No spider found. It had done its work and moved on: left me the souvenir.
reactions: Waking up in a cobweb means a spider walked across your face while you slept. Best not to dwell on it.

entries.intro: The moon was so bright last night it cast proper shadows. Hard-edged, dark, like midday but silver.
details: My own shadow stretched across the yard, sharp and clear. I waved at it and it waved back.
You could've read a page by that light: the brightest moon I've seen in months.
reactions: A moon bright enough to cast shadows makes the night feel like a borrowed day.

statCheck.pass: The brightest full moons happen when the moon is closest in its orbit: same reflected light, but from a shorter distance. The difference is small, but your eyes notice it.
statCheck.fail: Bright moon. Everything had a shadow.

entries.intro: I tried holding my breath in the dark to hear better. The silence rushed in like water filling a gap.
details: Without my breathing, I could hear the tiny sounds: wood ticking, fabric settling, air moving through cracks.
I lasted about forty seconds before my lungs demanded a vote.
reactions: Your own breathing is the loudest thing in a quiet room. Stop it, and the world opens up.

statCheck.pass: Your breathing moves more air than you realize: even quiet breathing displaces enough to mask the smallest sounds in a room. Hold your breath and the acoustic floor drops.
statCheck.fail: Holding my breath made the room quieter. That's about it.

entries.intro: I dreamed I was at a feast with strangers. Everyone was happy and I didn't know a single face.
details: They welcomed me like an old friend: poured my drink, pulled out my chair. All strangers, all warm.
Woke up to an empty room feeling oddly grateful for hospitality that never happened.
reactions: A dream where strangers are kind stays with you longer than one where friends are present.

entries.intro: The gutters were overflowing in the rain last night. A steady cascade past my window, close and loud.
details: The water was sheeting down the wall outside: a curtain of rain an inch from the glass.
I could feel the dampness in the air even with the window closed.
reactions: Overflowing gutters in the dark sound like the house is weeping. Not a relaxing soundtrack.

entries.intro: I noticed a spider lowering itself from the ceiling on a single thread. Descending slowly toward my pillow.
details: I watched it for ten seconds: a small, deliberate creature on its own absurd mission.
Blew gently and it swung away. Climbed back up its thread like nothing had happened.
reactions: Spiders have no sense of personal space and an incredible commitment to going where they please.

statCheck.pass: Spiders lower themselves on dragline silk, which is stronger per weight than steel: they control the descent by releasing more silk from their spinnerets. A puff of air pushes them sideways but they climb right back up.
statCheck.fail: A spider coming down from the ceiling. Blew it away.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} were hauling water {direction} before dawn. I could hear the sloshing and the creak of buckets.
details: A line of them, by the sound of it: footsteps and water sounds moving in a steady procession.
They were organized about it. Orderly, even.
reactions: Them needing water is a reminder that they live out there, not just lurk.

statCheck.pass: Hauling water at dawn means they don't have a reliable source right at camp. They have to fetch it, which means they depend on the same streams we do.
statCheck.fail: They were carrying water. Even they need to drink.

entries.intro: I lay awake and felt the exact moment tiredness finally arrived. A weight behind the eyes, settling in.
details: My thoughts slowed, my limbs got heavy, the pillow suddenly felt like it was pulling me down.
I remember thinking 'finally' and then nothing. It took me like a tide.
reactions: The moment sleep arrives is the one moment you can't witness. You're always gone before you know it.

entries.intro: I watched a thin crescent moon set behind the rooftops. A sliver of light balanced on the chimney before it dropped.
details: The crescent was so thin it looked like a scratch in the sky: a curved line, barely there.
It disappeared behind the roofline and the sky went completely dark. An immediate, total change.
reactions: A crescent moon setting is the fastest goodbye in the sky. One moment there, the next gone.

entries.intro: I woke up to the sound of something dripping inside the room. Not rain: something had tipped over in the night.
details: The water jug had shifted off its mark and was dripping off the edge of the table. Slow, steady drops.
By the time I found it in the dark, there was a small pool on the floor.
reactions: A drip inside the house is the most alarming of night sounds until you find the source.

statCheck.pass: Water migrates toward edges on a flat surface because of surface tension: even a slight tilt sends it creeping along the lowest line until it finds a drip point.
statCheck.fail: The water jug was dripping. Found the puddle in the dark.

entries.intro: I dreamed about walking through a forest where every tree was bare. No leaves, no wind, no sound.
details: Just grey trunks in every direction, evenly spaced, like a room made of wood.
I walked for a long time without arriving anywhere. The dream didn't mind.
reactions: Dreams with no destination in them are the mind going for a walk without planning a route.

entries.intro: I watched the last ember in the hearth pulse for twenty minutes before it finally went out. A slow, orange heartbeat.
details: Each breath of air from the chimney made it glow brighter, then fade. Bright, dim, bright, dim.
When it finally died, the darkness was total and sudden. Like closing a book.
reactions: The last ember is the fire's way of saying goodnight. You just have to be patient enough to hear it.

statCheck.pass: An ember pulses because the draft feeds it air in rhythm: the chimney draws air up, which cools, drops back down, and the cycle repeats. The glow matches the breathing of the chimney.
statCheck.fail: The ember glowed and faded. Then it was dark.
